title: Labels and Missing Values
text: Survey data SPSS, Stata, SAS stores two kinds labels: Variable labels describe variable measures (e.g., gender → “Respondent’s gender”) Value labels map numeric codes text (e.g., 1 → “Male”, 2 → “Female”) R, labels stored attributes haven_labelled columns. mariposa provides 10 functions inspecting, modifying, converting labelled data — plus tools declaring missing values searching variables.

title: Labels and Missing Values
text: Variable labels describe column contains. Use var_label() retrieve :
code: # Get labels for specific variables var_label(survey_data, gender, education, life_satisfaction) #>                                            gender  #>                                          "Gender"  #>                                         education  #>                  "Highest educational attainment"  #>                                 life_satisfaction  #> "Life satisfaction (1=dissatisfied, 5=satisfied)" # Get labels for all variables var_label(survey_data) #>                                                id  #>                                                NA  #>                                               age  #>                                    "Age in years"  #>                                            gender  #>                                          "Gender"  #>                                            region  #>                              "Region (East/West)"  #>                                         education  #>                  "Highest educational attainment"  #>                                            income  #>                  "Monthly household income (EUR)"  #>                                        employment  #>                               "Employment status"  #>                             political_orientation  #>         "Political orientation (1=left, 5=right)"  #>                             environmental_concern  #>           "Environmental concern (1=low, 5=high)"  #>                                 life_satisfaction  #> "Life satisfaction (1=dissatisfied, 5=satisfied)"  #>                                  trust_government  #>        "Trust in government (1=none, 5=complete)"  #>                                       trust_media  #>             "Trust in media (1=none, 5=complete)"  #>                                     trust_science  #>           "Trust in science (1=none, 5=complete)"  #>                                   sampling_weight  #>                                "Weighting factor"  #>                                           stratum  #>                         "Stratification variable"  #>                                    interview_mode  #>                                  "Interview mode"

title: Labels and Missing Values
text: Value labels map numeric codes meaningful text. Use val_labels() retrieve :
code: # Get value labels for a single variable val_labels(survey_data, gender) #> NULL # Get value labels for multiple variables val_labels(survey_data, education, employment) #> $education #> NULL #>  #> $employment #> NULL

title: Labels and Missing Values
text: SPSS datasets often cryptic variable names like q104a_1 v23. Use find_var() search name label:
code: # Search in both names and labels (default) find_var(survey_data, "trust") #>   col             name                                    label #> 1  11 trust_government Trust in government (1=none, 5=complete) #> 2  12      trust_media      Trust in media (1=none, 5=complete) #> 3  13    trust_science    Trust in science (1=none, 5=complete) # Search only in variable labels find_var(survey_data, "satisfaction", search = "label") #>   col              name                                           label #> 1  10 life_satisfaction Life satisfaction (1=dissatisfied, 5=satisfied) # Search only in variable names find_var(survey_data, "age|income", search = "name") #>   col   name                          label #> 1   2    age                   Age in years #> 2   6 income Monthly household income (EUR)

title: Labels and Missing Values
code: # Set labels for specific variables labeled_data <- var_label(survey_data,   age = "Age of respondent in years",   income = "Monthly net income in euros" )  # Verify var_label(labeled_data, age, income) #>                           age                        income  #>  "Age of respondent in years" "Monthly net income in euros"

title: Labels and Missing Values
code: # Set value labels labeled_data <- val_labels(survey_data,   gender = c("Male" = 1, "Female" = 2) )  # Verify val_labels(labeled_data, gender) #>   Male Female  #>      1      2 # Add labels without replacing existing ones labeled_data <- val_labels(survey_data,   gender = c("Diverse" = 3),   .add = TRUE )  val_labels(labeled_data, gender) #> Diverse  #>       3

title: Labels and Missing Values
text: Survey data often needs conversion labelled, factor, character, numeric formats depending analysis.

title: Labels and Missing Values
text: Use to_label() need factors plotting statistical models:
code: # Convert gender from labelled to factor factor_data <- to_label(survey_data, gender, education)  # Check the result class(factor_data$gender) #> [1] "factor" levels(factor_data$gender) #> [1] "Male"   "Female" head(factor_data$gender) #> [1] Female Male   Male   Female Male   Female #> Levels: Male Female # Create ordered factors (useful for ordinal variables) ordered_data <- to_label(survey_data, education, ordered = TRUE) levels(ordered_data$education) #> [1] "Basic Secondary"        "Intermediate Secondary" "Academic Secondary"     #> [4] "University"

title: Labels and Missing Values
text: Use to_character() string-based operations:
code: char_data <- to_character(survey_data, gender, region) head(char_data$gender) #> [1] "Female" "Male"   "Male"   "Female" "Male"   "Female"

title: Labels and Missing Values
text: Use to_numeric() convert factors labelled vectors back numbers:
code: # First convert to factor, then back to numeric factor_data <- to_label(survey_data, education)  # Parse numeric values from factor levels numeric_data <- to_numeric(factor_data, education) head(numeric_data$education) #> [1] 2 3 3 1 1 2

title: Labels and Missing Values
text: Use to_labelled() add labels plain numeric factor columns:
code: # Convert a factor back to haven_labelled plain_data <- data.frame(   gender = factor(c("Male", "Female", "Male")),   score = c(3, 4, 2) )  labelled_data <- to_labelled(plain_data, gender) class(labelled_data$gender) #> [1] "haven_labelled" "vctrs_vctr"     "double"

title: Labels and Missing Values
text: Use set_na() declare specific numeric codes missing values: tag = TRUE (default), missing code becomes distinct tagged NA, can later distinguish “refused” “don’t know” responses.
code: # After importing SPSS data where -9 = refused, -8 = don't know data <- read_spss("survey.sav", tag_na = FALSE)  # Declare -9 and -8 as missing across all numeric columns data <- set_na(data, -9, -8, tag = TRUE)  # Declare different missing codes for specific variables data <- set_na(data, q1 = c(-9, -8), q2 = c(99))

title: Labels and Missing Values
text: Use unlabel() need plain numeric data without label metadata: converts haven_labelled columns plain numeric character, tagged NAs regular NA, removes label attributes.
code: # Strip all labels from entire dataset plain_data <- unlabel(survey_data)  # Strip labels from specific variables only plain_data <- unlabel(survey_data, gender, education)

title: Labels and Missing Values
text: dplyr operations like filter(), mutate(), select() can strip label attributes columns:
code: filtered <- survey_data %>%   filter(age >= 30) %>%   select(gender, education, income)  # Labels may be lost after certain operations

title: Labels and Missing Values
text: Use copy_labels() restore labels original data:
code: # Restore labels from the source dataset filtered <- copy_labels(filtered, survey_data)  # Verify labels are back var_label(filtered, gender, education) #>                           gender                        education  #>                         "Gender" "Highest educational attainment"

title: Labels and Missing Values
text: filtering, value label categories may longer appear data. Use drop_labels() clean :
code: # Subset to one region only subset_data <- survey_data %>% filter(region == 1)  # Remove value labels for regions that are no longer in the data clean_data <- drop_labels(subset_data, region) val_labels(clean_data, region) #> NULL

title: Labels and Missing Values
text: typical workflow preparing SPSS data analysis:
code: # 1. Import SPSS file data <- read_spss("survey_2024.sav")  # 2. Explore what's in the data codebook(data) find_var(data, "satisf") find_var(data, "trust")  # 3. Check labels var_label(data, q1, q2, q3) val_labels(data, q1)  # 4. Rename variables for clarity data <- data %>%   rename(life_satisfaction = q1, income = q2, education = q3)  # Update variable labels data <- var_label(data,   life_satisfaction = "Overall life satisfaction (1-5)",   income = "Monthly net income in euros",   education = "Highest education level" )  # 5. Convert categorical variables for analysis data <- to_label(data, education, gender)  # 6. Analyze data %>%   describe(life_satisfaction, income, weights = sampling_weight)  data %>%   t_test(life_satisfaction, group = gender, weights = sampling_weight)

title: Labels and Missing Values
text: Keep labels long possible. Labels carry important context. convert factor character specific function requires . Use find_var() instead names(). searches names labels, essential SPSS datasets non-descriptive variable names. Use copy_labels() complex pipelines. pipeline involves joins, reshaping, operations might strip attributes, restore labels original data. Prefer to_label() .factor(). to_label() uses value labels factor levels, giving meaningful names instead numeric codes. Use set_na() early workflow. Declaring missing values immediately import ensures handled correctly downstream analyses.

title: Labels and Missing Values
text: var_label() val_labels() get set variable value labels find_var() searches variables name label pattern to_label(), to_character(), to_numeric(), to_labelled() convert formats set_na() declares values missing; unlabel() strips metadata copy_labels() restores labels dplyr operations; drop_labels() cleans unused labels

title: Labels and Missing Values
text: Transform recode variables — see vignette("data-transformation") Import data SPSS, Stata, SAS — see vignette("data-io") Start analyzing data — see vignette("descriptive-statistics")

title: Regression Analysis
text: Regression analysis predicts outcome one predictors. mariposa provides two regression functions SPSS-compatible output: functions support two interface styles: Formula: linear_regression(data, y ~ x1 + x2) — standard R syntax SPSS-style: linear_regression(data, dependent = y, predictors = c(x1, x2))

title: Regression Analysis
code: linear_regression(survey_data, life_satisfaction ~ age) #> Linear Regression: life_satisfaction ~ age #>   R2 = 0.001, adj.R2 = 0.000, F(1, 2419) = 2.00, p = 0.158 , N = 2421

title: Regression Analysis
text: detailed output includes four sections matching SPSS REGRESSION: Model Summary: R, R-squared, Adjusted R-squared ANOVA Table: Overall model significance Coefficients: B (unstandardized), Beta (standardized), t, p, confidence intervals Descriptives: Mean SD variables

title: Regression Analysis
text: B (unstandardized): 1-unit increase predictor, outcome changes B units Beta (standardized): Allows comparison across predictors different scales. Beta 0.30 means 1-SD increase predictor associated 0.30-SD change outcome p-value: 0.05 indicates statistical significance

title: Regression Analysis
text: Compare Beta values identify strongest predictor.
code: linear_regression(survey_data,                   life_satisfaction ~ age + income + trust_government) #> Linear Regression: life_satisfaction ~ age + income + trust_government #>   R2 = 0.198, adj.R2 = 0.197, F(3, 1991) = 163.89, p < 0.001 ***, N = 1995

title: Regression Analysis
code: linear_regression(survey_data,                   dependent = life_satisfaction,                   predictors = c(age, income, trust_government)) #> Linear Regression: life_satisfaction ~ age + income + trust_government #>   R2 = 0.198, adj.R2 = 0.197, F(3, 1991) = 163.89, p < 0.001 ***, N = 1995

title: Regression Analysis
text: Weights treated frequency weights, matching SPSS WEIGHT behavior.
code: linear_regression(survey_data,                   life_satisfaction ~ age + income,                   weights = sampling_weight) #> Linear Regression: life_satisfaction ~ age + income [Weighted] #>   R2 = 0.203, adj.R2 = 0.202, F(2, 2127) = 270.42, p < 0.001 ***, N = 2130

title: Regression Analysis
text: Run separate regressions subgroup:
code: survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   linear_regression(life_satisfaction ~ age + income) #> Linear Regression: life_satisfaction ~ age + income [Grouped: region] #>   region = East: R2 = 0.203, adj.R2 = 0.199, F(2, 407) = 51.95, p < 0.001 ***, N = 410 #>   region = West: R2 = 0.201, adj.R2 = 0.200, F(2, 1702) = 214.58, p < 0.001 ***, N = 1705

title: Regression Analysis
text: R-squared tells much variance predictors explain: 0.01 – 0.05: Small effect 0.06 – 0.13: Medium effect 0.14+: Large effect benchmarks follow Cohen (1988). Always check ANOVA table confirm overall model significance.

title: Regression Analysis
text: Combine data transformation functions better models:
code: # Standardize predictors for comparable coefficients survey_data_z <- survey_data %>%   std(age, income, suffix = "_z")  linear_regression(survey_data_z,                   life_satisfaction ~ age_z + income_z + trust_government,                   weights = sampling_weight) #> Linear Regression: life_satisfaction ~ age_z + income_z + trust_government [Weighted] #>   R2 = 0.200, adj.R2 = 0.199, F(3, 2005) = 167.49, p < 0.001 ***, N = 2009

title: Regression Analysis
text: Use logistic_regression() outcome binary. First, create binary variable:
code: survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(high_satisfaction = ifelse(life_satisfaction >= 4, 1, 0))

title: Regression Analysis
code: logistic_regression(survey_data, high_satisfaction ~ age + income) #> Logistic Regression: high_satisfaction ~ age + income #>   Nagelkerke R2 = 0.209, chi2(2) = 357.43, p < 0.001 ***, Accuracy = 68.4%, N = 2115

title: Regression Analysis
text: detailed output includes five sections matching SPSS LOGISTIC REGRESSION: Omnibus Test: Overall model significance Model Summary: -2 Log Likelihood pseudo R-squared values Hosmer-Lemeshow Test: Model fit assessment Classification Table: Prediction accuracy Coefficients: B, Wald, Exp(B) (odds ratios), confidence intervals

title: Regression Analysis
text: Exp(B) odds ratio — key statistic logistic regression: Exp(B) > 1: unit increase raises odds (e.g., 1.50 = 50% higher odds) Exp(B) < 1: unit increase lowers odds (e.g., 0.80 = 20% lower odds) Exp(B) = 1: effect

title: Regression Analysis
code: logistic_regression(survey_data,                     high_satisfaction ~ age + income + trust_government + education) #> Logistic Regression: high_satisfaction ~ age + income + trust_government + education #>   Nagelkerke R2 = 0.212, chi2(6) = 341.28, p < 0.001 ***, Accuracy = 68.1%, N = 1995

title: Regression Analysis
code: logistic_regression(survey_data,                     dependent = high_satisfaction,                     predictors = c(age, income, trust_government)) #> Logistic Regression: high_satisfaction ~ age + income + trust_government #>   Nagelkerke R2 = 0.207, chi2(3) = 333.76, p < 0.001 ***, Accuracy = 68.4%, N = 1995

title: Regression Analysis
code: logistic_regression(survey_data,                     high_satisfaction ~ age + income,                     weights = sampling_weight) #> Logistic Regression: high_satisfaction ~ age + income [Weighted] #>   Nagelkerke R2 = 0.208, chi2(2) = 357.40, p < 0.001 ***, Accuracy = 68.3%, N = 2130

title: Regression Analysis
code: survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   logistic_regression(high_satisfaction ~ age + income) #> Logistic Regression: high_satisfaction ~ age + income [Grouped: region] #>   region = East: Nagelkerke R2 = 0.178, chi2(2) = 57.88, p < 0.001 ***, Accuracy = 66.8%, N = 410 #>   region = West: Nagelkerke R2 = 0.218, chi2(2) = 301.11, p < 0.001 ***, Accuracy = 68.8%, N = 1705

title: Regression Analysis
text: Pseudo R-squared values directly comparable linear regression R-squared: Nagelkerke R-squared: Adjusted reach 1.0, commonly reported Cox & Snell R-squared: reach 1.0, always lower McFadden R-squared: Values 0.20 indicate good fit Hosmer-Lemeshow Test: Non-significant (p>.05p > .05) means model fits well. Classification Table: Compare correct predictions base rate — model outperform guessing common category.

title: Regression Analysis
Regression Analysis
text: Check correlations first. Use pearson_cor() explore bivariate relationships building model. Center standardize predictors. Centering makes intercept interpretable; standardizing (std()) makes Beta coefficients comparable. Use std(method = "2sd") make continuous predictors comparable binary ones. Compare Beta values. multiple regression, standardized Beta coefficients reveal predictor strongest effect, regardless measurement scale. Watch multicollinearity. Highly correlated predictors produce unstable coefficients. Check bivariate correlations interpreting results. Never use linear_regression() binary outcome. Predicted values can fall outside 0–1, statistical tests invalid. Use logistic_regression() instead. Report completely. Include R-squared, F-test Omnibus test, individual coefficients, sample size.

title: Regression Analysis
text: linear_regression() predicts continuous outcomes (B, Beta, ANOVA, R-squared) logistic_regression() predicts binary outcomes (odds ratios, classification, pseudo R-squared) support formula SPSS-style interfaces, survey weights, grouped analysis Use std() center() prepare predictors better interpretability Always explore bivariate relationships building regression models

title: Regression Analysis
text: Prepare variables std() center() — see vignette("data-transformation") Construct reliable scale scores — see vignette("scale-analysis") Compare groups directly — see vignette("hypothesis-testing") Handle survey weights — see vignette("survey-weights")

title: Scale Analysis
text: Scale analysis combines multiple survey items single score reliably measures concept. typical workflow : Check reliability reliability() — items measure thing? Explore structure efa() — items group dimensions? Create scores row_means() — compute mean indices Standardize pomps() — transform comparable 0–100 scale guide uses trust items survey_data (trust government, media, science).

title: Scale Analysis
code: reliability(survey_data, trust_government, trust_media, trust_science) #> Reliability Analysis: 3 items #>   Cronbach's Alpha = 0.047 (Poor), McDonald's Omega = 0.047, N = 2135

title: Scale Analysis
text: detailed output shows item-total correlations, alpha--item-deleted, inter-item correlations — matching SPSS RELIABILITY.
code: rel <- reliability(survey_data, trust_government, trust_media, trust_science) summary(rel) #>  #> Reliability Analysis Results #> ---------------------------- #> - Items: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - N of Items: 3 #>  #> Reliability Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>   Cronbach's Alpha:              0.047 #>   Alpha (standardized):          0.048 #>   McDonald's Omega:              0.047 #>   Omega (standardized):          0.048 #>   N of Items:                    3 #>   N (listwise):                  2135 #>  #> Item Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item  mean    sd    n #>  trust_government 2.621 1.162 2135 #>       trust_media 2.430 1.156 2135 #>     trust_science 3.624 1.034 2135 #>  #> Inter-Item Correlation Matrix: #> ------------------------------  #>                  trust_government trust_media trust_science #> trust_government            1.000       0.014         0.020 #> trust_media                 0.014       1.000         0.015 #> trust_science               0.020       0.015         1.000 #> ------------------------------  #>  #> Item-Total Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item scale_mean_deleted scale_var_deleted corrected_r #>  trust_government               6.05             2.440       0.024 #>       trust_media               6.25             2.467       0.020 #>     trust_science               5.05             2.723       0.025 #>  alpha_deleted omega_deleted #>          0.029            NA #>          0.040            NA #>          0.027            NA #> Note: Omega if item deleted requires at least 4 items #> (a one-factor model on the remaining 2 items is not identified).

title: Scale Analysis
text: Alpha > 0.90: Excellent 0.80 – 0.90: Good 0.70 – 0.80: Acceptable 0.60 – 0.70: Questionable 0.60: Reconsider items Item-Total Correlation shows well item fits scale. Values 0.40 indicate good fit; 0.20 suggests item belong. Alpha Item Deleted shows happens without item. alpha increases remove item, item weakens scale.

title: Scale Analysis
text: Alongside alpha, reliability() reports McDonald’s Omega, factor-model-based reliability coefficient. Alpha assumes items measure construct equally well; omega fits one-factor model lets item carry loading, usually makes accurate estimate item loadings differ. thresholds alpha commonly applied. Omega needs least 3 items (2 items reported NA), Omega Item Deleted appears item-total table scales 4 items. Note: omega currently R-statistic (SPSS reference run yet); see vignette("spss-compatibility") validation status.

title: Scale Analysis
code: reliability(survey_data, trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,             weights = sampling_weight) #> Reliability Analysis: 3 items [Weighted] #>   Cronbach's Alpha = 0.052 (Poor), McDonald's Omega = 0.053, N = 2150

title: Scale Analysis
code: reliability(survey_data, starts_with("trust")) #> Reliability Analysis: 3 items #>   Cronbach's Alpha = 0.047 (Poor), McDonald's Omega = 0.047, N = 2135

title: Scale Analysis
text: Check whether reliability holds across subgroups: scale works well overall might unreliable specific subgroups. Always check sample spans diverse populations.
code: survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   reliability(trust_government, trust_media, trust_science) #> [region = 1] #> Reliability Analysis: 3 items #>   Cronbach's Alpha = 0.037 (Poor), McDonald's Omega = 0.349, N = 422 #> [region = 2] #> Reliability Analysis: 3 items #>   Cronbach's Alpha = 0.050 (Poor), McDonald's Omega = 0.071, N = 1713

title: Scale Analysis
text: many items, efa() reveals group underlying dimensions:

title: Scale Analysis
text: KMO (Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin) measures sampling adequacy factor analysis: 0.80: Good excellent 0.60 – 0.80: Acceptable 0.60: Factor analysis may appropriate Bartlett’s Test significant (p<.05p < .05), confirming meaningful correlations exist. Eigenvalues show variance explained per component. default, components eigenvalue > 1 retained (Kaiser criterion). Factor Loadings show item-component associations: 0.70: Strong 0.40 – 0.70: Moderate 0.40: Suppressed default

title: Scale Analysis
text: Varimax (default) — assumes uncorrelated factors: Promax — allows correlated factors, produces Pattern Structure matrices: Oblimin — another oblique rotation, common psychology:
code: efa(survey_data,     political_orientation, environmental_concern, life_satisfaction,     trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,     rotation = "varimax") #> Exploratory Factor Analysis: 6 items, 3 components (PCA/Varimax) #>   KMO = 0.505 (Miserable), Variance explained: 61.0% efa(survey_data,     political_orientation, environmental_concern, life_satisfaction,     trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,     rotation = "promax") #> Exploratory Factor Analysis: 6 items, 3 components (PCA/Promax) #>   KMO = 0.505 (Miserable), Variance explained: 61.0% # Requires GPArotation package efa(survey_data,     political_orientation, environmental_concern, life_satisfaction,     trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,     rotation = "oblimin")

title: Scale Analysis
text: default, efa() uses PCA (Principal Component Analysis). true factor analysis model, use Maximum Likelihood: ML extraction provides goodness--fit test uses SMC (squared multiple correlations) initial communalities. Combine extraction rotation:
code: efa(survey_data,     political_orientation, environmental_concern, life_satisfaction,     trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,     extraction = "ml") #> Exploratory Factor Analysis: 6 items, 3 components (ML/Varimax) #>   KMO = 0.505 (Miserable), Variance explained: 61.0% efa(survey_data,     political_orientation, environmental_concern, life_satisfaction,     trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,     extraction = "ml", rotation = "promax") #> Exploratory Factor Analysis: 6 items, 3 components (ML/Promax) #>   KMO = 0.505 (Miserable), Variance explained: 61.0%

title: Scale Analysis
code: efa(survey_data,     political_orientation, environmental_concern, life_satisfaction,     trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,     n_factors = 2) #> Exploratory Factor Analysis: 6 items, 2 components (PCA/Varimax) #>   KMO = 0.505 (Miserable), Variance explained: 44.0%

title: Scale Analysis
code: efa(survey_data,     political_orientation, environmental_concern, life_satisfaction,     trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,     weights = sampling_weight) #> Exploratory Factor Analysis: 6 items, 3 components (PCA/Varimax) [Weighted] #>   KMO = 0.505 (Miserable), Variance explained: 61.0%

title: Scale Analysis
text: confirming reliability, create scores using row operation functions. details row_means(), row_sums(), row_count(), pomps(), see vignette("data-transformation").

title: Scale Analysis
code: # Create mean index survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(m_trust = row_means(., trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,                              min_valid = 2))  # Transform to 0-100 scale survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(trust_pomps = pomps(m_trust, scale_min = 1, scale_max = 5))  # Check the result survey_data %>%   describe(m_trust, trust_pomps) #>  #> Descriptive Statistics #> ---------------------- #>     Variable   Mean Median     SD Range IQR Skewness    N Missing #>      m_trust  2.916      3  0.691     4   1     0.02 2484      16 #>  trust_pomps 47.892     50 17.283   100  25     0.02 2484      16 #> ----------------------------------------

title: Scale Analysis
code: # Group comparison survey_data %>%   t_test(m_trust, group = gender, weights = sampling_weight) #> t-Test: m_trust by gender [Weighted] #>   t(2457.6) = -2.362, p = 0.018 *, g = -0.095 (negligible), N = 2499 # As a predictor in regression survey_data %>%   linear_regression(life_satisfaction ~ m_trust + age + income,                     weights = sampling_weight) #> Linear Regression: life_satisfaction ~ m_trust + age + income [Weighted] #>   R2 = 0.201, adj.R2 = 0.200, F(3, 2109) = 177.02, p < 0.001 ***, N = 2113

title: Scale Analysis
code: # 1. Check reliability rel <- reliability(survey_data, trust_government, trust_media, trust_science) rel #> Reliability Analysis: 3 items #>   Cronbach's Alpha = 0.047 (Poor), McDonald's Omega = 0.047, N = 2135 summary(rel) #>  #> Reliability Analysis Results #> ---------------------------- #> - Items: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - N of Items: 3 #>  #> Reliability Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>   Cronbach's Alpha:              0.047 #>   Alpha (standardized):          0.048 #>   McDonald's Omega:              0.047 #>   Omega (standardized):          0.048 #>   N of Items:                    3 #>   N (listwise):                  2135 #>  #> Item Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item  mean    sd    n #>  trust_government 2.621 1.162 2135 #>       trust_media 2.430 1.156 2135 #>     trust_science 3.624 1.034 2135 #>  #> Inter-Item Correlation Matrix: #> ------------------------------  #>                  trust_government trust_media trust_science #> trust_government            1.000       0.014         0.020 #> trust_media                 0.014       1.000         0.015 #> trust_science               0.020       0.015         1.000 #> ------------------------------  #>  #> Item-Total Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item scale_mean_deleted scale_var_deleted corrected_r #>  trust_government               6.05             2.440       0.024 #>       trust_media               6.25             2.467       0.020 #>     trust_science               5.05             2.723       0.025 #>  alpha_deleted omega_deleted #>          0.029            NA #>          0.040            NA #>          0.027            NA #> Note: Omega if item deleted requires at least 4 items #> (a one-factor model on the remaining 2 items is not identified).  # 2. Explore factor structure efa_result <- efa(survey_data, trust_government, trust_media, trust_science) efa_result #> Exploratory Factor Analysis: 3 items, 1 component (PCA/Unrotated) #>   KMO = 0.506 (Miserable), Variance explained: 34.7%  # 3. Create mean index (Alpha was acceptable) survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(m_trust = row_means(., trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,                              min_valid = 2))  # 4. Transform to POMPS survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(trust_pomps = pomps(m_trust, scale_min = 1, scale_max = 5))  # 5. Use in further analysis survey_data %>%   group_by(education) %>%   describe(m_trust, trust_pomps, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Descriptive Statistics #> ------------------------------- #>  #> Group: education = Basic Secondary #> ---------------------------------- #> ---------------------------------------- #>     Variable   Mean Median     SD Range IQR Skewness Effective_N #>      m_trust  2.924      3  0.696     4   1    0.024       829.7 #>  trust_pomps 48.093     50 17.390   100  25    0.024       829.7 #> ---------------------------------------- #>  #> Group: education = Intermediate Secondary #> ----------------------------------------- #> ---------------------------------------- #>     Variable   Mean Median     SD Range IQR Skewness Effective_N #>      m_trust  2.920      3  0.697     4   1    0.035       617.7 #>  trust_pomps 48.002     50 17.421   100  25    0.035       617.7 #> ---------------------------------------- #>  #> Group: education = Academic Secondary #> ------------------------------------- #> ---------------------------------------- #>     Variable   Mean Median     SD Range    IQR Skewness Effective_N #>      m_trust  2.922      3  0.691     4  0.833   -0.006       616.4 #>  trust_pomps 48.053     50 17.267   100 20.833   -0.006       616.4 #> ---------------------------------------- #>  #> Group: education = University #> ----------------------------- #> ---------------------------------------- #>     Variable   Mean Median     SD Range IQR Skewness Effective_N #>      m_trust  2.885      3  0.686     4   1    0.063       389.6 #>  trust_pomps 47.132     50 17.157   100  25    0.063       389.6 #> ----------------------------------------

title: Scale Analysis
text: Always check reliability first. mean index unreliable items produces meaningless results. Aim Alpha > .70. Use min_valid wisely. 3–5 item scales, min_valid = 2 reasonable compromise. longer scales, require least half items. Specify theoretical scale_min / scale_max pomps(). Using observed values makes scores sample-dependent non-comparable. Run EFA 6+ items. Factor analysis can reveal unexpected dimensions create indices. Check grouped reliability. scale works well overall may unreliable specific subgroups (e.g., different regions education levels).

title: Scale Analysis
text: reliability() checks whether items form consistent scale (Cronbach’s Alpha) efa() discovers underlying dimensions (PCA ML extraction, Varimax/Oblimin/Promax rotation) row_means() creates mean indices; row_sums() row_count() provide alternatives pomps() transforms scores comparable 0–100 scale Always validate reliability creating scale scores

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: Survey weights correct sampling biases results represent target population, just sample. Common reasons weighting include oversampling small groups, differential non-response, post-stratification known demographics. mariposa, adding weights simple weights = sampling_weight function call. guide covers dedicated w_* functions, effective sample size concept, best practices.

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: difference weighted unweighted results reflects biased sample . Larger differences mean weights important work.
code: # Without weights (describes the sample) unweighted <- survey_data %>%   summarise(     mean_age = mean(age, na.rm = TRUE),     mean_income = mean(income, na.rm = TRUE)   )  # With weights (represents the population) age_w <- w_mean(survey_data, age, weights = sampling_weight) income_w <- w_mean(survey_data, income, weights = sampling_weight)  comparison <- data.frame(   Variable = c("Age", "Income"),   Unweighted = c(unweighted$mean_age, unweighted$mean_income),   Weighted = c(age_w$results$weighted_mean, income_w$results$weighted_mean) ) print(comparison) #>   Variable Unweighted  Weighted #> 1      Age    50.5496   50.5144 #> 2   Income  3753.9341 3743.0994

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: mariposa provides 11 w_* functions individual weighted statistics.

title: Working with Survey Weights
code: # Weighted mean w_mean(survey_data, income, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Mean Statistics #> ------------------------ #>  #> --- income --- #>  Variable weighted_mean Effective_N #>    income      3743.099      2158.9  # Weighted median w_median(survey_data, income, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Median Statistics #> -------------------------- #>  #> --- income --- #>  Variable weighted_median Effective_N #>    income            3500      2158.9  # Weighted mode w_modus(survey_data, education, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Mode Statistics #> ------------------------ #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>   Variable  weighted_mode   effective_n #>   <chr>     <ord>                 <dbl> #> 1 education Basic Secondary       2469.

title: Working with Survey Weights
code: # Weighted standard deviation w_sd(survey_data, income, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Standard Deviation Statistics #> -------------------------------------- #>  #> --- income --- #>  Variable weighted_sd Effective_N #>    income    1423.966      2158.9  # Weighted variance w_var(survey_data, income, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Variance Statistics #> ---------------------------- #>  #> --- income --- #>  Variable weighted_var Effective_N #>    income      2027678      2158.9  # Weighted interquartile range w_iqr(survey_data, income, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Interquartile Range Statistics #> --------------------------------------- #>  #> --- income --- #>  Variable weighted_iqr Effective_N #>    income         1900      2158.9

title: Working with Survey Weights
code: # Weighted skewness w_skew(survey_data, income, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Skewness Statistics #> ---------------------------- #>  #> --- income --- #>  Variable weighted_skew Effective_N #>    income         0.725      2158.9  # Weighted kurtosis w_kurtosis(survey_data, income, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Excess Kurtosis Statistics #> ----------------------------------- #>  #> --- income --- #>  Variable weighted_kurtosis Effective_N #>    income             0.388      2158.9

title: Working with Survey Weights
code: # Weighted standard error w_se(survey_data, income, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Standard Error Statistics #> ---------------------------------- #>  #> --- income --- #>  Variable weighted_se Effective_N #>    income      30.353      2158.9  # Weighted quantiles w_quantile(survey_data, income,            probs = c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75),            weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Quantile Statistics #> ---------------------------- #>  Variable Quantile Value    N Effective_N         Weights #>    income      25%  2700 2186      2158.9 sampling_weight #>    income      50%  3500 2186      2158.9 sampling_weight #>    income      75%  4600 2186      2158.9 sampling_weight #> ----------------------------------------  # Weighted range w_range(survey_data, income, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Range Statistics #> ------------------------- #>  #> --- income --- #>  Variable weighted_range Effective_N #>    income           7200      2158.9

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: w_* functions accept multiple variables:
code: w_mean(survey_data, age, income, life_satisfaction,        weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Mean Statistics #> ------------------------ #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable weighted_mean Effective_N #>       age        50.514      2468.8 #>  #> --- income --- #>  Variable weighted_mean Effective_N #>    income      3743.099      2158.9 #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>           Variable weighted_mean Effective_N #>  life_satisfaction         3.625      2390.9

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: Weighting reduces statistical precision. effective sample size (neffn_{eff}) tells much information weighted sample actually carries: neff=(∑wi)2∑wi2n_{eff} = \frac{\left(\sum w_i\right)^2}{\sum w_i^2} design effect 1.0 means weights impact precision. Values 1 mean larger standard errors unweighted sample size.
code:
actual_n <- nrow(survey_data) age_w <- w_mean(survey_data, age, weights = sampling_weight) effective_n <- age_w$results$effective_n  cat("Actual sample size:", actual_n, "
") #> Actual sample size: 2500 cat("Effective sample size:", round(effective_n), "
") #> Effective sample size: 2469 cat("Design effect:", round(actual_n / effective_n, 2), "
") #> Design effect: 1.01

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: Every analysis function mariposa supports weights argument:
code: # Descriptive statistics survey_data %>%   describe(income, life_satisfaction, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Descriptive Statistics #> ------------------------------- #>           Variable     Mean Median       SD Range  IQR Skewness Effective_N #>             income 3743.099   3500 1423.966  7200 1900    0.725      2158.9 #>  life_satisfaction    3.625      4    1.152     4    2   -0.499      2390.9 #> ---------------------------------------- # Frequency tables survey_data %>%   frequency(education, weights = sampling_weight) #> Frequency: education [Weighted] #>   4 categories, N valid = 2516, missing = 0 #> Use summary() for detailed output. # t-test survey_data %>%   t_test(income, group = gender, weights = sampling_weight) #> t-Test: income by gender [Weighted] #>   t(2178.7) = 0.751, p = 0.453 , g = 0.032 (negligible), N = 2201 # ANOVA survey_data %>%   oneway_anova(life_satisfaction, group = education,                weights = sampling_weight) #> One-Way ANOVA: life_satisfaction by education [Weighted] #>   F(3, 2432) = 65.333, p < 0.001 ***, eta2 = 0.075 (medium), N = 2437 # Chi-square survey_data %>%   chi_square(education, employment, weights = sampling_weight) #> Chi-Squared Test: education × employment [Weighted] #>   chi2(12) = 130.696, p < 0.001 ***, V = 0.132 (small), N = 2518 # Correlation survey_data %>%   pearson_cor(age, income, weights = sampling_weight) #> Pearson Correlation: age x income [Weighted] #>   r = -0.005, p = 0.828 , N = 2201 # Regression survey_data %>%   linear_regression(life_satisfaction ~ age + income,                     weights = sampling_weight) #> Linear Regression: life_satisfaction ~ age + income [Weighted] #>   R2 = 0.203, adj.R2 = 0.202, F(2, 2127) = 270.42, p < 0.001 ***, N = 2130

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: functions work seamlessly group_by():
code: survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   describe(age, income, life_satisfaction,            weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Descriptive Statistics #> ------------------------------- #>  #> Group: region = East #> -------------------- #> ---------------------------------------- #>           Variable     Mean Median       SD Range  IQR Skewness Effective_N #>                age   52.278     53   17.595    77   24    0.098       477.0 #>             income 3760.687   3600 1388.321  7200 1700    0.721       421.9 #>  life_satisfaction    3.623      4    1.203     4    2   -0.558       457.4 #> ---------------------------------------- #>  #> Group: region = West #> -------------------- #> ---------------------------------------- #>           Variable     Mean Median       SD Range  IQR Skewness Effective_N #>                age   50.067     49   16.927    77   24    0.170      1993.1 #>             income 3738.586   3500 1433.325  7200 1900    0.727      1738.1 #>  life_satisfaction    3.625      4    1.139     4    2   -0.481      1934.8 #> ---------------------------------------- survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   t_test(income, group = gender, weights = sampling_weight) #> [region = 1] #> t-Test: income by gender [Weighted] #>   t(431.2) = 1.674, p = 0.095 , g = 0.158 (negligible), N = 450 #> [region = 2] #> t-Test: income by gender [Weighted] #>   t(1740.2) = 0.009, p = 0.993 , g = 0.000 (negligible), N = 1751

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: rule thumb: weights range 0.2 5. Extreme values (0.1 10) may indicate problems weighting scheme.
code: weight_stats <- survey_data %>%   summarise(     min = min(sampling_weight, na.rm = TRUE),     max = max(sampling_weight, na.rm = TRUE),     mean = mean(sampling_weight, na.rm = TRUE),     sd = sd(sampling_weight, na.rm = TRUE)   ) print(weight_stats) #> # A tibble: 1 × 4 #>     min   max  mean    sd #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 0.702  1.40  1.01 0.113

title: Working with Survey Weights
code: missing <- sum(is.na(survey_data$sampling_weight)) total <- nrow(survey_data) cat("Missing weights:", missing, "/", total,     "(", round(missing / total * 100, 1), "%)\n") #> Missing weights: 0 / 2500 ( 0 %)

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: weights extreme, trim reduce variance cost small increase bias:
code: trimmed <- survey_data %>%   mutate(     weight_trimmed = case_when(       sampling_weight > 5 ~ 5,       sampling_weight < 0.2 ~ 0.2,       TRUE ~ sampling_weight     )   )  original <- w_mean(survey_data, income, weights = sampling_weight) trimmed_result <- w_mean(trimmed, income, weights = weight_trimmed)  cat("Original weighted mean:", round(original$results$weighted_mean, 1), "\n") #> Original weighted mean: 3743.1 cat("Trimmed weighted mean:", round(trimmed_result$results$weighted_mean, 1), "\n") #> Trimmed weighted mean: 3743.1

title: Working with Survey Weights
code: # 1. Inspect weight properties survey_data %>%   summarise(     n = n(),     mean_weight = mean(sampling_weight, na.rm = TRUE),     sd_weight = sd(sampling_weight, na.rm = TRUE),     min_weight = min(sampling_weight, na.rm = TRUE),     max_weight = max(sampling_weight, na.rm = TRUE)   ) #> # A tibble: 1 × 5 #>       n mean_weight sd_weight min_weight max_weight #>   <int>       <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1  2500        1.01     0.113      0.702       1.40  # 2. Compare weighted vs unweighted vars <- c("age", "income", "life_satisfaction") for (var in vars) {   unw <- mean(survey_data[[var]], na.rm = TRUE)   w_result <- w_mean(survey_data, !!sym(var), weights = sampling_weight)   w <- w_result$results$weighted_mean   cat(sprintf("%s: unweighted = %.2f, weighted = %.2f (diff = %.1f%%)\n",               var, unw, w, (w - unw) / unw * 100)) } #> age: unweighted = 50.55, weighted = 50.51 (diff = -0.1%) #> income: unweighted = 3753.93, weighted = 3743.10 (diff = -0.3%) #> life_satisfaction: unweighted = 3.63, weighted = 3.62 (diff = -0.1%)  # 3. Weighted group comparison survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   describe(income, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Descriptive Statistics #> ------------------------------- #>  #> Group: region = East #> -------------------- #> ---------------------------------------- #>  Variable     Mean Median       SD Range  IQR Skewness Effective_N #>    income 3760.687   3600 1388.321  7200 1700    0.721       421.9 #> ---------------------------------------- #>  #> Group: region = West #> -------------------- #> ---------------------------------------- #>  Variable     Mean Median       SD Range  IQR Skewness Effective_N #>    income 3738.586   3500 1433.325  7200 1900    0.727      1738.1 #> ----------------------------------------  # 4. Weighted hypothesis test survey_data %>%   t_test(income, group = gender, weights = sampling_weight) #> t-Test: income by gender [Weighted] #>   t(2178.7) = 0.751, p = 0.453 , g = 0.032 (negligible), N = 2201

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: Always use weights provided. correct sampling biases make results generalizable. Report weighted unweighted sample sizes. actual n tells readers data collection; effective n tells statistical precision. Check extreme weights. Large variation inflates standard errors reduces statistical power. Consider trimming necessary. Document weight source. Explain weights calculated adjust (e.g., age, gender, region post-stratification). Report effective sample size. tells readers much precision lost due weighting.

title: Working with Survey Weights
text: Survey weights make results representative population, just sample 11 w_* functions provide individual weighted statistics analysis functions support weights via weights = effective sample size measures much precision weighting costs Check document weight distributions, extreme values, missing weights

what: mariposa - Professional Survey Analysis in R
title: mariposa - Professional Survey Analysis in R
text: Professional statistical analysis survey data R. mariposa (Marburg Initiative Political Social Analysis) provides 76 functions importing, managing, transforming, analyzing survey data. Covers full workflow data import (SPSS, Stata, SAS, Excel) label management, recoding, standardization statistical analysis survey weights, grouped operations via dplyr::group_by(), publication-ready output. Statistical results validated SPSS v29 within documented per-tier tolerances; see vignette("spss-compatibility") per-function status.

title: mariposa - Professional Survey Analysis in R
code: # Install from GitHub devtools::install_github("YannickDiehl/mariposa")

title: mariposa - Professional Survey Analysis in R
code: library(mariposa) library(dplyr)  # Load example survey data (2,500 respondents) data(survey_data)  # Interactive HTML codebook in RStudio Viewer codebook(survey_data)  # Descriptive statistics with survey weights survey_data %>%   describe(age, income, life_satisfaction, weights = sampling_weight)  # Frequency table survey_data %>%   frequency(education, weights = sampling_weight)  # Compare groups with t-test survey_data %>%   t_test(life_satisfaction, group = gender, weights = sampling_weight)  # Detailed SPSS-style output with summary() survey_data %>%   t_test(life_satisfaction, group = gender, weights = sampling_weight) %>%   summary()  # Scale analysis workflow reliability(survey_data, trust_government, trust_media, trust_science) %>%   summary()    # item statistics, inter-item correlations  survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(m_trust = row_means(., trust_government, trust_media, trust_science))  # Regression survey_data %>%   linear_regression(life_satisfaction ~ age + income, weights = sampling_weight) %>%   summary()    # coefficients, ANOVA table, diagnostics

title: mariposa - Professional Survey Analysis in R
text: statistical functions accept survey weights:
code: # Weighted mean, median, SD survey_data %>%   w_mean(age, income, weights = sampling_weight)  # Grouped weighted analysis survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   describe(satisfaction, weights = sampling_weight)

title: mariposa - Professional Survey Analysis in R
text: Full support pipes grouped operations:
code: survey_data %>%   filter(age >= 18) %>%   group_by(region) %>%   t_test(life_satisfaction, group = gender, weights = sampling_weight)

title: mariposa - Professional Survey Analysis in R
code: # Factorial ANOVA with Type III SS survey_data %>%   factorial_anova(dv = income, between = c(gender, education),                   weights = sampling_weight)  # ANCOVA with covariate adjustment survey_data %>%   ancova(dv = income, between = gender, covariate = age,          weights = sampling_weight)

title: mariposa - Professional Survey Analysis in R
code: # Parametric: ANOVA → Tukey/Scheffe result <- survey_data %>%   oneway_anova(life_satisfaction, group = education, weights = sampling_weight)  result %>% tukey_test()    # Pairwise comparisons result %>% levene_test()   # Variance homogeneity  # Non-parametric: Kruskal-Wallis → Dunn kw_result <- survey_data %>%   kruskal_wallis(life_satisfaction, group = education)  kw_result %>% dunn_test()  # Pairwise Dunn comparisons

title: mariposa - Professional Survey Analysis in R
text: Every analysis function provides two output levels. Typing result name prints compact one-line summary. Calling summary() produces full SPSS-style output details. can toggle individual sections : works analysis functions — t_test(), oneway_anova(), chi_square(), pearson_cor(), reliability(), linear_regression(), .
code: # Compact one-line summary (default) survey_data %>%   t_test(life_satisfaction, group = gender)  # Full detailed output survey_data %>%   t_test(life_satisfaction, group = gender) %>%   summary()  # Toggle individual sections survey_data %>%   t_test(life_satisfaction, group = gender) %>%   summary(effect_sizes = FALSE)

title: mariposa - Professional Survey Analysis in R
text: Statistical functions validated SPSS v29 within documented per-tier tolerances, typically across four scenarios: weighted/unweighted grouped/ungrouped. statistics SPSS equivalent (example, weighted variants rank-based tests, SPSS’s NPAR TESTS support) covered internal regression tests instead. See vignette("spss-compatibility") per-function validation status. ’re migrating SPSS, workflows translate directly: SPSS: mariposa:
code: WEIGHT BY sampling_weight. T-TEST GROUPS=gender(1 2)   /VARIABLES=satisfaction. survey_data %>%   t_test(satisfaction, group = gender, weights = sampling_weight)

title: Analysis of Covariance: ANCOVA — ancova
text: ancova() tests whether group means differ controlling one continuous covariates. performs factorial ANCOVA using Type III Sum Squares, matching SPSS UNIANOVA output keyword. Think : ANOVA removes effect confounding variables first Testing group differences "adjusted" means Combining regression (covariates) ANOVA (factors) one model test tells : Whether factor significant effect controlling covariates relationship covariate outcome Effect sizes factors covariates (partial eta squared) Estimated marginal means (group means adjusted covariates)

title: Analysis of Covariance: ANCOVA — ancova
code: ancova(data, dv, between, covariate, weights = NULL, ss_type = 3)

title: Analysis of Covariance: ANCOVA — ancova
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) dv numeric dependent variable analyze (unquoted) Character vector unquoted variable names specifying -subjects factors (1-3 factors). must categorical variables (factor, character, labelled numeric). covariate Unquoted variable names continuous covariates control . Use c(age, income) multiple covariates. weights Optional survey weights population-representative results (unquoted variable name) ss_type Deprecated; 3 (Type III, SPSS default) implemented. Passing 2 issues warning computes Type III.

title: Analysis of Covariance: ANCOVA — ancova
text: object class "ancova" containing: anova_table Tibble Source, SS, df, MS, F, p, Partial Eta Squared parameter_estimates Tibble regression coefficients (B, SE, t, p) descriptives Tibble unadjusted cell means, SDs, Ns estimated_marginal_means Tibble adjusted means (covariates grand mean) levene_test Tibble Levene's test results r_squared R-squared Adjusted R-squared model underlying lm model object call_info List metadata (dv, factors, covariates, weighted, etc.) Use summary() full SPSS-style output toggleable sections.

title: Analysis of Covariance: ANCOVA — ancova
text: Adjusted Means (Estimated Marginal Means): group means statistically removing effect covariate(s). answer: "group means groups covariate values?" Covariate Effects: covariate row ANOVA table shows whether covariate significantly predicts DV adjusting factors. Factor Effects: show whether factor affects DV controlling covariate. primary test interest. Partial Eta Squared (Effect Size): Less 0.01: Negligible 0.01 0.06: Small 0.06 0.14: Medium 0.14 greater: Large

title: Analysis of Covariance: ANCOVA — ancova
text: Use ANCOVA : group comparisons (ANOVA) want control confound covariate continuous linearly related DV want increase statistical power removing known variance sources covariate's relationship DV across groups (homogeneity regression slopes assumption)

title: Analysis of Covariance: ANCOVA — ancova
text: Huitema, B. E. (2011). Analysis Covariance Alternatives (2nd ed.). Wiley. IBM Corp. (2023). IBM SPSS Statistics 29 Algorithms. IBM Corporation.

title: Analysis of Covariance: ANCOVA — ancova
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: ‘dplyr’ #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:stats’: #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:base’: #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union data(survey_data)  # One-way ANCOVA: income by education, controlling for age survey_data %>%   ancova(dv = income, between = c(education), covariate = c(age)) #> ANCOVA: income by education, covariate: age #>   age (covariate): F(1, 2181) = 0.030, p = 0.862 , eta2p = 0.000 #>   education:       F(3, 2181) = 466.246, p < 0.001 ***, eta2p = 0.391, N = 2186  # Two-way ANCOVA with weights survey_data %>%   ancova(dv = income, between = c(gender, education),          covariate = c(age), weights = sampling_weight) #> ANCOVA: income by gender, education, covariate: age [Weighted] #>   age (covariate):  F(1, 2177) = 0.013, p = 0.911 , eta2p = 0.000 #>   gender:           F(1, 2177) = 0.115, p = 0.735 , eta2p = 0.000 #>   education:        F(3, 2177) = 455.614, p < 0.001 ***, eta2p = 0.386 #>   gender:education: F(3, 2177) = 0.298, p = 0.827 , eta2p = 0.000, N = 2186  # Multiple covariates survey_data %>%   ancova(dv = income, between = c(education),          covariate = c(age, political_orientation)) #> ANCOVA: income by education, covariate: age, political_orientation #>   age (covariate):                   F(1, 2002) = 0.018, p = 0.895 , eta2p = 0.000 #>   political_orientation (covariate): F(1, 2002) = 1.366, p = 0.243 , eta2p = 0.001 #>   education:                         F(3, 2002) = 419.350, p < 0.001 ***, eta2p = 0.386, N = 2008  # --- Three-layer output --- result <- ancova(survey_data, dv = income, between = c(education),                  covariate = c(age)) result              # compact overview #> ANCOVA: income by education, covariate: age #>   age (covariate): F(1, 2181) = 0.030, p = 0.862 , eta2p = 0.000 #>   education:       F(3, 2181) = 466.246, p < 0.001 ***, eta2p = 0.391, N = 2186 summary(result)     # full detailed output with all sections #> ANCOVA (One-Way ANCOVA) Results #> ------------------------------- #>  #> - Dependent variable: income #> - Factor(s): education #> - Covariate(s): age #> - Type III Sum of Squares: Type 3 #> - N (complete cases): 2186 #> - Missing: 314 #>  #> Tests of Between-Subjects Effects #> -----------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  Source          Type III SS  df   Mean Square  F        Sig.  Partial Eta Sq #>  Corrected Model 1.752821e+09    4 4.382053e+08  349.723 <.001 0.391          #>  Intercept       3.472401e+09    1 3.472401e+09 2771.252 <.001 0.560          #>  age             3.772141e+04    1 3.772141e+04    0.030 0.862 0.000          #>  education       1.752631e+09    3 5.842102e+08  466.246 <.001 0.391          #>  Error           2.732810e+09 2181 1.253008e+06                               #>  Total           3.529079e+10 2186                                            #>  Corrected Total 4.485631e+09 2185                                            #>      #>  *** #>  *** #>      #>  *** #>      #>      #>      #> -----------------------------------------------------------------------------  #> R Squared = 0.391 (Adjusted R Squared = 0.390) #>  #> Parameter Estimates #> ---------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  Parameter   B        Std. Error t      Sig.  Lower Bound Upper Bound #>  (Intercept) 3990.641 75.806     52.643 <.001 3841.981    4139.301    #>  age           -0.245  1.412     -0.174 0.862   -3.014       2.524    #>  education.L 1870.578 50.838     36.795 <.001 1770.881    1970.274    #>  education.Q  139.427 49.566      2.813 0.005   42.226     236.629    #>  education.C  152.695 48.167      3.170 0.002   58.236     247.154    #>  Partial Eta Sq #>  0.560          #>  0.000          #>  0.383          #>  0.004          #>  0.005          #> ---------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Estimated Marginal Means #> (Evaluated at covariate means) #> -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  education              Mean     Std. Error Lower Bound Upper Bound #>  Basic Secondary        2758.939 41.294     2677.960    2839.918    #>  Intermediate Secondary 3592.634 47.822     3498.852    3686.416    #>  Academic Secondary     4224.320 47.836     4130.511    4318.130    #>  University             5336.870 59.438     5220.309    5453.431    #> -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Levene's Test of Equality of Error Variances #>   F(3, 2182) = 103.901, p = <.001 #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 summary(result, marginal_means = FALSE)  # hide estimated marginal means #> ANCOVA (One-Way ANCOVA) Results #> ------------------------------- #>  #> - Dependent variable: income #> - Factor(s): education #> - Covariate(s): age #> - Type III Sum of Squares: Type 3 #> - N (complete cases): 2186 #> - Missing: 314 #>  #> Tests of Between-Subjects Effects #> -----------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  Source          Type III SS  df   Mean Square  F        Sig.
