what: Test Whether a Proportion Matches an Expected Value — binomial_test
title: Test Whether a Proportion Matches an Expected Value — binomial_test
text: binomial_test() tests whether observed proportion binary variable differs hypothesized proportion. uses exact binomial test, making valid sample size. Think : Testing whether coin fair (proportion heads = 50 percent) Checking sample's gender ratio matches population Verifying satisfaction rates meet target proportion test tells : Whether observed proportion differs significantly expected exact p-value (based binomial distribution) confidence interval true proportion

title: Test Whether a Proportion Matches an Expected Value — binomial_test
code: binomial_test(data, ..., p = 0.5, weights = NULL, conf.level = 0.95)

title: Test Whether a Proportion Matches an Expected Value — binomial_test
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... One binary variables test. must exactly 2 categories (e.g., Yes/, Male/Female, 0/1, TRUE/FALSE) p hypothesized proportion test (Default: 0.50 = 50 percent). refers proportion first category (first factor level, lower numeric value). weights Optional survey weights population-representative results conf.level Confidence level intervals (Default: 0.95 = 95 percent)

title: Test Whether a Proportion Matches an Expected Value — binomial_test
text: Test results showing whether proportion differs expected, including: Category counts observed proportions Test proportion (null hypothesis) Exact p-value (two-sided) Confidence interval true proportion

title: Test Whether a Proportion Matches an Expected Value — binomial_test
text: P-value: p < 0.05, proportion differs significantly test proportion p < 0.001: strong evidence proportion differs p < 0.01: Strong evidence proportion differs p < 0.05: Moderate evidence proportion differs p > 0.05: significant difference expected proportion Observed Proportion: actual proportion data. Compare test proportion see direction difference. Confidence Interval: range likely contain true population proportion. test proportion falls outside range, result significant.

title: Test Whether a Proportion Matches an Expected Value — binomial_test
text: Use binomial test : want compare observed proportion known value variable exactly 2 categories need exact test (relying normal approximation) Sample size small (chi-square may reliable)

title: Test Whether a Proportion Matches an Expected Value — binomial_test
text: testing association two categorical variables: Use chi_square() instead comparing proportions groups: Use chi-square z-test proportions larger samples normal approximation:

title: Test Whether a Proportion Matches an Expected Value — binomial_test
text: SPSS NPAR TESTS ignores WEIGHT , weighted results SPSS reference. weighted variant R-frequency-weight extension reduces exactly unweighted test weights equal 1 (enforced internal invariance suite); see vignette("spss-compatibility") validation status. Results similar one-sample z-test proportions

title: Test Whether a Proportion Matches an Expected Value — binomial_test
text: Conover, W. J. (1999). Practical nonparametric statistics (3rd ed.). John Wiley & Sons.

title: Test Whether a Proportion Matches an Expected Value — binomial_test
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Test whether gender split is 50/50 survey_data %>%   binomial_test(gender, p = 0.50) #> Binomial Test: gender #>   Group 1 (Male): prop = 0.478 vs 0.500, p = 0.026 *, N = 2500 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Test whether East region proportion is 50% survey_data %>%   binomial_test(region, p = 0.50) #> Binomial Test: region #>   Group 1 (East): prop = 0.194 vs 0.500, p < 0.001 ***, N = 2500 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Multiple variables at once survey_data %>%   binomial_test(gender, region, p = 0.50) #> Binomial Test: gender #>   Group 1 (Male): prop = 0.478 vs 0.500, p = 0.026 *, N = 2500 #> Binomial Test: region #>   Group 1 (East): prop = 0.194 vs 0.500, p < 0.001 ***, N = 2500 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Weighted analysis survey_data %>%   binomial_test(gender, p = 0.50, weights = sampling_weight) #> Binomial Test: gender [Weighted] #>   Group 1 (Male): prop = 0.478 vs 0.500, p = 0.026 *, N = 2500 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Grouped analysis (separate test per region) survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   binomial_test(gender, p = 0.50) #> [region = 1] #> Binomial Test: gender #>   Group 1 (Male): prop = 0.491 vs 0.500, p = 0.716 , N = 485 #> [region = 2] #> Binomial Test: gender #>   Group 1 (Male): prop = 0.474 vs 0.500, p = 0.023 *, N = 2015 #> Use summary() for detailed output.

title: Center Variables (Mean Centering) — center
text: Centers variables subtracting mean. used grouped data frame (via dplyr::group_by()), becomes group-mean centering — R equivalent separate centering within group.

title: Center Variables (Mean Centering) — center
code: center(data, ..., weights = NULL, suffix = NULL, na.rm = TRUE)

title: Center Variables (Mean Centering) — center
text: data data frame numeric vector. ... Variables center (tidyselect). used data data frame. weights Optional survey weights (unquoted column name numeric vector). provided, weighted mean subtracted instead unweighted mean. suffix character string appended column names (e.g., "_c"). NULL (default), original columns overwritten. na.rm Remove missing values computing mean? Default: TRUE.

title: Center Variables (Mean Centering) — center
text: data vector, centered numeric vector. data data frame, modified data frame (invisibly).

title: Center Variables (Mean Centering) — center
text: Grand-mean centering (ungrouped): Subtracts overall mean. centered value 0 means respondent sample average. Group-mean centering (grouped): Subtracts group mean. Useful multilevel models separate within-group -group effects. replaces sjmisc's separate de_mean() function.

title: Center Variables (Mean Centering) — center
text: weights provided, weighted mean used centering. accounts survey design centering computation.

title: Center Variables (Mean Centering) — center
code: library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Grand-mean centering data <- center(survey_data, income, age, suffix = "_c")  # Weighted centering data <- center(survey_data, income, age,                weights = sampling_weight, suffix = "_c")  # Group-mean centering (replaces sjmisc::de_mean) data <- survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   center(income, age, suffix = "_gmc")

what: Test If Two Categories Are Related — chi_square
title: Test If Two Categories Are Related — chi_square
text: chi_square() helps discover two categorical variables related independent. example, education level related voting preference? independent ? test tells : Whether relationship statistically significant strong relationship (effect sizes) patterns exist data

title: Test If Two Categories Are Related — chi_square
code: chi_square(data, ..., weights = NULL, correct = FALSE)

title: Test If Two Categories Are Related — chi_square
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... Two categorical variables test (e.g., gender, region) weights Optional survey weights population-representative results correct Apply continuity correction small samples? (Default: FALSE)

title: Test If Two Categories Are Related — chi_square
text: Test results showing whether variables related, including: Chi-squared statistic p-value Observed vs expected frequencies Effect sizes measure relationship strength Use summary() full SPSS-style output toggleable sections.

title: Test If Two Categories Are Related — chi_square
text: P-value: p < 0.05, variables likely related (independent) p < 0.001: strong evidence relationship p < 0.01: Strong evidence relationship p < 0.05: Moderate evidence relationship p ≥ 0.05: significant relationship found Effect Sizes (strong relationship?): Cramer's V: Works table size (0 = relationship, 1 = perfect relationship) < 0.1: Negligible relationship 0.1-0.3: Small relationship 0.3-0.5: Medium relationship 0.5 higher: Large relationship Phi: 2x2 tables (similar interpretation Cramer's V) Gamma: ordinal data (-1 +1, shows direction relationship)

title: Test If Two Categories Are Related — chi_square
text: Use chi-squared test : variables categorical (gender, region, education level, etc.) want know related independent least 5 observations cells

title: Test If Two Categories Are Related — chi_square
text: Observed: actually found data Expected: expect variables independent Large differences suggest relationship exists

title: Test If Two Categories Are Related — chi_square
text: Check cells least 5 observations Use weights population estimates Look significance (p-value) strength (effect sizes) Consider using crosstab() detailed percentage breakdowns

title: Test If Two Categories Are Related — chi_square
text: Pearson, K. (1900). criterion given system deviations probable case correlated system variables can reasonably supposed arisen random sampling. Philosophical Magazine, 50(302), 157–175. Cramer, H. (1946). Mathematical Methods Statistics. Princeton University Press. IBM Corp. (2023). IBM SPSS Statistics 29 Algorithms. IBM Corporation.

title: Test If Two Categories Are Related — chi_square
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Basic chi-squared test for independence survey_data %>% chi_square(gender, region) #> Chi-Squared Test: gender × region #>   chi2(1) = 0.415, p = 0.519 , V = 0.013 (neglig.), N = 2500  # With weights survey_data %>% chi_square(gender, education, weights = sampling_weight) #> Chi-Squared Test: gender × education [Weighted] #>   chi2(3) = 4.403, p = 0.221 , V = 0.042 (neglig.), N = 2517  # Grouped analysis survey_data %>%    group_by(region) %>%    chi_square(gender, employment) #> [region = 1] #> Chi-Squared Test: gender × employment #>   chi2(4) = 5.970, p = 0.201 , V = 0.111 (small), N = 485 #> [region = 2] #> Chi-Squared Test: gender × employment #>   chi2(4) = 4.166, p = 0.384 , V = 0.045 (neglig.), N = 2015  # With continuity correction survey_data %>% chi_square(gender, region, correct = TRUE) #> Chi-Squared Test: gender × region #>   chi2(1) = 0.353, p = 0.553 , V = 0.012 (neglig.), N = 2500  # --- Three-layer output --- result <- chi_square(survey_data, gender, education) result              # compact one-line overview #> Chi-Squared Test: gender × education #>   chi2(3) = 3.470, p = 0.325 , V = 0.037 (neglig.), N = 2500 summary(result)     # full detailed output with all sections #>  #> Chi-Squared Test of Independence  #> --------------------------------- #>  #> - Variables: gender × education #>  #> Observed Frequencies: #>         education #> gender   Basic Secondary Intermediate Seco... Academic Secondary University #>   Male               401                  289                320        184 #>   Female             440                  340                311        215 #>  #> Expected Frequencies: #>         education #> gender   Basic Secondary Intermediate Seco... Academic Secondary University #>   Male           401.662               300.41            301.366    190.562 #>   Female         439.338               328.59            329.634    208.438 #>  #> Chi-Squared Test Results: #> --------------------------------------------------  #>  Chi_squared df p_value sig #>         3.47  3   0.325     #> --------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Effect Sizes: #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------  #>     Measure  Value p_value sig Interpretation #>  Cramer's V  0.037   0.325            Neglig. #>       Gamma -0.008   0.850               Weak #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------  #> Table size: 2×4 | N = 2500 #> Note: Phi coefficient only shown for 2x2 tables #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 summary(result, cross_tabulation = FALSE)  # hide cross-tabulation #>  #> Chi-Squared Test of Independence  #> --------------------------------- #>  #> - Variables: gender × education #>  #> Observed Frequencies: #>         education #> gender   Basic Secondary Intermediate Seco... Academic Secondary University #>   Male               401                  289                320        184 #>   Female             440                  340                311        215 #>  #> Expected Frequencies: #>         education #> gender   Basic Secondary Intermediate Seco... Academic Secondary University #>   Male           401.662               300.41            301.366    190.562 #>   Female         439.338               328.59            329.634    208.438 #>  #> Chi-Squared Test Results: #> --------------------------------------------------  #>  Chi_squared df p_value sig #>         3.47  3   0.325     #> --------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Effect Sizes: #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------  #>     Measure  Value p_value sig Interpretation #>  Cramer's V  0.037   0.325            Neglig. #>       Gamma -0.008   0.850               Weak #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------  #> Table size: 2×4 | N = 2500 #> Note: Phi coefficient only shown for 2x2 tables #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05

title: Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test — chisq_gof
text: chisq_gof() tests whether observed frequencies categorical variable match expected frequencies. default, tests equal proportions (uniform distribution). can also specify custom expected proportions. Think : Testing whether categories equally distributed Comparing observed distribution theoretical distribution one-sample version chi-square test test tells : Whether observed frequencies differ expected frequencies strong deviation (chi-square statistic) frequency table observed, expected, residual counts

title: Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test — chisq_gof
code: chisq_gof(data, ..., expected = NULL, weights = NULL)

title: Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test — chisq_gof
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... One categorical variables test (tidyselect supported) expected Optional numeric vector expected proportions (must sum 1). used single variable tested. NULL (default), equal proportions assumed. weights Optional survey weights population-representative results

title: Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test — chisq_gof
text: Test results showing whether observed frequencies match expected, including: Chi-square statistic (chi_squared) p-value variable Degrees freedom Frequency table observed, expected, residual counts Sample size (N)

title: Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test — chisq_gof
text: P-value: p < 0.05, distribution differs expected p < 0.001: strong evidence distribution differs p < 0.01: Strong evidence distribution differs p < 0.05: Moderate evidence distribution differs p >= 0.05: significant deviation expected distribution Residuals: difference observed expected counts. Large positive residuals indicate category cases expected; large negative residuals indicate fewer cases expected.

title: Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test — chisq_gof
text: Use test : want check whether categorical variable follows specific distribution want test categories equally distributed (uniform) single categorical variable hypothesised distribution

title: Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test — chisq_gof
text: $$\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$$ O_i = observed frequency, E_i = expected frequency. Degrees freedom = number categories - 1.

title: Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test — chisq_gof
text: testing association two categorical variables: Use chi_square() instead testing single binary proportion: Use binomial_test() instead small samples expected frequencies 5: Use fisher_test() instead

title: Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test — chisq_gof
text: SPSS: NPAR TESTS /CHISQUARE=variable /EXPECTED=EQUAL : NPAR TESTS /CHISQUARE=variable /EXPECTED=50 30 20

title: Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test — chisq_gof
text: Pearson, K. (1900). criterion given system deviations probable case correlated system variables can reasonably supposed arisen random sampling. Philosophical Magazine, 50(302), 157-175.

title: Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test — chisq_gof
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Test whether gender is equally distributed survey_data %>%   chisq_gof(gender) #> Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test: gender #>   chi2(1) = 5.018, p = 0.025 *, N = 2500 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Test multiple variables at once survey_data %>%   chisq_gof(gender, region, education) #> Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test: gender #>   chi2(1) = 5.018, p = 0.025 *, N = 2500 #> Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test: region #>   chi2(1) = 936.360, p < 0.001 ***, N = 2500 #> Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test: education #>   chi2(3) = 156.454, p < 0.001 ***, N = 2500 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Custom expected proportions survey_data %>%   chisq_gof(interview_mode, expected = c(0.5, 0.3, 0.2)) #> Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test: interview_mode #>   chi2(2) = 95.413, p < 0.001 ***, N = 2500 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # With weights survey_data %>%   chisq_gof(gender, weights = sampling_weight) #> Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test: gender [Weighted] #>   chi2(1) = 6.310, p = 0.012 *, N = 2516 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Grouped analysis survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   chisq_gof(education) #> [region = 1] #> Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test: education #>   chi2(3) = 34.645, p < 0.001 ***, N = 485 #> [region = 2] #> Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test: education #>   chi2(3) = 122.888, p < 0.001 ***, N = 2015 #> Use summary() for detailed output.

what: Create a Codebook for Your Data — codebook
title: Create a Codebook for Your Data — codebook
text: codebook() creates interactive HTML data dictionary opens RStudio Viewer. shows everything dataset glance: variable names, types, labels, empirical values found data, value labels, frequency counts. Think professional "cheat sheet" dataset – especially useful working labelled survey data imported SPSS, Stata, SAS.

title: Create a Codebook for Your Data — codebook
code: codebook(   data,   ...,   weights = NULL,   show_id = TRUE,   show_type = TRUE,   show_labels = TRUE,   show_values = TRUE,   show_freq = TRUE,   show_na = TRUE,   show_unused = FALSE,   max_values = 10,   max_len = 50,   sort_by_name = FALSE,   file = NULL,   view = interactive() )

title: Create a Codebook for Your Data — codebook
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... Optional: specific variables include. empty, variables shown. Supports tidyselect helpers like starts_with("trust"). weights Optional survey weights weighted frequency calculations show_id Show variable position number? (Default: TRUE) show_type Show data type? (Default: TRUE) show_labels Show variable labels? (Default: TRUE) show_values Show empirical values value labels? (Default: TRUE) show_freq Show frequency counts? (Default: TRUE) show_na Show tagged missing value types codebook? (Default: TRUE). data imported read_spss(), read_stata(), read_sas(), read_xpt() using tagged NAs, displayed original missing value codes, labels, frequencies valid values, separated thin gray line. show_unused Show defined value labels, even zero observations? (Default: FALSE). Useful seeing full codebook including response options respondent selected. max_values Maximum number values display per variable truncating showing range (Default: 10) max_len Maximum character width labels truncation (Default: 50) sort_by_name Sort variables alphabetically instead position? (Default: FALSE) file Path save HTML codebook. NULL (default), file written unless codebook opened Viewer (temporary file used). directory file must already exist. view Open HTML codebook RStudio Viewer (browser)? Defaults interactive(), interactive sessions open Viewer scripts/tests . Set view = FALSE suppress Viewer side effect entirely; file = writing unaffected argument.

title: Create a Codebook for Your Data — codebook
text: Invisibly returns list class "codebook" containing: codebook Tibble one row per variable metadata data_info List dataset-level information (name, nrow, ncol, etc.) html generated HTML htmltools object weights Name weight variable, NULL options List display options frequencies Named list frequency tables per variable

title: Create a Codebook for Your Data — codebook
text: variable, codebook displays (depending options): ID: Column position dataset Name: Variable name (monospace font) Type: Data type (numeric, factor, ordered factor, haven_labelled, etc.) Label: Variable label (SPSS/Stata/SAS imports manual assignment) Values: empirical values found data Value Labels: Labels assigned values () Freq.: Frequency count value

title: Create a Codebook for Your Data — codebook
text: Use codebook() : First receive new dataset want understand structure Work labelled data (SPSS, Stata, SAS) need see value labels Want document dataset colleagues publications Need quickly see value distributions across variables

title: Create a Codebook for Your Data — codebook
code: data(survey_data)  # Compact console overview (no Viewer) cb <- codebook(survey_data) print(cb) #>  #> Codebook: survey_data #> 16 variables | 2,500 observations | 15 labelled #> Types: 3 dbl, 2 fct(2), 1 fct(3), 1 fct(5), 1 fct(6), 7 int, 1 ord(4) #> -- Use summary() for details or codebook(..., view = TRUE) for the HTML viewer  # Detailed console output summary(cb) #>  #> Codebook #> -------- #>   Dataset: survey_data #>   Observations: 2,500 #>   Variables: 16 (10 numeric, 6 factor, 0 character) #>   Variables with labels: 15 #>   Variables with missing data: 7 #>  #> Variable Details: #> --------------------------------------------------  #>  #> [1] id (integer) #>     Values: 1 - 2500 (2500 distinct) #>  #> [2] age (numeric) #>     Label: Age in years #>     Values: 18 - 95 (78 distinct) #>  #> [3] gender (factor) #>     Label: Gender #>     Values: Male, Female #>     Value labels: #>       Male = Male #>       Female = Female #>  #> [4] region (factor) #>     Label: Region (East/West) #>     Values: East, West #>     Value labels: #>       East = East #>       West = West #>  #> [5] education (ordered factor) #>     Label: Highest educational attainment #>     Values: Basic Secondary, Intermediate Secondary, Academic Secondary, Univer... #>     Value labels: #>       Basic Secondary = Basic Secondary #>       Intermediate Secondary = Intermediate Secondary #>       Academic Secondary = Academic Secondary #>       University = University #>  #> [6] income (numeric) #>     Label: Monthly household income (EUR) #>     Values: 800 - 8000 (73 distinct) #>  #> [7] employment (factor) #>     Label: Employment status #>     Values: Student, Employed, Unemployed, Retired, Other #>     Value labels: #>       Student = Student #>       Employed = Employed #>       Unemployed = Unemployed #>       Retired = Retired #>       Other = Other #>  #> [8] political_orientation (integer) #>     Label: Political orientation (1=left, 5=right) #>     Values: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 #>  #> [9] environmental_concern (integer) #>     Label: Environmental concern (1=low, 5=high) #>     Values: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 #>  #> [10] life_satisfaction (integer) #>     Label: Life satisfaction (1=dissatisfied, 5=satisfied) #>     Values: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 #>  #> [11] trust_government (integer) #>     Label: Trust in government (1=none, 5=complete) #>     Values: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 #>  #> [12] trust_media (integer) #>     Label: Trust in media (1=none, 5=complete) #>     Values: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 #>  #> [13] trust_science (integer) #>     Label: Trust in science (1=none, 5=complete) #>     Values: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 #>  #> [14] sampling_weight (numeric) #>     Label: Weighting factor #>     Values: 0.7016 - 1.398 (2500 distinct) #>  #> [15] stratum (factor) #>     Label: Stratification variable #>     Values: East_Middle, East_Old, East_Young, West_Middle, West_Old, West_Young #>     Value labels: #>       East_Middle = East_Middle #>       East_Old = East_Old #>       East_Young = East_Young #>       West_Middle = West_Middle #>       West_Old = West_Old #>       West_Young = West_Young #>  #> [16] interview_mode (factor) #>     Label: Interview mode #>     Values: Face-to-face, Telephone, Online #>     Value labels: #>       Face-to-face = Face-to-face #>       Telephone = Telephone #>       Online = Online  # \donttest{ # Full codebook (opens in RStudio Viewer when interactive) codebook(survey_data)  # Only trust-related variables codebook(survey_data, starts_with("trust"))  # Save to file for sharing codebook(survey_data, file = tempfile(fileext = ".html")) # }

what: Copy Labels from One Data Frame to Another — copy_labels
title: Copy Labels from One Data Frame to Another — copy_labels
text: Copies variable labels, value labels, tagged NA metadata source data frame target data frame. essential dplyr operations like dplyr::filter(), dplyr::select(), dplyr::mutate() can strip label attributes.

title: Copy Labels from One Data Frame to Another — copy_labels
code: copy_labels(data, source)

title: Copy Labels from One Data Frame to Another — copy_labels
text: data target data frame (e.g., filtering subsetting). source source data frame original labels.

title: Copy Labels from One Data Frame to Another — copy_labels
text: target data frame labels copied source. columns present data frames affected. Columns target left unchanged.

title: Copy Labels from One Data Frame to Another — copy_labels
text: following attributes copied shared column: "label" — variable label "labels" — value labels "na_tag_map" — tagged NA mapping "na_tag_format" — tagged NA format (spss/stata/sas) "class" — vector class (e.g., haven_labelled)

title: Copy Labels from One Data Frame to Another — copy_labels
code: # Labels are lost after dplyr operations data_subset <- dplyr::filter(survey_data, age >= 18)  # Restore them data_subset <- copy_labels(data_subset, survey_data)

what: Compare Two Categories: See How They Relate — crosstab
title: Compare Two Categories: See How They Relate — crosstab
text: crosstab() shows two categorical variables relate . creates table reveals patterns - like whether education level differs region, gender influences product preferences. Think two-way frequency table shows: many people fall combination categories percentage cell represents Whether patterns associations

title: Compare Two Categories: See How They Relate — crosstab
code: crosstab(   data,   row,   col,   weights = NULL,   percentages = c("row", "none", "col", "total", "all"),   na.rm = TRUE,   digits = 1 )

title: Compare Two Categories: See How They Relate — crosstab
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) row variable table rows (e.g., education, age_group) col variable table columns (e.g., region, gender) weights Optional survey weights population-representative results percentages percentages show: "row" (default): Percentages across row (adds 100% horizontally) "col": Percentages column (adds 100% vertically) "total": Percentage entire table "": Show three types "none": Just counts, percentages na.rm Remove missing values calculating? (Default: TRUE) digits Decimal places percentages (Default: 1)

title: Compare Two Categories: See How They Relate — crosstab
text: cross-tabulation table showing relationship two variables

title: Compare Two Categories: See How They Relate — crosstab
text: crosstab table shows: Cell counts: Number people combination Row %: Distribution within row (e.g., "Among high school education, X% live East") Column %: Distribution within column (e.g., "Among East, X% high school education") Total %: Percentage entire sample (e.g., "X% respondents high school education live East")

title: Compare Two Categories: See How They Relate — crosstab
text: Use crosstab want : See two categorical variables related Compare distributions across groups Find patterns survey responses Create demographic breakdowns

title: Compare Two Categories: See How They Relate — crosstab
text: Row %: Use row variable grouping factor (e.g., "region vary education level?") Column %: Use column variable grouping factor (e.g., "education vary region?") Total %: Use understand overall sample composition

title: Compare Two Categories: See How They Relate — crosstab
text: Start row column percentages, Use chi-squared test check relationship statistically significant Watch small cell counts (< 5) may unreliable Consider combining sparse categories many cells empty

title: Compare Two Categories: See How They Relate — crosstab
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Basic crosstab survey_data %>% crosstab(gender, region) #> Crosstab: gender x region #>   2 x 2 table, N = 2500 #>   Note: no significance test included - use chi_square() for a test of independence. #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # With weights and all percentages survey_data %>% crosstab(gender, education,                          weights = sampling_weight,                          percentages = "all") #> Crosstab: gender x education [Weighted] #>   2 x 4 table, N = 2516 #>   Note: no significance test included - use chi_square() for a test of independence. #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Grouped analysis survey_data %>%   group_by(employment) %>%   crosstab(gender, region, weights = sampling_weight) #> Crosstab: gender x region [Weighted] #>   [employment = Student] 2 x 2 table, N = 80 #>   [employment = Employed] 2 x 2 table, N = 1603 #>   [employment = Unemployed] 2 x 2 table, N = 184 #>   [employment = Retired] 2 x 2 table, N = 534 #>   [employment = Other] 2 x 2 table, N = 115 #>   Note: no significance test included - use chi_square() for a test of independence. #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Column percentages only survey_data %>% crosstab(education, employment, percentages = "col") #> Crosstab: education x employment #>   4 x 5 table, N = 2500 #>   Note: no significance test included - use chi_square() for a test of independence. #> Use summary() for detailed output.

what: Get to Know Your Numeric Data — describe
title: Get to Know Your Numeric Data — describe
text: describe() gives complete summary numeric variables - like age, income, satisfaction scores. first step analysis, helping understand typical, unusual, spread data . Think health check data reveals: average value? middle value? spread responses? unusual patterns outliers?

title: Get to Know Your Numeric Data — describe
code: describe(   data,   ...,   weights = NULL,   show = "short",   probs = c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75),   na.rm = TRUE,   excess = TRUE )

title: Get to Know Your Numeric Data — describe
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... numeric variables want summarize. List separated commas, use helpers like starts_with("trust") weights Optional survey weights population-representative results. Without weights, describe sample. weights, describe population. show statistics display: "short" (default): Essential stats (mean, median, SD, range, IQR, skewness) "": Everything including variance, kurtosis, mode, quantiles Custom list: Choose specific stats like c("mean", "sd", "range") probs quantiles, percentiles show (default: 25th, 50th, 75th) na.rm Remove missing values calculating? (Default: TRUE) excess kurtosis, show excess kurtosis? (Default: TRUE, easier interpret)

title: Get to Know Your Numeric Data — describe
text: summary table descriptive statistics variable

title: Get to Know Your Numeric Data — describe
text: Key statistics tell : n: many valid responses (watch many missing) Mean: average value Median: middle value (half , half ) SD: Standard deviation - spread values Range: minimum maximum values IQR: Interquartile range - middle 50% values Skewness: Whether data leans left (negative) right (positive) Kurtosis: Whether unusual outliers

title: Get to Know Your Numeric Data — describe
text: Always start ! Use describe() : Check data quality (impossible values?) Understand distributions testing Spot outliers might affect analyses Compare groups side side

title: Get to Know Your Numeric Data — describe
text: Mean ≈ Median: Data roughly symmetric Mean > Median: Right-skewed (tail extends right) Mean < Median: Left-skewed (tail extends left) Large SD: Responses vary widely Small SD: Responses similar

title: Get to Know Your Numeric Data — describe
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Basic unweighted analysis survey_data %>% describe(age) #>  #> Descriptive Statistics #> ---------------------- #>  Variable  Mean Median     SD Range IQR Skewness    N Missing #>       age 50.55     50 16.976    77  24    0.172 2500       0 #> ----------------------------------------  # Weighted analysis survey_data %>% describe(age, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Descriptive Statistics #> ------------------------------- #>  Variable   Mean Median     SD Range IQR Skewness Effective_N #>       age 50.514     50 17.084    77  25    0.159      2468.8 #> ----------------------------------------  # Multiple variables with custom statistics survey_data %>% describe(age, income, life_satisfaction,                          weights = sampling_weight,                          show = c("mean", "sd", "skew")) #>  #> Weighted Descriptive Statistics #> ------------------------------- #>           Variable     Mean       SD Skewness Effective_N #>                age   50.514   17.084    0.159      2468.8 #>             income 3743.099 1423.966    0.725      2158.9 #>  life_satisfaction    3.625    1.152   -0.499      2390.9 #> ----------------------------------------  # Grouped analysis survey_data %>%    group_by(region) %>%    describe(age, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Descriptive Statistics #> ------------------------------- #>  #> Group: region = East #> -------------------- #> ---------------------------------------- #>  Variable   Mean Median     SD Range IQR Skewness Effective_N #>       age 52.278     53 17.595    77  24    0.098         477 #> ---------------------------------------- #>  #> Group: region = West #> -------------------- #> ---------------------------------------- #>  Variable   Mean Median     SD Range IQR Skewness Effective_N #>       age 50.067     49 16.927    77  24     0.17      1993.1 #> ----------------------------------------

title: Remove Unused Value Labels — drop_labels
text: Removes value labels values present data. useful filtering subsetting, categories may longer exist labels remain attached.

title: Remove Unused Value Labels — drop_labels
code: drop_labels(data, ..., drop_na = FALSE)

title: Remove Unused Value Labels — drop_labels
text: data data frame single vector. ... Optional: unquoted variable names (tidyselect supported). empty, applies labelled columns. drop_na TRUE, also removes tagged NA labels. Default: FALSE (tagged NA labels preserved even tagged NAs type exist).

title: Remove Unused Value Labels — drop_labels
text: data unused labels removed.

title: Remove Unused Value Labels — drop_labels
text: useful subsetting data (e.g., filtering category). removed category's label still exists variable, can cause confusing output frequency() codebook(). drop_labels() cleans keeping labels values actually present data. default, tagged NA labels preserved (drop_na = FALSE) represent missing value types, substantive categories.

title: Remove Unused Value Labels — drop_labels
code: # After filtering, region == 4 no longer exists but its label remains data_subset <- dplyr::filter(survey_data, region != 4) data_clean <- drop_labels(data_subset)

what: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
text: dunn_test() tells exactly groups different Kruskal-Wallis finds overall differences. non-parametric equivalent Tukey Scheffe post-hoc test. Think : Kruskal-Wallis says "differences somewhere" Dunn test says "specifically, Group differs Group C" way make possible pairwise comparisons using rank-based statistics

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
code: dunn_test(x, ...)  # Default S3 method dunn_test(x, ...)

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
text: x Kruskal-Wallis results kruskal_wallis() ... Additional arguments passed methods. method kruskal_wallis objects accepts p_adjust (character): method adjusting p-values multiple comparisons. Options: "bonferroni" (default, conservative), "holm", "BH", "hochberg", "hommel", "", "fdr", "none".

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
text: Pairwise comparison results showing: group pairs significantly different Z-statistics based rank differences Adjusted p-values (controlling multiple comparisons)

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
text: Z-Statistics: Based differences mean ranks groups Large absolute Z values indicate big rank differences Positive Z: First group higher mean rank second Negative Z: Second group higher mean rank first Adjusted P-values: Control multiple comparisons p < 0.05: Groups significantly different p >= 0.05: significant difference groups

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
text: pair groups (, j): $$Z_{ij} = \frac{\bar{R}_i - \bar{R}_j}{\sqrt{\frac{N(N+1)}{12}   \left(\frac{1}{n_i} + \frac{1}{n_j}\right)}}$$ \(\bar{R}_i\) mean rank group , \(N\) total sample size, \(n_i\) size group .

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
text: Bonferroni (default): conservative, multiplies p number comparisons Holm: Step-method, less conservative Bonferroni BH: Controls false discovery rate, good many comparisons

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
text: Use Dunn test : Kruskal-Wallis test shows significant differences (p < 0.05) want know specific groups differ data violates normality assumptions ordinal data skewed distributions

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
text: Non-parametric equivalent tukey_test() scheffe_test() Follow-kruskal_wallis(), just like Tukey follows ANOVA

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
text: parent kruskal_wallis() result weighted, pairwise z statistics use frequency-weighted midranks. SPSS NPAR TESTS ignores WEIGHT , weighted results SPSS reference (R-, guarded internal invariance suite); see vignette("spss-compatibility") validation status. Uses ranks instead raw values, making robust outliers

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
text: Dunn, O. J. (1964). Multiple comparisons using rank sums. Technometrics, 6(3), 241-252.

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After Kruskal-Wallis — dunn_test
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Perform Kruskal-Wallis followed by Dunn post-hoc test kw_result <- survey_data %>%   kruskal_wallis(life_satisfaction, group = education)  # Dunn post-hoc comparisons (default: Bonferroni) kw_result %>% dunn_test() #> Dunn Post-Hoc Test (Bonferroni) by education #>   life_satisfaction: 6 comparisons, 5 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table.  # With Holm correction (less conservative) kw_result %>% dunn_test(p_adjust = "holm") #> Dunn Post-Hoc Test (Holm) by education #>   life_satisfaction: 6 comparisons, 6 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table.  # With Benjamini-Hochberg (controls false discovery rate) kw_result %>% dunn_test(p_adjust = "BH") #> Dunn Post-Hoc Test (Benjamini-Hochberg) by education #>   life_satisfaction: 6 comparisons, 6 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table.  # With weights kw_weighted <- survey_data %>%   kruskal_wallis(life_satisfaction, group = education,                  weights = sampling_weight)  kw_weighted %>% dunn_test() #> Dunn Post-Hoc Test (Bonferroni) by education [Weighted] #>   life_satisfaction: 6 comparisons, 5 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table.  # Multiple variables kw_multi <- survey_data %>%   kruskal_wallis(life_satisfaction, trust_government,                  group = education)  kw_multi %>% dunn_test() #> Dunn Post-Hoc Test (Bonferroni) by education #>   life_satisfaction: 6 comparisons, 5 significant (p < .05) #>   trust_government: 6 comparisons, 0 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table.  # Grouped analysis kw_grouped <- survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   kruskal_wallis(life_satisfaction, group = education)  kw_grouped %>% dunn_test() #> Dunn Post-Hoc Test (Bonferroni) by education #> [region = East] #>   life_satisfaction: 6 comparisons, 2 significant (p < .05) #> [region = West] #>   life_satisfaction: 6 comparisons, 5 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table.

what: Explore the Structure Behind Your Survey Items — efa
title: Explore the Structure Behind Your Survey Items — efa
text: efa() performs Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA) discover underlying patterns survey items. Supports Principal Component Analysis (PCA) Maximum Likelihood (ML) extraction. R equivalent SPSS's FACTOR procedure. example, 6 items measuring different attitudes, EFA can reveal whether group 2-3 underlying dimensions (factors components).

title: Explore the Structure Behind Your Survey Items — efa
code: efa(   data,   ...,   n_factors = NULL,   rotation = "varimax",   extraction = "pca",   weights = NULL,   use = "pairwise",   sort = TRUE,   blank = 0.4,   na.rm = TRUE )

title: Explore the Structure Behind Your Survey Items — efa
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... items analyze. Use bare column names separated commas, tidyselect helpers like starts_with("trust"). n_factors Number components extract. Default NULL uses Kaiser criterion (eigenvalue > 1). rotation Rotation method: "varimax" (default, orthogonal), "oblimin" (oblique, allows correlated factors), "promax" (oblique, power-based), "none". extraction Extraction method: "pca" (default, Principal Component Analysis) "ml" (Maximum Likelihood, enables goodness--fit testing, assumes multivariate normality). weights Optional survey weights population-representative results. use handle missing data correlation computation: "pairwise" (default, matches SPSS) "complete" (listwise). sort Logical. Sort loadings size within component? Default TRUE. blank Numeric. Suppress (hide) loadings absolute value threshold print output. Default 0.40 (matches SPSS BLANK(.40)). Set 0 show loadings. na.rm Logical. Remove missing values? Default TRUE.

title: Explore the Structure Behind Your Survey Items — efa
text: efa result object containing: loadings Component loading matrix (rotated rotation applied) unrotated_loadings Unrotated component matrix eigenvalues eigenvalues correlation matrix variance_explained Tibble Total, % Variance, Cumulative % rotation_variance Tibble rotation sums squared loadings communalities Extraction communalities variable kmo List overall KMO per-item MSA values bartlett List chi_sq, df, p_value rotation Rotation method used extraction Extraction method used n_factors Number components extracted correlation_matrix Correlation matrix used analysis initial_communalities Initial communalities (1.0 PCA, SMC ML) goodness_of_fit Goodness--fit test (ML ): chi_sq, df, p_value. NULL PCA. uniquenesses Unique variances per variable (ML , NULL PCA) pattern_matrix Pattern matrix (oblimin/promax , NULL otherwise) structure_matrix Structure matrix (oblimin/promax , NULL otherwise) factor_correlations Factor correlation matrix (oblimin/promax , NULL otherwise) variables Character vector variable names weights Weights variable name NULL n Sample size col_prefix Column name prefix: "PC" PCA, "Factor" ML sort Whether loadings sorted blank Suppression threshold Use summary() full SPSS-style output toggleable sections.

title: Explore the Structure Behind Your Survey Items — efa
text: KMO (Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin) measures sampling adequacy: KMO > 0.90: Marvelous KMO 0.80 - 0.90: Meritorious KMO 0.70 - 0.80: Middling KMO 0.60 - 0.70: Mediocre KMO 0.50 - 0.60: Miserable KMO < 0.50: Unacceptable - use factor analysis Bartlett's Test Sphericity tests whether correlations significantly different zero. significant result (p < .05) means correlation matrix suitable factor analysis. Eigenvalues indicate much variance component explains. Kaiser criterion retains components eigenvalue > 1. Factor Loadings show strongly item relates component: |loading| > 0.70: Strong association |loading| 0.40 - 0.70: Moderate association |loading| < 0.40: Weak (suppressed default) Communalities show much item's variance explained extracted components. Low communalities (< 0.40) suggest item fit well others.

title: Explore the Structure Behind Your Survey Items — efa
text: PCA (default): Extracts components explaining maximum total variance. Simple robust. assume normality. ML: Extracts factors explaining shared variance . Assumes multivariate normality. Provides goodness--fit test evaluate model fit. non-significant chi-square (p > .05) suggests adequate fit.

title: Explore the Structure Behind Your Survey Items — efa
text: Varimax (default): Assumes factors uncorrelated. Produces simpler, easier--interpret results. Oblimin: Allows factors correlated. realistic social science data. Produces Pattern Matrix (unique contributions) Structure Matrix (total correlations). Promax: Oblique rotation based power transformation Varimax results. Like Oblimin, produces Pattern Structure matrices. Common alternative Oblimin SPSS. None: rotation. Rarely useful interpretation.

title: Explore the Structure Behind Your Survey Items — efa
code: library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Basic EFA with Varimax rotation efa(survey_data,     political_orientation, environmental_concern, life_satisfaction,     trust_government, trust_media, trust_science) #> Exploratory Factor Analysis: 6 items, 3 components (PCA/Varimax) #>   KMO = 0.505 (Miserable), Variance explained: 61.0%  # With Oblimin rotation (requires GPArotation package) # \donttest{ efa(survey_data,     political_orientation, environmental_concern, life_satisfaction,     trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,     rotation = "oblimin") #> Exploratory Factor Analysis: 6 items, 3 components (PCA/Oblimin) #>   KMO = 0.505 (Miserable), Variance explained: 61.0% # }  # Maximum Likelihood extraction efa(survey_data,     political_orientation, environmental_concern, life_satisfaction,     trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,     extraction = "ml") #> Exploratory Factor Analysis: 6 items, 3 components (ML/Varimax) #>   KMO = 0.505 (Miserable), Variance explained: 61.0%  # Promax rotation (oblique) efa(survey_data,     political_orientation, environmental_concern, life_satisfaction,     trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,     rotation = "promax") #> Exploratory Factor Analysis: 6 items, 3 components (PCA/Promax) #>   KMO = 0.505 (Miserable), Variance explained: 61.0%  # Fix number of factors efa(survey_data,     political_orientation, environmental_concern, life_satisfaction,     trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,     n_factors = 2) #> Exploratory Factor Analysis: 6 items, 2 components (PCA/Varimax) #>   KMO = 0.505 (Miserable), Variance explained: 44.0%  # With survey weights efa(survey_data,     political_orientation, environmental_concern, life_satisfaction,     trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,     weights = sampling_weight) #> Exploratory Factor Analysis: 6 items, 3 components (PCA/Varimax) [Weighted] #>   KMO = 0.505 (Miserable), Variance explained: 61.0%  # Grouped by region survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   efa(political_orientation, environmental_concern, life_satisfaction,       trust_government, trust_media, trust_science) #> [region = 1] #> Exploratory Factor Analysis: 6 items, 3 components (PCA/Varimax) #>   KMO = 0.475 (Unacceptable), Variance explained: 62.1% #> [region = 2] #> Exploratory Factor Analysis: 6 items, 3 components (PCA/Varimax) #>   KMO = 0.505 (Miserable), Variance explained: 61.3%  # --- Three-layer output --- result <- efa(survey_data, political_orientation, environmental_concern,               life_satisfaction, trust_government, trust_media, trust_science) result              # compact overview #> Exploratory Factor Analysis: 6 items, 3 components (PCA/Varimax) #>   KMO = 0.505 (Miserable), Variance explained: 61.0% summary(result)     # full detailed output with all sections #>  #> Exploratory Factor Analysis (PCA, Varimax) Results #> -------------------------------------------------- #> - Variables: political_orientation, environmental_concern, life_satisfaction, trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - Extraction: Principal Component Analysis #> - Rotation: Varimax with Kaiser Normalization #> - N of Factors: 3 #>  #> KMO and Bartlett's Test #> ----------------------------------------  #>   Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin Measure:     0.505 #>   Bartlett's Chi-Square:          932.068 #>   df:                             15 #>   Sig.:                           0.000 #>  #> Communalities #> life_satisfaction       1      0.668 #>       trust_government       1      0.347 #>            trust_media       1      0.475 #>          trust_science       1      0.598 #> Extraction Method: what: Compare Groups Across Multiple Factors: Factorial ANOVA — factorial_anova
title: Compare Groups Across Multiple Factors: Factorial ANOVA — factorial_anova
text: factorial_anova() tests whether group means differ across two factors simultaneously, including interactions. performs factorial (two-way, three-way) ANOVA using Type III Sum Squares, matching SPSS UNIANOVA output. Think : Testing multiple grouping variables Detecting interaction effects (factor combinations matter?) extension one-way ANOVA multiple factors test tells : Whether factor main effect outcome Whether factors interact (effect one depends ) much variance factor explains (partial eta squared)

title: Compare Groups Across Multiple Factors: Factorial ANOVA — factorial_anova
code: factorial_anova(data, dv, between, weights = NULL, ss_type = 3)

title: Compare Groups Across Multiple Factors: Factorial ANOVA — factorial_anova
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) dv numeric dependent variable analyze (unquoted) Character vector unquoted variable names specifying -subjects factors (2-3 factors). must categorical variables (factor, character, labelled numeric). weights Optional survey weights population-representative results (unquoted variable name) ss_type Deprecated; 3 (Type III, SPSS default) implemented. Passing 2 issues warning computes Type III.

title: Compare Groups Across Multiple Factors: Factorial ANOVA — factorial_anova
text: object class "factorial_anova" containing: anova_table Tibble Source, SS, df, MS, F, p, Partial Eta Squared descriptives Tibble cell means, SDs, Ns factor combination levene_test Tibble Levene's test results (F, df1, df2, p) r_squared R-squared Adjusted R-squared model underlying model object S3 dispatch call_info List metadata (dv, factors, weighted, n_total, n_missing) Use summary() full SPSS-style output toggleable sections.

title: Compare Groups Across Multiple Factors: Factorial ANOVA — factorial_anova
text: Main Effects: factor independently affect outcome? Significant main effect = group means differ factor Example: Education affects income regardless gender Interaction Effects: effect one factor depend another? Significant interaction = pattern differs across factor combinations Example: gender gap income varies education level Partial Eta Squared (Effect Size): Less 0.01: Negligible 0.01 0.06: Small 0.06 0.14: Medium 0.14 greater: Large

title: Compare Groups Across Multiple Factors: Factorial ANOVA — factorial_anova
text: Type III SS tests effect adjusting effects. standard SPSS recommended unbalanced designs (unequal cell sizes). uses orthogonal contrasts (contr.sum) internally.

title: Compare Groups Across Multiple Factors: Factorial ANOVA — factorial_anova
text: Use factorial ANOVA : one numeric outcome variable 2-3 categorical grouping factors want test main effects interactions data approximately normally distributed within cells

title: Compare Groups Across Multiple Factors: Factorial ANOVA — factorial_anova
text: ANOVA significant: Check effects significant (main effects vs. interactions) Use tukey_test() post-hoc comparisons main effects Examine cell means interpret interaction patterns Consider effect sizes practical significance

title: Compare Groups Across Multiple Factors: Factorial ANOVA — factorial_anova
text: Cohen, J. (1988). Statistical Power Analysis Behavioral Sciences (2nd ed.). Lawrence Erlbaum Associates. IBM Corp. (2023). IBM SPSS Statistics 29 Algorithms. IBM Corporation. Maxwell, S. E., & Delaney, H. D. (2004). Designing Experiments Analyzing Data (2nd ed.). Lawrence Erlbaum Associates.

title: Compare Groups Across Multiple Factors: Factorial ANOVA — factorial_anova
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Two-way ANOVA: income by gender and education survey_data %>%   factorial_anova(dv = income, between = c(gender, education)) #> Factorial ANOVA (2-Way): income by gender, education #>   gender:           F(1, 2178) = 0.098, p = 0.755 , eta2p = 0.000 #>   education:        F(3, 2178) = 463.521, p < 0.001 ***, eta2p = 0.390 #>   gender:education: F(3, 2178) = 0.399, p = 0.754 , eta2p = 0.001, N = 2186  # Two-way ANOVA with weights survey_data %>%   factorial_anova(dv = life_satisfaction, between = c(gender, region),                   weights = sampling_weight) #> Factorial ANOVA (2-Way): life_satisfaction by gender, region [Weighted] #>   gender:        F(1, 2417) = 0.008, p = 0.930 , eta2p = 0.000 #>   region:        F(1, 2417) = 0.001, p = 0.979 , eta2p = 0.000 #>   gender:region: F(1, 2417) = 1.642, p = 0.200 , eta2p = 0.001, N = 2421  # Three-way ANOVA survey_data %>%   factorial_anova(dv = income, between = c(gender, region, education)) #> Factorial ANOVA (3-Way): income by gender, region, education #>   gender:                  F(1, 2170) = 2.976, p = 0.085 , eta2p = 0.001 #>   region:                  F(1, 2170) = 0.056, p = 0.812 , eta2p = 0.000 #>   education:               F(3, 2170) = 279.309, p < 0.001 ***, eta2p = 0.279 #>   gender:region:           F(1, 2170) = 5.769, p = 0.016 *, eta2p = 0.003 #>   gender:education:        F(3, 2170) = 0.597, p = 0.617 , eta2p = 0.001 #>   region:education:        F(3, 2170) = 0.990, p = 0.396 , eta2p = 0.001 #>   gender:region:education: F(3, 2170) = 3.889, p = 0.009 **, eta2p = 0.005, N = 2186  # Follow up with post-hoc tests result <- survey_data %>%   factorial_anova(dv = income, between = c(gender, education)) result %>% tukey_test() #> Tukey HSD Post-Hoc Test by gender x education #>   gender: 1 comparison, 0 significant (p < .05) #>   education: 6 comparisons, 6 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table. result %>% levene_test() #> Levene's Test: income by gender * education #>   F(7, 2178) = 44.988, p < 0.001 ***, variances unequal #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # --- Three-layer output --- result              # compact overview #> Factorial ANOVA (2-Way): income by gender, education #>   gender:           F(1, 2178) = 0.098, p = 0.755 , eta2p = 0.000 #>   education:        F(3, 2178) = 463.521, p < 0.001 ***, eta2p = 0.390 #>   gender:education: F(3, 2178) = 0.399, p = 0.754 , eta2p = 0.001, N = 2186 summary(result)     # full detailed output with all sections #> Factorial ANOVA (2-Way ANOVA) Results #> ------------------------------------- #>  #> - Dependent variable: income #> - Factors: gender x education #> - Type III Sum of Squares: Type 3 #> - N (complete cases): 2186 #> - Missing: 314 #>  #> Tests of Between-Subjects Effects #> ------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  Source             Type III SS  df   Mean Square  F         Sig.  #>  Corrected Model    1.754652e+09    7 2.506646e+08   199.909 <.001 #>  Intercept          3.221261e+10    1 3.221261e+10 25690.075 <.001 #>  gender             1.226376e+05    1 1.226376e+05     0.098 0.755 #>  education          1.743618e+09    3 5.812059e+08   463.521 <.001 #>  gender * education 1.499441e+06    3 4.998136e+05     0.399 0.754 #>  Error              2.730979e+09 2178 1.253893e+06                 #>  Total              3.529079e+10 2186                              #>  Corrected Total    4.485631e+09 2185                              #>  Partial Eta Sq     #>  0.391          *** #>  0.922          *** #>  0.000              #>  0.390          *** #>  0.001              #>                     #>                     #>                     #> ------------------------------------------------------------------  #> R Squared = 0.391 (Adjusted R Squared = 0.389) #>  #> Descriptive Statistics #> -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  gender education              Mean    Std. Deviation N   #>  Male   Basic Secondary        2803.43  774.959       350 #>  Male   Intermediate Secondary 3574.09  996.649       247 #>  Male   Academic Secondary     4246.45 1180.779       282 #>  Male   University             5318.56 1718.805       167 #>  Female Basic Secondary        2718.70  795.831       385 #>  Female Intermediate Secondary 3607.64  996.214       301 #>  Female Academic Secondary     4200.38 1178.118       266 #>  Female University             5353.72 1612.265       188 #> -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Levene's Test of Equality of Error Variances #>   F(7, 2178) = 44.988, p = <.001 #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 summary(result, marginal_means = FALSE)  # hide estimated marginal means #> Factorial ANOVA (2-Way ANOVA) Results #> ------------------------------------- #>  #> - what: Find Variables by Name or Label — find_var
title: Find Variables by Name or Label — find_var
text: Searches variables data matching pattern variable names, variable labels, . especially useful SPSS datasets variable names often cryptic codes (e.g., v104, q23a_1) actual meaning stored variable labels.

title: Find Variables by Name or Label — find_var
code: find_var(data, pattern, search = c("name_label", "name", "label"))

title: Find Variables by Name or Label — find_var
text: data data frame (typically imported SPSS read_spss). pattern search term regular expression match variable names /labels. Case-insensitive default. search search: "name_label" (default) searches variable names labels, "name" searches names, "label" searches labels.

title: Find Variables by Name or Label — find_var
text: data frame columns: col Column position data name Variable name label Variable label ("" none)

title: Find Variables by Name or Label — find_var
text: imported SPSS file need find variable contains "trust" "satisfaction" want quickly identify variables related topic know German/English label text variable code

title: Find Variables by Name or Label — find_var
text: pattern argument supports regular expressions. Matching case-insensitive. examples: "trust" — matches "trust", "Trust", "distrust", "trustworthy" "^trust" — matches names/labels starting "trust" "^q[0-9]+" — matches variable names like q1, q23, q104 "zufried" — finds German labels containing "Zufriedenheit"

title: Find Variables by Name or Label — find_var
code: library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Find all variables related to "trust" find_var(survey_data, "trust") #>   col             name                                    label #> 1  11 trust_government Trust in government (1=none, 5=complete) #> 2  12      trust_media      Trust in media (1=none, 5=complete) #> 3  13    trust_science    Trust in science (1=none, 5=complete)  # Search only in variable labels find_var(survey_data, "satisfaction", search = "label") #>   col              name                                           label #> 1  10 life_satisfaction Life satisfaction (1=dissatisfied, 5=satisfied)  # Use regex to find numbered items find_var(survey_data, "^q[0-9]+", search = "name") #> No variables found matching "^q[0-9]+". #> [1] col   name  label #> <0 rows> (or 0-length row.names)

what: Fisher's Exact Test for Small Samples — fisher_test
title: Fisher's Exact Test for Small Samples — fisher_test
text: fisher_test() performs Fisher's exact test independence contingency table. Use instead chi_square() sample small expected cell frequencies 5. Think : exact alternative chi-square test independence Best small samples chi-square approximation may inaccurate SPSS automatically reports Fisher's Exact Test 2x2 tables test tells : Whether two categorical variables related (exact p-value) contingency table observed frequencies Results valid even small samples

title: Fisher's Exact Test for Small Samples — fisher_test
code: fisher_test(data, row, col, weights = NULL, ...)

title: Fisher's Exact Test for Small Samples — fisher_test
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) row row variable (categorical) col column variable (categorical) weights Optional survey weights population-representative results ... Additional arguments (currently unused)

title: Fisher's Exact Test for Small Samples — fisher_test
text: Test results showing whether two categorical variables related, including: Exact p-value (two-sided) Contingency table observed frequencies Sample size (N)

title: Fisher's Exact Test for Small Samples — fisher_test
text: P-value: p < 0.05, variables likely related (independent) p < 0.001: strong evidence relationship p < 0.01: Strong evidence relationship p < 0.05: Moderate evidence relationship p >= 0.05: significant relationship found Unlike chi-square test uses large-sample approximation, Fisher's exact test computes exact probability observing given table (extreme one) null hypothesis independence. tables larger 2x2, Fisher-Freeman-Halton extension used.

title: Fisher's Exact Test for Small Samples — fisher_test
text: Use Fisher's exact test : expected cell frequency less 5 total sample size small (typically N < 30) 2x2 table (Fisher's exact standard ) want exact p-value rather approximation

title: Fisher's Exact Test for Small Samples — fisher_test
text: large samples expected frequencies >= 5: Use chi_square() instead paired binary data (/): Use mcnemar_test() instead testing single proportion: Use binomial_test() instead

title: Fisher's Exact Test for Small Samples — fisher_test
text: SPSS: CROSSTABS /STATISTICS=CHISQ (Fisher's exact test automatically reported 2x2 tables)

title: Fisher's Exact Test for Small Samples — fisher_test

title: Fisher's Exact Test for Small Samples — fisher_test
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Basic 2x2 Fisher test survey_data %>%   fisher_test(row = gender, col = region) #> Fisher's Exact Test: gender x region #>   p = 0.5435 , N = 2500 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Fisher test for larger table survey_data %>%   fisher_test(row = gender, col = interview_mode) #> Fisher's Exact Test: gender x interview_mode #>   p = 0.6782 , N = 2500 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # With weights survey_data %>%   fisher_test(row = gender, col = region, weights = sampling_weight) #> Fisher's Exact Test: gender x region [Weighted] #>   p = 0.4867 , N = 2516 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Grouped analysis survey_data %>%   group_by(education) %>%   fisher_test(row = gender, col = region) #> [education = 1] #> Fisher's Exact Test: gender x region #>   p = 0.9315 , N = 841 #> [education = 2] #> Fisher's Exact Test: gender x region #>   p = 0.4170 , N = 629 #> [education = 3] #> Fisher's Exact Test: gender x region #>   p = 0.9181 , N = 631 #> [education = 4] #> Fisher's Exact Test: gender x region #>   p = 0.9003 , N = 399 #> Use summary() for detailed output.

Without weights, get sample frequencies. weights, get population estimates. sort_frq order results: "none" (default): Keep original order "asc": Sort lowest highest frequency "desc": Sort highest lowest frequency show_na Include missing values table? (Default: TRUE) show_prc Show raw percentages including missing values? (Default: TRUE) show_valid Show percentages excluding missing values? (Default: TRUE) show_sum Show cumulative totals? (Default: TRUE) show_labels Show category labels available? (Default: "auto" - shows labels exist) show_unused Show defined value labels, even zero observations? (Default: FALSE). TRUE, values labels defined (e.g., statistical software files) cases data included frequency 0. useful labelled datasets unused categories still appear output. Automatically enables label display. sort.frq, show.na, show.prc, show.valid, show.sum, show.labels, show.unused Defunct dot-case argument names, removed mariposa 0.6.9. title: Count How Many People Chose Each Option — frequency
