title: Count How Many People Chose Each Option — frequency
text: frequency table shows: Freq: Number responses category %: Percentage including missing values (use "response rate") Valid %: Percentage excluding missing values (use "among answered") Cum %: Running total percentage (helps identify cutoff points)

title: Count How Many People Chose Each Option — frequency
text: Use frequency() : Categorical variables (gender, region, education level) Yes/questions Rating scales (satisfied/neutral/dissatisfied) question fixed set options

title: Count How Many People Chose Each Option — frequency
text: Without weights, describing sample. weights, estimating population values. Always use weights population inference.

title: Count How Many People Chose Each Option — frequency
text: data imported tagged NAs (e.g., via read_spss() tag_na = TRUE, read_stata(), read_sas(), read_xpt() tag_na parameter), frequency() automatically expands missing value section show missing type individually (original missing value code label), plus summary rows Total Valid Total Missing.

title: Count How Many People Chose Each Option — frequency
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Basic categorical analysis survey_data %>% frequency(gender) #> Frequency: gender #>   2 categories, N valid = 2500, missing = 0 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Multiple variables with weights survey_data %>% frequency(gender, region, weights = sampling_weight) #> Frequency: gender [Weighted] #>   2 categories, N valid = 2516, missing = 0 #> Frequency: region [Weighted] #>   2 categories, N valid = 2516, missing = 0 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Grouped analysis by region survey_data %>%    group_by(region) %>%    frequency(gender, weights = sampling_weight) #> Frequency: gender [Weighted] #>   [region = East] 2 categories, N valid = 509, missing = 0 #>   [region = West] 2 categories, N valid = 2007, missing = 0 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Education levels with sorting survey_data %>% frequency(education, sort_frq = "desc") #> Frequency: education #>   4 categories, N valid = 2500, missing = 0 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Employment status with custom display options survey_data %>% frequency(employment, weights = sampling_weight,                           show_na = TRUE, show_sum = TRUE) #> Frequency: employment [Weighted] #>   5 categories, N valid = 2516, missing = 0 #> Use summary() for detailed output.

what: Compare Three or More Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — friedman_test
title: Compare Three or More Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — friedman_test
text: friedman_test() compares three related measurements subjects data normally distributed. non-parametric alternative repeated-measures ANOVA. Think : Comparing ratings multiple items respondents Testing whether scores change across three time points robust repeated-measures comparison works data shape test tells : Whether least one measurement significantly different others measurements tend rated higher lower (via mean ranks) strength overall effect (Kendall's W)

title: Compare Three or More Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — friedman_test
code: friedman_test(data, ..., weights = NULL, conf.level = 0.95)

title: Compare Three or More Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — friedman_test
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) wide format, one row per subject measurement separate column ... measurement variables compare (least 3). can list individually use helpers like starts_with("trust_") weights Optional survey weights population-representative results conf.level Confidence level intervals (Default: 0.95 = 95 percent)

title: Compare Three or More Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — friedman_test
text: Test results showing whether measurements differ, including: Chi-Square statistic (chi_squared, Friedman test statistic) Degrees freedom (number measurements minus 1) P-value (measurements different?) Kendall's W (effect size: strong overall pattern?) Mean rank measurement (measurements higher/lower?)

title: Compare Three or More Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — friedman_test
text: P-value: p < 0.05, least one measurement significantly different p < 0.001: strong evidence differences p < 0.01: Strong evidence differences p < 0.05: Moderate evidence differences p > 0.05: significant differences found Kendall's W (Effect size: consistent pattern?): < 0.1: Negligible agreement/effect 0.1 - 0.3: Weak agreement 0.3 - 0.5: Moderate agreement 0.5 higher: Strong agreement Mean Ranks: Higher mean rank = measurement tends higher values Lower mean rank = measurement tends lower values Compare mean ranks see measurements stand

title: Compare Three or More Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — friedman_test
text: Use Friedman test : 3 related measurements subjects data normally distributed ordinal data (ratings, rankings) want robust alternative repeated-measures ANOVA comparing multiple ratings respondents

title: Compare Three or More Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — friedman_test
text: 2 related measurements: Use wilcoxon_test() instead normally distributed repeated measures: Use repeated-measures ANOVA

title: Compare Three or More Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — friedman_test
text: SPSS NPAR TESTS ignores WEIGHT , weighted results SPSS reference. weighted variant R-frequency-weight extension reduces exactly unweighted test weights equal 1 (enforced internal invariance suite); see vignette("spss-compatibility") validation status. independent groups: Use kruskal_wallis() instead

title: Compare Three or More Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — friedman_test
text: Friedman, M. (1937). use ranks avoid assumption normality implicit analysis variance. Journal American Statistical Association, 32(200), 675-701. Kendall, M. G., & Babington Smith, B. (1939). problem m rankings. Annals Mathematical Statistics, 10(3), 275-287.

title: Compare Three or More Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — friedman_test
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Compare three trust items (rated by same respondents) survey_data %>%   friedman_test(trust_government, trust_media, trust_science) #> Friedman Test: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #>   chi2(2) = 1009.035, p < 0.001 ***, W = 0.236, N = 2135 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Using tidyselect helpers survey_data %>%   friedman_test(starts_with("trust_")) #> Friedman Test: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #>   chi2(2) = 1009.035, p < 0.001 ***, W = 0.236, N = 2135 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Weighted analysis survey_data %>%   friedman_test(trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,                 weights = sampling_weight) #> Friedman Test: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science [Weighted] #>   chi2(2) = 1012.084, p < 0.001 ***, W = 0.235, N = 2150 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Grouped analysis (separate test per region) survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   friedman_test(trust_government, trust_media, trust_science) #> [region = 1] #> Friedman Test: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #>   chi2(2) = 217.100, p < 0.001 ***, W = 0.257, N = 422 #> [region = 2] #> Friedman Test: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #>   chi2(2) = 792.344, p < 0.001 ***, W = 0.231, N = 1713 #> Use summary() for detailed output.

title: Kendall's Tau Correlation Analysis — kendall_tau
text: Calculates Kendall's tau-b rank correlation coefficients variables support weighted correlations, grouped data, multiple variable pairs. Provides significance testing SPSS-compatible output formatting. function computes tau-b, adjusted ties particularly suitable ordinal data assumptions Pearson correlation met. weighted correlations, uses survey-weighted rank calculations.

title: Kendall's Tau Correlation Analysis — kendall_tau
code: kendall_tau(   data,   ...,   weights = NULL,   alternative = c("two.sided", "less", "greater"),   use = c("pairwise", "listwise"),   na.rm = NULL )

title: Kendall's Tau Correlation Analysis — kendall_tau
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... variables want correlate. List two single correlation correlation matrix. can use helpers like starts_with("trust"). weights Optional survey weights population-representative results. alternative Direction test: "two.sided" (default): Two-tailed test "less": One-tailed test (negative correlation) "greater": One-tailed test (positive correlation) use handle missing values: "pairwise" (default): Pairwise deletion - correlation uses available cases "listwise": Listwise deletion - complete cases across variables na.rm Deprecated. Use use instead.

title: Kendall's Tau Correlation Analysis — kendall_tau
text: Correlation results showing rank-based relationships variables, including tau-b coefficient, p-value, z-score, sample size pair. multiple variables, correlation, significance, sample size matrices also provided. Use summary() full SPSS-style output toggleable sections.

title: Kendall's Tau Correlation Analysis — kendall_tau
text: Kendall's tau measures often pairs observations order (concordant) versus different order (discordant). particularly useful ordinal data, data outliers, small sample sizes, non-linear monotonic relationships. tau value ranges -1 +1: Strong positive (0.5 1.0): High values one variable tend go high values Moderate positive (0.3 0.5): tendency values increase together Weak positive (0 0.3): Slight tendency values increase together correlation (near 0): relationship variables Negative values: one variable increases, tends decrease output also provides: p-value: Probability seeing correlation chance (smaller = stronger evidence) n: Number observations used significance stars: Quick visual indicator statistical significance

title: Kendall's Tau Correlation Analysis — kendall_tau
text: Choose Kendall's tau : data ordinal (ranked categories) small sample size (< 30 observations) data outliers might affect Pearson correlation

title: Kendall's Tau Correlation Analysis — kendall_tau
text: weighted tau-b exact frequency-weighted computation (reproduces unweighted tau weights equal 1, enforced internal invariance suite), z statistic p-value use -ties normal approximation. SPSS NONPAR CORR ignores WEIGHT , weighted results SPSS reference; see vignette("spss-compatibility") validation status. want conservative measure Spearman's rho

title: Kendall's Tau Correlation Analysis — kendall_tau
text: Kendall, M. G. (1938). new measure rank correlation. Biometrika, 30(1/2), 81–93. Kendall, M. G. (1945). treatment ties ranking problems. Biometrika, 33(3), 239–251. Agresti, . (2010). Analysis Ordinal Categorical Data (2nd ed.). John Wiley & Sons.

title: Kendall's Tau Correlation Analysis — kendall_tau
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Basic correlation between two variables survey_data %>%   kendall_tau(life_satisfaction, political_orientation) #> Kendall's Tau: life_satisfaction x political_orientation #>   tau = -0.004, p = 0.832 , N = 2228  # Correlation matrix for multiple variables survey_data %>%   kendall_tau(life_satisfaction, political_orientation, trust_media) #> Kendall's Tau: 3 variables #>   life_satisfaction x political_orientation: tau = -0.004, p = 0.832   #>   life_satisfaction x trust_media: tau = 0.023, p = 0.176   #>   political_orientation x trust_media: tau = 0.003, p = 0.883   #>   0/3 pairs significant (p < .05), N = 2228  # Weighted correlations survey_data %>%   kendall_tau(age, income, weights = sampling_weight) #> Kendall's Tau: age x income [Weighted] #>   tau = 0.003, p = 0.840 , N = 2201  # Listwise deletion for missing data survey_data %>%   kendall_tau(age, income, use = "listwise") #> Kendall's Tau: age x income #>   tau = 0.002, p = 0.867 , N = 2186  # One-tailed test survey_data %>%   kendall_tau(age, income, alternative = "greater") #> Kendall's Tau: age x income #>   tau = 0.002, p = 0.433 , N = 2186  # \donttest{ # Grouped correlations survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   kendall_tau(age, income, life_satisfaction) #> [region = 1] #> Kendall's Tau: 3 variables #>   age x income:                  tau = 0.040, p = 0.227   #>   age x life_satisfaction:       tau = -0.030, p = 0.380   #>   income x life_satisfaction:    tau = 0.338, p < 0.001 ***  #>   1/3 pairs significant (p < .05), N = 429 #> [region = 2] #> Kendall's Tau: 3 variables #>   age x income:                  tau = -0.006, p = 0.726   #>   age x life_satisfaction:       tau = -0.015, p = 0.377   #>   income x life_satisfaction:    tau = 0.357, p < 0.001 ***  #>   1/3 pairs significant (p < .05), N = 1757  # Using tidyselect helpers for ordinal variables survey_data %>%   kendall_tau(starts_with("trust"), weights = sampling_weight) #> Kendall's Tau: 3 variables [Weighted] #>   trust_government x trust_media: tau = 0.007, p = 0.616   #>   trust_government x trust_science: tau = 0.022, p = 0.110   #>   trust_media x trust_science:   tau = 0.014, p = 0.311   #>   0/3 pairs significant (p < .05), N = 2242  # --- Three-layer output --- result <- survey_data %>%   kendall_tau(life_satisfaction, political_orientation, trust_media,               weights = sampling_weight) result              # compact one-line overview #> Kendall's Tau: 3 variables [Weighted] #>   life_satisfaction x political_orientation: tau = -0.005, p = 0.747   #>   life_satisfaction x trust_media: tau = 0.023, p = 0.105   #>   political_orientation x trust_media: tau = 0.004, p = 0.797   #>   0/3 pairs significant (p < .05), N = 2241 summary(result)     # full correlation, p-value, and N matrices #>  #> Weighted Kendall's Tau-b Correlation  #> ------------------------------------- #>  #> - Weights variable: sampling_weight #> - Missing data handling: pairwise deletion #> - Alternative hypothesis: two.sided #>  #>  #> Kendall's Tau-b Matrix: #> -----------------------  #>                       life_satisfaction political_orientation trust_media #> life_satisfaction                 1.000                -0.005       0.023 #> political_orientation            -0.005                 1.000       0.004 #> trust_media                       0.023                 0.004       1.000 #> -----------------------  #>  #> Significance Matrix (p-values, 2-tailed): #> -----------------------------------------  #>                       life_satisfaction political_orientation trust_media #> life_satisfaction                0.0000                0.7473      0.1046 #> political_orientation            0.7473                0.0000      0.7972 #> trust_media                      0.1046                0.7972      0.0000 #> -----------------------------------------  #>  #> Sample Size Matrix: #> -------------------  #>                       life_satisfaction political_orientation trust_media #> life_satisfaction                  2437                  2241        2305 #> political_orientation              2241                  2312        2190 #> trust_media                        2305                  2190        2382 #> -------------------  #>  #> Pairwise Results: #> ----------------  #>                                       Pair  tau_b      z      p    n sig #>  life_satisfaction × political_orientation -0.005 -0.322 0.7473 2241     #>            life_satisfaction × trust_media  0.023  1.623 0.1046 2305     #>        political_orientation × trust_media  0.004  0.257 0.7972 2190     #> ----------------  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 summary(result, pvalue_matrix = FALSE)  # hide p-values #>  #> Weighted Kendall's Tau-b Correlation  #> ------------------------------------- #>  #> - Weights variable: sampling_weight #> - Missing data handling: pairwise deletion #> - Alternative hypothesis: two.sided #>  #>  #> Kendall's Tau-b Matrix: #> -----------------------  #>                       life_satisfaction political_orientation trust_media #> life_satisfaction                 1.000                -0.005       0.023 #> political_orientation            -0.005                 1.000       0.004 #> trust_media                       0.023                 0.004       1.000 #> -----------------------  #>  #> Sample Size Matrix: #> -------------------  #>                       life_satisfaction political_orientation trust_media #> life_satisfaction                  2437                  2241        2305 #> political_orientation              2241                  2312        2190 #> trust_media                        2305                  2190        2382 #> -------------------  #>  #> Pairwise Results: #> ----------------  #>                                       Pair  tau_b      z      p    n sig #>  life_satisfaction × political_orientation -0.005 -0.322 0.7473 2241     #>            life_satisfaction × trust_media  0.023  1.623 0.1046 2305     #>        political_orientation × trust_media  0.004  0.257 0.7972 2190     #> ----------------  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 # }

what: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
title: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
text: kruskal_wallis() compares three groups data normally distributed ordinal data (like ratings rankings). non-parametric alternative one-way ANOVA. Think : extension Mann-Whitney test two groups robust way compare groups works data shape Perfect Likert scales, rankings, skewed distributions test tells : Whether least one group different others strong overall group effect (effect size) groups tend higher lower values (via mean ranks)

title: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
code: kruskal_wallis(data, ..., group, weights = NULL, conf.level = 0.95)

title: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... variables want compare groups. can list multiple variables use helpers like starts_with("satisfaction") group categorical variable defines groups (e.g., education, employment). Must least 2 groups (3+ meaningful use). weights Optional survey weights population-representative results conf.level Confidence level intervals (Default: 0.95 = 95%)

title: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
text: Test results showing whether groups differ, including: H statistic (Kruskal-Wallis chi-square test statistic) Degrees freedom (number groups minus 1) P-value (groups different?) Effect size epsilon-squared (big group effect?) Mean rank group (groups higher/lower?)

title: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
text: P-value: p < 0.05, least one group significantly different p < 0.001: strong evidence group differences p < 0.01: Strong evidence group differences p < 0.05: Moderate evidence group differences p > 0.05: significant group differences found Effect Size Epsilon-squared (much groups matter?): < 0.01: Negligible effect 0.01-0.06: Small effect 0.06-0.14: Medium effect 0.14 higher: Large effect Mean Ranks: Higher mean rank = group tends higher values Lower mean rank = group tends lower values Compare mean ranks see pattern group differences

title: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
text: Use Kruskal-Wallis test : data normally distributed (skewed, outliers) ordinal data (rankings, Likert scales) Sample sizes small unequal across groups want robust alternative one-way ANOVA comparing satisfaction ratings, income, skewed variables

title: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
text: Kruskal-Wallis test significant: Look mean ranks see pattern Use pairwise Mann-Whitney tests Bonferroni correction find specific groups differ Consider effect sizes judge practical importance

title: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
text: 2 groups: Use mann_whitney() instead normally distributed data: Use oneway_anova() instead

title: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
text: SPSS NPAR TESTS ignores WEIGHT , weighted results SPSS reference. weighted variant R-frequency-weight extension reduces exactly unweighted test weights equal 1 (enforced internal invariance suite); see vignette("spss-compatibility") validation status. repeated measures (subjects): Use friedman_test() instead

title: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
text: Kruskal, W. H., & Wallis, W. . (1952). Use ranks one-criterion variance analysis. Journal American Statistical Association, 47(260), 583-621. Tomczak, M., & Tomczak, E. (2014). need report effect size estimates revisited. overview recommended measures effect size. Trends Sport Sciences, 1(21), 19-25.

title: Compare Multiple Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — kruskal_wallis
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Basic Kruskal-Wallis test (comparing across education levels) survey_data %>%   kruskal_wallis(life_satisfaction, group = education) #> Kruskal-Wallis Test: life_satisfaction by education #>   H(3) = 171.178, p < 0.001 ***, eps2 = 0.071, N = 2421 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Multiple variables survey_data %>%   kruskal_wallis(life_satisfaction, income, trust_government,                  group = education) #> Kruskal-Wallis Test: life_satisfaction by education #>   H(3) = 171.178, p < 0.001 ***, eps2 = 0.071, N = 2421 #> Kruskal-Wallis Test: income by education #>   H(3) = 814.174, p < 0.001 ***, eps2 = 0.373, N = 2186 #> Kruskal-Wallis Test: trust_government by education #>   H(3) = 1.235, p = 0.745 , eps2 = 0.001, N = 2354 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Using tidyselect helpers survey_data %>%   kruskal_wallis(starts_with("trust_"), group = education) #> Kruskal-Wallis Test: trust_government by education #>   H(3) = 1.235, p = 0.745 , eps2 = 0.001, N = 2354 #> Kruskal-Wallis Test: trust_media by education #>   H(3) = 2.709, p = 0.439 , eps2 = 0.001, N = 2367 #> Kruskal-Wallis Test: trust_science by education #>   H(3) = 3.047, p = 0.384 , eps2 = 0.001, N = 2398 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Weighted analysis survey_data %>%   kruskal_wallis(life_satisfaction, group = education,                  weights = sampling_weight) #> Kruskal-Wallis Test: life_satisfaction by education [Weighted] #>   H(3) = 167.075, p < 0.001 ***, eps2 = 0.069, N = 2437 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Grouped analysis (separate test for each region) survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   kruskal_wallis(life_satisfaction, group = education) #> [region = 1] #> Kruskal-Wallis Test: life_satisfaction by education #>   H(3) = 17.105, p < 0.001 ***, eps2 = 0.037, N = 465 #> [region = 2] #> Kruskal-Wallis Test: life_satisfaction by education #>   H(3) = 158.807, p < 0.001 ***, eps2 = 0.081, N = 1956 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Compare across employment status (5 groups) survey_data %>%   kruskal_wallis(income, group = employment) #> Kruskal-Wallis Test: income by employment #>   H(4) = 28.026, p < 0.001 ***, eps2 = 0.013, N = 2186 #> Use summary() for detailed output.

what: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
text: levene_test() checks different groups similar amounts variation. important assumption many statistical tests - groups spread similar ways. test tells : Whether variance consistent across groups can trust standard ANOVA t-test results use alternative tests assume equal variance

title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
code: levene_test(x, ...)  # Default S3 method levene_test(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'data.frame' levene_test(x, ..., group, weights = NULL, center = c("mean", "median"))  # S3 method for class 'oneway_anova' levene_test(x, center = c("mean", "median"), ...)  # S3 method for class 't_test' levene_test(x, center = c("mean", "median"), ...)  # S3 method for class 'mann_whitney' levene_test(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'grouped_df' levene_test(x, variable, group = NULL, weights = NULL, center = "mean", ...)

title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
text: x Either data test results t_test() oneway_anova() ... Variables test (using data frame) group grouping variable comparison weights Optional survey weights population-representative results center measure center: "mean" (default) "median" (robust) variable Variable test (using grouped data frame) data survey data (x test result)

title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
text: Test results showing: Whether groups equal variances (p-value) F-statistic measuring variance differences variables meet assumption

title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
text: P-value interpretation: p > 0.05: Good! Groups similar variance (assumption met) p ≤ 0.05: Problem - groups vary differently (assumption violated) Think like checking groups equally "spread ": Similar spread = can use standard tests Different spread = need special methods

title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
text: Check variance equality : running t-tests ANOVA Comparing groups different sizes statistical test assumes equal variances see different standard deviations

title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
text: Levene's test significant (p ≤ 0.05): t-tests: Use Welch's t-test (var.equal = FALSE) ANOVA: Use Welch's ANOVA Consider transforming data Use non-parametric alternatives Report equal variance assumption violated

title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
text: can use function two ways: Standalone: Check variables equal variance tests: Pipe t_test() oneway_anova() verify assumptions

title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
text: Always check assumption group comparisons Visual inspection (boxplots) can supplement test Large samples make test sensitive Use median-based test skewed data (center = "median") panic violated - alternatives exist!

title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
text: Levene, H. (1960). Robust tests equality variances. . Olkin (Ed.), Contributions Probability Statistics (pp. 278–292). Stanford University Press. Brown, M. B., & Forsythe, . B. (1974). Robust tests equality variances. Journal American Statistical Association, 69(346), 364–367. IBM Corp. (2023). IBM SPSS Statistics 29 Algorithms. IBM Corporation.

title: Test If Groups Vary Similarly — levene_test
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Standalone Levene test (test homogeneity of variances) survey_data %>% levene_test(life_satisfaction, group = region) #> Levene's Test: life_satisfaction by region #>   F(1, 2419) = 3.164, p = 0.075 , variances equal #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Multiple variables survey_data %>% levene_test(life_satisfaction, trust_government, group = region) #> Levene's Test: life_satisfaction by region #>   F(1, 2419) = 3.164, p = 0.075 , variances equal #> Levene's Test: trust_government by region #>   F(1, 2352) = 0.145, p = 0.703 , variances equal #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Weighted analysis survey_data %>% levene_test(income, group = education, weights = sampling_weight) #> Levene's Test: income by education [Weighted] #>   F(3, 2196.9) = 102.048, p < 0.001 ***, variances unequal #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Piped after ANOVA (common workflow) result <- survey_data %>%   oneway_anova(life_satisfaction, group = education) result %>% levene_test() #> Levene's Test: life_satisfaction by education #>   F(3, 2417) = 31.634, p < 0.001 ***, variances unequal #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Piped after t-test survey_data %>%   t_test(age, group = gender) %>%   levene_test() #> Levene's Test: age by gender #>   F(1, 2498) = 0.534, p = 0.465 , variances equal #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Using mean instead of median as center survey_data %>% levene_test(income, group = region, center = "mean") #> Levene's Test: income by region #>   F(1, 2184) = 1.631, p = 0.202 , variances equal #> Use summary() for detailed output.

title: Run a Linear Regression — linear_regression
text: linear_regression() performs bivariate multiple linear regression SPSS-compatible output. Wraps stats::lm() adds standardized coefficients (Beta), formatted ANOVA table, model summary matching SPSS REGRESSION output. Supports two interface styles: Formula interface: linear_regression(data, life_satisfaction ~ age + education) SPSS-style: linear_regression(data, dependent = life_satisfaction, predictors = c(age, education))

title: Run a Linear Regression — linear_regression
code: linear_regression(   data,   formula = NULL,   dependent = NULL,   predictors = NULL,   weights = NULL,   use = c("listwise", "pairwise"),   standardized = TRUE,   conf.level = 0.95,   factors = c("dummy", "numeric") )

title: Run a Linear Regression — linear_regression
text: data survey data (data frame tibble). grouped (via dplyr::group_by()), separate regressions run group. formula formula specifying model (e.g., y ~ x1 + x2). provided, dependent predictors ignored. dependent dependent variable (unquoted). Used predictors formula given. predictors Predictor variable(s) (unquoted, supports tidyselect). Used dependent formula given. weights Optional survey weights (unquoted variable name). specified, weighted least squares (WLS) used, matching SPSS WEIGHT . use handle missing data: "listwise" (default) drops case missing value variable (matching SPSS /MISSING LISTWISE). "pairwise" computes regression pairwise covariance/correlation matrix, retaining cases (matching SPSS /MISSING PAIRWISE). standardized Logical. TRUE (default), standardized coefficients (Beta) calculated included output. conf.level Confidence level coefficient intervals (default 0.95). factors factor predictors entered model: "dummy" (default, matches base R lm()) expands factor L levels L - 1 dummy contrasts; "numeric" silently coerces factor levels integer codes, matching SPSS REGRESSION default behavior (ordinal--scale). "numeric" mode emits one-line cli::cli_inform() listing coerced variables. "numeric" mode required reproduce SPSS results factor predictors carry ordered meaning (e.g., 4-level education).

title: Run a Linear Regression — linear_regression
text: ungrouped + listwise data, object class c("linear_regression", "lm") — fitted lm , mariposa-specific slots attached: coef_table SPSS-style tibble B, Std.Error, Beta, t, p, CI_lower, CI_upper. weighted models, SE / t / p adjusted SPSS's frequency-weight df (see Technical Details). anova_table SPSS-style overall-model ANOVA tibble (Source × Sum_of_Squares / df / Mean_Square / F_statistic / Sig). model_summary List R, R_squared, adj_R_squared, std_error. descriptives Tibble Mean, Std.Deviation, N variables. n Sample size (listwise complete cases; weighted N weighted). formula, dependent, predictor_names, weighted, weight_name, use, is_grouped, standardized, conf.level Call metadata. object inherits "lm", standard generics (predict(), anova(), vcov(), confint(), residuals(), fitted(), coef(), model.matrix(), broom::tidy(), broom::glance(), broom::augment()) dispatch natively without unwrapping. summary() returns SPSS-style mariposa summary; raw lm summary use stats::summary.lm() object. use = "pairwise" (single fitted lm available) grouped data, returns list class "linear_regression". Pairwise results expose SPSS-style tables lm generics; grouped results hold one fitted lm-inheriting model per group $groups.

title: Run a Linear Regression — linear_regression
text: output includes four sections matching SPSS REGRESSION output: Model Summary: R, R-squared, Adjusted R-squared, Standard Error Estimate. R-squared tells much variance dependent variable explained predictors. ANOVA: Tests whether overall model significant. significant F-test means least one predictor matters. Coefficients: B (unstandardized), Beta (standardized), t-value, p-value, confidence intervals predictor. Descriptives: Mean, SD, N variables model. Interpreting coefficients: B (unstandardized): 1-unit increase predictor, dependent variable changes B units Beta (standardized): Allows comparison across predictors different scales. Larger absolute Beta = stronger effect p-value: Values 0.05 indicate statistically significant predictors

title: Run a Linear Regression — linear_regression
text: Use linear_regression() : dependent variable continuous (e.g., income, satisfaction score) want predict outcome one predictors need standardized coefficients compare predictor importance binary outcomes (yes/, 0/1), use logistic_regression instead.

title: Run a Linear Regression — linear_regression
text: Missing Data: default, listwise deletion used (matching SPSS REGRESSION /MISSING LISTWISE). Set use = "pairwise" match SPSS /MISSING PAIRWISE, computes regression pairwise covariance matrix. Pairwise deletion retains cases produces results closer SPSS output data varying patterns missingness. Weights: weights specified, treated frequency weights (matching SPSS WEIGHT behavior). model fitted using weighted least squares via lm(weights = ...). Standardized Coefficients: Beta = B * (SD_x / SD_y). matches SPSS standardized coefficient output. available intercept. dummy-coded factor terms (factors = "dummy"), SD contrast column design matrix used. Factor Predictors: default (factors = "dummy"), factor predictors expanded L - 1 dummy contrasts via R's stats::model.matrix(), matching base R lm(). Pass factors = "numeric" silently coerce factor levels integer codes (SPSS REGRESSION default). "numeric" mode required reproduce SPSS results ordinal predictors like education Likert scales SPSS treats continuous. Grouped Analysis: data grouped via dplyr::group_by(), separate regression run group (matching SPSS SPLIT FILE ).

title: Run a Linear Regression — linear_regression
code: library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Bivariate regression linear_regression(survey_data, life_satisfaction ~ age) #> Linear Regression: life_satisfaction ~ age #>   R2 = 0.001, adj.R2 = 0.000, F(1, 2419) = 2.00, p = 0.158 , N = 2421  # Multiple regression linear_regression(survey_data, income ~ age + education + life_satisfaction) #> Linear Regression: income ~ age + education + life_satisfaction #>   R2 = 0.477, adj.R2 = 0.476, F(5, 2109) = 385.29, p < 0.001 ***, N = 2115  # SPSS-style interface linear_regression(survey_data,                   dependent = life_satisfaction,                   predictors = c(trust_government, trust_media, trust_science)) #> Linear Regression: life_satisfaction ~ trust_government + trust_media + trust_science #>   R2 = 0.002, adj.R2 = 0.000, F(3, 2062) = 1.16, p = 0.322 , N = 2066  # Weighted regression linear_regression(survey_data, life_satisfaction ~ age, weights = sampling_weight) #> Linear Regression: life_satisfaction ~ age [Weighted] #>   R2 = 0.001, adj.R2 = 0.000, F(1, 2435) = 2.08, p = 0.150 , N = 2437  # Grouped by region survey_data |>   dplyr::group_by(region) |>   linear_regression(life_satisfaction ~ age) #> Linear Regression: life_satisfaction ~ age [Grouped: region] #>   region = East: R2 = 0.002, adj.R2 = -0.000, F(1, 463) = 0.88, p = 0.350 , N = 465 #>   region = West: R2 = 0.001, adj.R2 = 0.000, F(1, 1954) = 1.20, p = 0.274 , N = 1956  # Factor predictors: dummy-coding (default, matches base R lm()) linear_regression(survey_data, income ~ age + education) #> Linear Regression: income ~ age + education #>   R2 = 0.391, adj.R2 = 0.390, F(4, 2181) = 349.72, p < 0.001 ***, N = 2186  # Factor predictors: SPSS-style ordinal-as-scale linear_regression(survey_data, income ~ age + education,                   factors = "numeric") #> ℹ Factor predictor(s) coerced to numeric (SPSS-style ordinal scaling): #> • `education` #> Linear Regression: income ~ age + education #>   R2 = 0.386, adj.R2 = 0.386, F(2, 2183) = 686.59, p < 0.001 ***, N = 2186  # --- Three-layer output --- result <- linear_regression(survey_data, life_satisfaction ~ age + income) result                                  # compact one-line overview #> Linear Regression: life_satisfaction ~ age + income #>   R2 = 0.201, adj.R2 = 0.200, F(2, 2112) = 265.60, p < 0.001 ***, N = 2115 summary(result)                         # full detailed SPSS-style output #>  #> Linear Regression Results #> ------------------------- #> - Formula: life_satisfaction ~ age + income #> - Method: ENTER (all predictors) #> - N: 2115 #>  #>   Descriptive Statistics #>   ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #>   Variable                                    Mean     Std.Dev.      N #>   ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #>   life_satisfaction                          3.638        1.148   2115 #>   age                                       50.827       16.995   2115 #>   income                                  3757.683     1430.923   2115 #>   ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #>  #>   Model Summary #>   ------------------------------------------------------------ #>   R                              0.448 #>   R Square                       0.201 #>   Adjusted R Square              0.200 #>   Std. Error of Estimate         1.026 #>   ------------------------------------------------------------ #>  #>   ANOVA #>   ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ #>   Source           Sum of Squares    df      Mean Square          F     Sig. #>   ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ #>   Regression              559.609     2          279.804    265.598    0.000 *** #>   Residual               2224.965  2112            1.053                      #>   Total                  2784.574  2114                                       #>   ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ #>  #>   Coefficients #>   ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- #>   Term                               B  Std.Error     Beta          t     Sig.  #>   ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- #>   (Intercept)                    2.321      0.092              25.237    0.000 *** #>   age                           -0.001      0.001   -0.010     -0.508    0.611  #>   income                         0.000      0.000    0.448     23.037    0.000 *** #>   ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- #>  #>   Collinearity Statistics #>   -------------------------------------------------- #>   Term                       Tolerance        VIF #>   -------------------------------------------------- #>   age                            1.000      1.000 #>   income                         1.000      1.000 #>   -------------------------------------------------- #>   VIF > 10 (Tolerance < 0.1) indicates problematic collinearity. #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 summary(result, descriptives = FALSE)   # hide descriptives section #>  #> Linear Regression Results #> ------------------------- #> - Formula: life_satisfaction ~ age + income #> - Method: ENTER (all predictors) #> - N: 2115 #>  #>   Model Summary #>   ------------------------------------------------------------ #>   R                              0.448 #>   R Square                       0.201 #>   Adjusted R Square              0.200 #>   Std. Error of Estimate         1.026 #>   ------------------------------------------------------------ #>  #>   ANOVA #>   ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ #>   Source           Sum of Squares    df      Mean Square          F     Sig. #>   ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ #>   Regression              559.609     2          279.804    265.598    0.000 *** #>   Residual               2224.965  2112            1.053                      #>   Total                  2784.574  2114                                       #>   ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ #>  #>   Coefficients #>   ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- #>   Term                               B  Std.Error     Beta          t     Sig.  #>   ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- #>   (Intercept)                    2.321      0.092              25.237    0.000 *** #>   age                           -0.001      0.001   -0.010     -0.508    0.611  #>   income                         0.000      0.000    0.448     23.037    0.000 *** #>   ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- #>  #>   Collinearity Statistics #>   -------------------------------------------------- #>   Term                       Tolerance        VIF #>   -------------------------------------------------- #>   age                            1.000      1.000 #>   income                         1.000      1.000 #>   -------------------------------------------------- #>   VIF > 10 (Tolerance < 0.1) indicates problematic collinearity. #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05

title: Run a Logistic Regression — logistic_regression
text: logistic_regression() performs binary logistic regression SPSS-compatible output. Wraps stats::glm(family = binomial) adds odds ratios, pseudo R-squared measures, classification table, model tests matching SPSS LOGISTIC REGRESSION output. Supports two interface styles: Formula interface: logistic_regression(data, high_satisfaction ~ age + income) SPSS-style: logistic_regression(data, dependent = high_satisfaction, predictors = c(age, income))

title: Run a Logistic Regression — logistic_regression
code: logistic_regression(   data,   formula = NULL,   dependent = NULL,   predictors = NULL,   weights = NULL,   conf.level = 0.95,   factors = c("dummy", "numeric") )

title: Run a Logistic Regression — logistic_regression
text: data survey data (data frame tibble). grouped (via dplyr::group_by()), separate regressions run group. formula formula specifying model (e.g., y ~ x1 + x2). provided, dependent predictors ignored. dependent dependent variable (unquoted). Used predictors formula given. Must binary (0/1 two-level factor). predictors Predictor variable(s) (unquoted, supports tidyselect). Used dependent formula given. weights Optional survey weights (unquoted variable name). specified, weighted maximum likelihood estimation used, matching SPSS WEIGHT behavior. conf.level Confidence level odds ratio intervals (default 0.95). factors factor predictors entered model: "dummy" (default, matches base R glm()) expands factor L levels L - 1 dummy contrasts; "numeric" silently coerces factor levels integer codes, matching SPSS LOGISTIC REGRESSION default behavior /CATEGORICAL subcommand given. "numeric" mode emits one-line cli::cli_inform() listing coerced variables.

title: Run a Logistic Regression — logistic_regression
text: ungrouped data, object class c("logistic_regression", "glm", "lm") — fitted glm , mariposa-specific slots attached: coef_table Tibble B, S.E., Wald, df, Sig., Exp(B), CI_lower, CI_upper model_summary List minus2LL, cox_snell_r2, nagelkerke_r2, mcfadden_r2 omnibus_test List chi_sq, df, p overall model test classification List table, overall_pct, pct_correct_0, pct_correct_1 hosmer_lemeshow List chi_sq, df, p (goodness--fit test) n Sample size (listwise complete cases; weighted N weighted) formula, dependent, predictor_names, weighted, weight_name, is_grouped, conf.level Call metadata. object inherits "glm", standard generics (predict(), anova(), vcov(), confint(), residuals(), fitted(), coef(), broom::tidy(), broom::glance(), broom::augment()) dispatch natively without unwrapping. summary() returns SPSS-style mariposa summary; raw glm summary use stats::summary.glm() object. grouped data, returns list class "logistic_regression" $groups holding one fitted glm-inheriting result per group.

title: Run a Logistic Regression — logistic_regression
text: output includes five sections matching SPSS LOGISTIC REGRESSION output: Omnibus Test: Tests whether model whole significant. significant chi-square means model predicts better chance. Model Summary: -2 Log Likelihood pseudo R-squared values. Lower -2LL = better fit. Higher R-squared = variance explained. Hosmer-Lemeshow Test: Goodness--fit test. non-significant result (p > 0.05) means model fits data well. Classification Table: well model classifies cases. Shows percentage correctly predicted group overall. Coefficients: B, Wald test, odds ratios (Exp(B)), CIs. Interpreting odds ratios (Exp(B)): Exp(B) > 1: Predictor increases odds outcome Exp(B) < 1: Predictor decreases odds outcome Exp(B) = 1: Predictor effect odds

title: Run a Logistic Regression — logistic_regression
text: Use logistic_regression() : dependent variable binary (yes/, 0/1, pass/fail) want predict group membership one predictors need odds ratios interpret predictor effects continuous outcomes, use linear_regression instead.

title: Run a Logistic Regression — logistic_regression
text: Dependent Variable: Must binary. Factors exactly 2 levels automatically converted 0/1 (first level = 0, second level = 1). Numeric variables must contain 0 1 values. Missing Data: Listwise deletion used (matching SPSS LOGISTIC REGRESSION default behavior). Weights: weights specified, treated frequency weights (matching SPSS WEIGHT behavior). Pseudo R-squared: Three measures reported: Cox & Snell R-squared (bounded 1) Nagelkerke R-squared (adjusted reach 1) McFadden R-squared (1 - LL_model/LL_null) Factor Predictors: default (factors = "dummy"), factor predictors expanded L - 1 dummy contrasts via R's stats::model.matrix(), matching base R glm(). Pass factors = "numeric" silently coerce factor levels integer codes (SPSS LOGISTIC REGRESSION default without explicit /CATEGORICAL subcommand). Grouped Analysis: data grouped via dplyr::group_by(), separate regression run group (matching SPSS SPLIT FILE ).

title: Run a Logistic Regression — logistic_regression
code: library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Create binary DV survey_data$high_satisfaction <- ifelse(survey_data$life_satisfaction >= 4, 1, 0)  # Bivariate logistic regression logistic_regression(survey_data, high_satisfaction ~ age) #> Logistic Regression: high_satisfaction ~ age #>   Nagelkerke R2 = 0.000, chi2(1) = 0.28, p = 0.595 , Accuracy = 57.7%, N = 2421  # Multiple logistic regression logistic_regression(survey_data, high_satisfaction ~ age + income + education) #> Logistic Regression: high_satisfaction ~ age + income + education #>   Nagelkerke R2 = 0.213, chi2(5) = 364.62, p < 0.001 ***, Accuracy = 68.3%, N = 2115  # SPSS-style interface logistic_regression(survey_data,                     dependent = high_satisfaction,                     predictors = c(age, income)) #> Logistic Regression: high_satisfaction ~ age + income #>   Nagelkerke R2 = 0.209, chi2(2) = 357.43, p < 0.001 ***, Accuracy = 68.4%, N = 2115  # Weighted logistic regression logistic_regression(survey_data, high_satisfaction ~ age,                     weights = sampling_weight) #> Logistic Regression: high_satisfaction ~ age [Weighted] #>   Nagelkerke R2 = 0.000, chi2(1) = 0.28, p = 0.595 , Accuracy = 57.6%, N = 2437  # Grouped by region survey_data |>   dplyr::group_by(region) |>   logistic_regression(high_satisfaction ~ age) #> Logistic Regression: high_satisfaction ~ age [Grouped: region] #>   region = East: Nagelkerke R2 = 0.002, chi2(1) = 0.74, p = 0.390 , Accuracy = 58.3%, N = 465 #>   region = West: Nagelkerke R2 = 0.000, chi2(1) = 0.03, p = 0.860 , Accuracy = 57.6%, N = 1956  # Factor predictors: dummy-coding (default, matches base R glm()) logistic_regression(survey_data, high_satisfaction ~ age + education) #> Logistic Regression: high_satisfaction ~ age + education #>   Nagelkerke R2 = 0.084, chi2(4) = 156.25, p < 0.001 ***, Accuracy = 63.4%, N = 2421  # Factor predictors: SPSS-style ordinal-as-scale logistic_regression(survey_data, high_satisfaction ~ age + education,                     factors = "numeric") #> ℹ Factor predictor(s) coerced to numeric (SPSS-style ordinal scaling): #> • `education` #> Logistic Regression: high_satisfaction ~ age + education #>   Nagelkerke R2 = 0.078, chi2(2) = 144.84, p < 0.001 ***, Accuracy = 63.4%, N = 2421  # --- Three-layer output --- result <- logistic_regression(survey_data, high_satisfaction ~ age + income) result                                    # compact one-line overview #> Logistic Regression: high_satisfaction ~ age + income #>   Nagelkerke R2 = 0.209, chi2(2) = 357.43, p < 0.001 ***, Accuracy = 68.4%, N = 2115 summary(result)                           # full detailed SPSS-style output #>  #> Logistic Regression Results #> --------------------------- #> - title: Longitudinal Study Data (Synthetic) — longitudinal_data
text: synthetic repeated measures dataset suitable testing longitudinal analysis functions. Contains realistic treatment effects, missing data patterns, within-subject correlations.

title: Longitudinal Study Data (Synthetic) — longitudinal_data
text: data frame 480 rows 10 variables: subject_id Subject identifier (factor, 1-120) group Treatment group (Control, Treatment) age Age years (numeric) gender Gender (Male, Female) time Time point (T1, T2, T3, T4) time_numeric Time point numeric (1-4) outcome_score Primary outcome measure (continuous) secondary_outcome Secondary outcome measure (continuous) physio_measure Physiological measure (continuous) questionnaire_score Questionnaire rating (ordered factor, 1-7)

title: Longitudinal Study Data (Synthetic) — longitudinal_data
text: Generated synthetically realistic longitudinal correlation patterns treatment effects. real study data used.

title: Longitudinal Study Data (Synthetic) — longitudinal_data
text: Study design features: 120 subjects (60 per group) 4 measurement occasions Balanced treatment assignment Realistic dropout patterns (0% T1, 35% T4) Treatment effect grows time (interaction effect) Autoregressive error structure Effect size approximately Cohen's d = 0.6 Missing data patterns reflect realistic longitudinal study attrition higher dropout later time points. treatment effect demonstrates clear Group x Time interaction suitable repeated measures testing.

title: Compare Two Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — mann_whitney
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... variables want compare groups. can list multiple variables use helpers like starts_with("satisfaction") group categorical variable defines two groups (e.g., gender, treatment/control). Must exactly two groups. weights Optional survey weights population-representative results mu hypothesized difference (Default: 0, meaning difference) alternative Direction test: "two.sided" (default): Test groups different "greater": Test group 1 > group 2 "less": Test group 1 < group 2 conf.level Confidence level intervals (Default: 0.95 = 95%)

title: Compare Two Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — mann_whitney

title: Compare Two Groups Without Assuming Normal Data — mann_whitney

code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Basic Mann-Whitney test (non-parametric comparison) survey_data %>%   mann_whitney(age, group = gender) #> Mann-Whitney U Test: age by gender #>   U = 776,732, Z = -0.164, p = 0.870 , r = 0.003 (negligible), N = 2500  # Multiple variables survey_data %>%   mann_whitney(age, income, life_satisfaction, group = region) #> Mann-Whitney U Test: age by region #>   U = 462,234, Z = -1.850, p = 0.064 , r = 0.037 (negligible), N = 2500 #> Mann-Whitney U Test: income by region #>   U = 374,226, Z = -0.226, p = 0.821 , r = 0.005 (negligible), N = 2186 #> Mann-Whitney U Test: life_satisfaction by region #>   U = 452,568, Z = -0.168, p = 0.867 , r = 0.003 (negligible), N = 2421  # Using tidyselect helpers survey_data %>%   mann_whitney(starts_with("trust_"), group = gender) #> Mann-Whitney U Test: trust_government by gender #>   U = 678,806, Z = -0.775, p = 0.438 , r = 0.016 (negligible), N = 2354 #> Mann-Whitney U Test: trust_media by gender #>   U = 661,523, Z = -2.320, p = 0.020 *, r = 0.048 (negligible), N = 2367 #> Mann-Whitney U Test: trust_science by gender #>   U = 697,309, Z = -1.234, p = 0.217 , r = 0.025 (negligible), N = 2398  # Weighted analysis survey_data %>%   mann_whitney(income, group = region, weights = sampling_weight) #> Mann-Whitney U Test: income by region [Weighted] #>   U = 386,321, Z = -0.536, p = 0.592 , r = 0.011 (negligible), N = 2201  # Grouped analysis (separate tests for each education level) survey_data %>%   group_by(education) %>%   mann_whitney(life_satisfaction, group = gender) #> [education = 1] #> Mann-Whitney U Test: life_satisfaction by gender #>   U = 81,439, Z = -0.036, p = 0.971 , r = 0.001 (negligible), N = 809 #> [education = 2] #> Mann-Whitney U Test: life_satisfaction by gender #>   U = 45,218, Z = -1.001, p = 0.317 , r = 0.040 (negligible), N = 618 #> [education = 3] #> Mann-Whitney U Test: life_satisfaction by gender #>   U = 45,788, Z = -0.130, p = 0.897 , r = 0.005 (negligible), N = 607 #> [education = 4] #> Mann-Whitney U Test: life_satisfaction by gender #>   U = 17,319, Z = -1.281, p = 0.200 , r = 0.065 (negligible), N = 387  # One-sided test survey_data %>%   mann_whitney(life_satisfaction, group = region, alternative = "greater") #> Mann-Whitney U Test: life_satisfaction by region #>   U = 452,568, Z = -0.168, p = 0.433 , r = 0.003 (negligible), N = 2421  # --- Three-layer output --- result <- survey_data %>%   mann_whitney(income, group = gender, weights = sampling_weight) result              # compact one-line overview #> Mann-Whitney U Test: income by gender [Weighted] #>   U = 592,273, Z = -0.755, p = 0.450 , r = 0.016 (negligible), N = 2201 summary(result)     # full detailed output with all sections #> Weighted Mann-Whitney U Test Results #> ------------------------------------ #>  #> - Grouping variable: gender #> - Groups compared: Male vs. Female #> - Weights variable: sampling_weight #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #> - Alternative hypothesis: two.sided #> - Null hypothesis (mu): 0.000 #>  #>  #> --- income --- #>  #>   Male: rank mean = 1111.3, n = 1047.9 #>   Female: rank mean = 1090.7, n = 1153.1 #>  #>  #> Weighted Mann-Whitney U Test Results: #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>            Test       U         W      Z p_value effect_r sig #>  Mann-Whitney U 592,273 1,257,619 -0.755    0.45    0.016     #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Note: Weighted analysis uses design-based rank test (Lumley & Scott, 2013). #> U and W are descriptive statistics derived from weighted ranks. #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Effect Size Interpretation (r): #> - Negligible effect: |r| < 0.1 #> - Small effect: |r| ~ 0.1 #> - Medium effect: |r| ~ 0.3 #> - Large effect: |r| ~ 0.5 summary(result, effect_sizes = FALSE)  # hide effect sizes #> Weighted Mann-Whitney U Test Results #> ------------------------------------ #>  #> - Grouping variable: gender #> - Groups compared: Male vs. Female #> - Weights variable: sampling_weight #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #> - Alternative hypothesis: two.sided #> - Null hypothesis (mu): 0.000 #>  #>  #> --- income --- #>  #>   Male: rank mean = 1111.3, n = 1047.9 #>   Female: rank mean = 1090.7, n = 1153.1 #>  #>  #> Weighted Mann-Whitney U Test Results: #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>            Test       U         W      Z p_value effect_r sig #>  Mann-Whitney U 592,273 1,257,619 -0.755    0.45    0.016     #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Note: Weighted analysis uses design-based rank test (Lumley & Scott, 2013). #> U and W are descriptive statistics derived from weighted ranks. #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05

text: mcnemar_test() tests whether paired proportions changed two dichotomous measurements. Use /comparisons categorical outcomes. Think : paired comparison test binary (yes/) data categorical equivalent paired t-test Tests whether proportion "changers" symmetric test tells : Whether paired proportions changed significantly asymptotic exact p-values maximum reliability number discordant pairs (actually changed)

Additional arguments (currently unused)

title: McNemar's Test for Paired Proportions — mcnemar_test

title: McNemar's Test for Paired Proportions — mcnemar_test
text: P-value: p < 0.05, paired proportions significantly different p < 0.001: strong evidence change p < 0.01: Strong evidence change p < 0.05: Moderate evidence change p >= 0.05: significant change found Exact vs Asymptotic: exact binomial p-value reliable small samples. large samples, p-values similar. Discordant Pairs: pairs two measurements differ (b c) contribute test. b approximately equals c, evidence systematic change.

code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Create dichotomous variables test_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(     trust_gov_high = as.integer(trust_government >= 4),     trust_media_high = as.integer(trust_media >= 4)   )  # McNemar test test_data %>%   mcnemar_test(var1 = trust_gov_high, var2 = trust_media_high) #> McNemar Test: trust_gov_high x trust_media_high #>   chi2 = 15.116, p < 0.001 (asymp), p < 0.001 (exact) ***, N = 2227 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Grouped analysis test_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   mcnemar_test(var1 = trust_gov_high, var2 = trust_media_high) #> [region = 1] #> McNemar Test: trust_gov_high x trust_media_high #>   chi2 = 8.255, p = 0.004 (asymp), p = 0.004 (exact) **, N = 435 #> [region = 2] #> McNemar Test: trust_gov_high x trust_media_high #>   chi2 = 8.242, p = 0.004 (asymp), p = 0.004 (exact) **, N = 1792 #> Use summary() for detailed output.

#> One-Way ANOVA: life_satisfaction by education #>   F(3, 2417) = 67.096, p < 0.001 ***, eta2 = 0.077 (medium), N = 2421  # Multiple dependent variables survey_data %>%   oneway_anova(life_satisfaction, trust_government, group = education) #> One-Way ANOVA: life_satisfaction by education #>   F(3, 2417) = 67.096, p < 0.001 ***, eta2 = 0.077 (medium), N = 2421 #> One-Way ANOVA: trust_government by education #>   F(3, 2350) = 0.431, p = 0.731 , eta2 = 0.001 (negligible), N = 2354  # Using tidyselect helpers survey_data %>%   oneway_anova(starts_with("trust_"), group = education) #> One-Way ANOVA: trust_government by education #>   F(3, 2350) = 0.431, p = 0.731 , eta2 = 0.001 (negligible), N = 2354 #> One-Way ANOVA: trust_media by education #>   F(3, 2363) = 0.902, p = 0.439 , eta2 = 0.001 (negligible), N = 2367 #> One-Way ANOVA: trust_science by education #>   F(3, 2394) = 0.605, p = 0.612 , eta2 = 0.001 (negligible), N = 2398  # Weighted analysis survey_data %>%   oneway_anova(income, group = education, weights = sampling_weight) #> One-Way ANOVA: income by education [Weighted] #>   F(3, 2196) = 462.115, p < 0.001 ***, eta2 = 0.387 (large), N = 2200  # Grouped analysis (separate ANOVA for each region) survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   oneway_anova(life_satisfaction, group = education) #> [region = 1] #> One-Way ANOVA: life_satisfaction by education #>   F(3, 461) = 6.950, p < 0.001 ***, eta2 = 0.043 (small), N = 465 #> [region = 2] #> One-Way ANOVA: life_satisfaction by education #>   F(3, 1952) = 62.153, p < 0.001 ***, eta2 = 0.087 (medium), N = 1956  # Store results for post-hoc analysis result <- survey_data %>%   oneway_anova(life_satisfaction, group = education)  # Follow up with post-hoc tests result %>% what: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
text: pairwise_wilcoxon() tells exactly pairs measurements differ Friedman test finds overall differences. performs pairwise Wilcoxon signed-rank tests p-value correction. Think : Friedman says "differences somewhere among measurements" Pairwise Wilcoxon says "specifically, Measurement differs Measurement C" way make possible pairwise comparisons repeated measures

title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon

title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
text: x Friedman test results friedman_test() ... Additional arguments passed methods. method friedman_test objects accepts p_adjust (character): method adjusting p-values multiple comparisons. Options: "bonferroni" (default, conservative), "holm", "BH", "hochberg", "hommel", "", "fdr", "none".

title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
text: Pairwise comparison results showing: measurement pairs significantly different Z-statistics Wilcoxon signed-rank tests Adjusted p-values (controlling multiple comparisons)

title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
text: Z-Statistics: Based Wilcoxon signed-rank test pair Large absolute Z values indicate big differences two measurements Positive Z: Values var1 tend higher var2 Negative Z: Values var2 tend higher var1 Adjusted P-values: Control multiple comparisons p < 0.05: Measurements significantly different p >= 0.05: significant difference measurements

title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
text: pair measurements, Wilcoxon signed-rank test: Computes differences two measurements Ranks absolute differences Computes Z-statistic based rank sums Uses normal approximation tie correction

title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
text: Bonferroni (default): conservative, multiplies p number comparisons Holm: Step-method, less conservative Bonferroni BH: Controls false discovery rate, good many comparisons

title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
text: Use pairwise Wilcoxon : Friedman test shows significant differences (p < 0.05) want know specific measurements differ data ordinal violate normality assumptions repeated measures matched groups

title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
text: Non-parametric post-hoc repeated measures (like Dunn independent groups) Follow-friedman_test(), like dunn_test() follows kruskal_wallis()

title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
text: parent friedman_test() result weighted, pairwise test uses frequency-weighted signed-rank formulas. SPSS NPAR TESTS ignores WEIGHT , weighted results SPSS reference (R-, guarded internal invariance suite); see vignette("spss-compatibility") validation status. pairwise comparison uses wilcoxon_test() logic internally

text: Wilcoxon, F. (1945). Individual comparisons ranking methods. Biometrics Bulletin, 1(6), 80-83.

title: Find Which Specific Measurements Differ After Friedman Test — pairwise_wilcoxon
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Perform Friedman followed by pairwise Wilcoxon post-hoc friedman_result <- survey_data %>%   friedman_test(trust_government, trust_media, trust_science)  # Pairwise Wilcoxon comparisons (default: Bonferroni) friedman_result %>% pairwise_wilcoxon() #> Pairwise Wilcoxon Post-Hoc Test (Bonferroni) #>   3 comparisons, 3 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table.  # With Holm correction (less conservative) friedman_result %>% pairwise_wilcoxon(p_adjust = "holm") #> Pairwise Wilcoxon Post-Hoc Test (Holm) #>   3 comparisons, 3 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table.  # With Benjamini-Hochberg (controls false discovery rate) friedman_result %>% pairwise_wilcoxon(p_adjust = "BH") #> Pairwise Wilcoxon Post-Hoc Test (Benjamini-Hochberg) #>   3 comparisons, 3 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table.  # With weights fw_weighted <- survey_data %>%   friedman_test(trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,                 weights = sampling_weight)  fw_weighted %>% pairwise_wilcoxon() #> Pairwise Wilcoxon Post-Hoc Test (Bonferroni) [Weighted] #>   3 comparisons, 3 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table.  # Grouped analysis fw_grouped <- survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   friedman_test(trust_government, trust_media, trust_science)  fw_grouped %>% pairwise_wilcoxon() #> Pairwise Wilcoxon Post-Hoc Test (Bonferroni) #> [region = East] #>   3 comparisons, 3 significant (p < .05) #> [region = West] #>   3 comparisons, 3 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table.

what: Measure How Strongly Variables Are Related — pearson_cor
title: Measure How Strongly Variables Are Related — pearson_cor
text: pearson_cor() shows strongly numeric variables related . example, age related income? satisfaction increase experience? helps understand patterns data. correlation tells : Direction: Positive (increase together) negative (one increases decreases) Strength: closely variables move together (0 = relationship 1 = perfect relationship) Significance: Whether relationship real due chance

title: Measure How Strongly Variables Are Related — pearson_cor
code: pearson_cor(   data,   ...,   weights = NULL,   conf.level = 0.95,   alternative = c("two.sided", "less", "greater"),   use = c("pairwise", "listwise"),   na.rm = NULL )

title: Measure How Strongly Variables Are Related — pearson_cor
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... numeric variables want correlate. List two single correlation correlation matrix. weights Optional survey weights population-representative results. Following SPSS CORRELATIONS, weighted degrees freedom use n = sum(w) (Kish's effective sample size). appropriate normalized survey weights mean \(\approx 1\). raw expansion weights (e.g., summing millions population units), resulting standard errors confidence intervals drastically narrow — case, normalize weights sum(w) == n, use survey package design-based inference. conf.level Confidence level intervals (Default: 0.95 = 95%) alternative Direction test: "two.sided" (default), "less", "greater". use handle missing values: "pairwise" (default): Use available data pair "listwise": use complete cases across variables na.rm Deprecated. Use use instead.

title: Measure How Strongly Variables Are Related — pearson_cor
text: Correlation results showing relationships variables, including: Correlation coefficient (r): Strength direction relationship P-value: Whether relationship statistically significant Confidence interval: Range plausible correlation values Sample size: Number observations used Use summary() full SPSS-style output toggleable sections.

title: Measure How Strongly Variables Are Related — pearson_cor
text: Correlation coefficient (r) ranges -1 +1: +1: Perfect positive relationship (one goes , always goes ) 0: linear relationship -1: Perfect negative relationship (one goes , always goes ) Interpreting strength (absolute value r): 0.00 - 0.10: Negligible relationship 0.10 - 0.30: Weak relationship 0.30 - 0.50: Moderate relationship 0.50 - 0.70: Strong relationship 0.70 - 0.90: strong relationship 0.90 - 1.00: Extremely strong relationship P-value interpretation: p < 0.001: strong evidence relationship p < 0.01: Strong evidence relationship p < 0.05: Moderate evidence relationship p >= 0.05: significant relationship found correlation 0.65 p < 0.001 means: Strong positive relationship (r = 0.65) one variable increases, tends increase unlikely due chance (p < 0.001) 42% variation shared (r-squared = 0.65 squared = 0.42)

title: Measure How Strongly Variables Are Related — pearson_cor
text: Use Pearson correlation : variables numeric continuous expect linear relationship Data roughly normally distributed want measure strength linear association use : Data extreme outliers (consider Spearman instead) Relationship curved/non-linear Variables categorical (use chi-squared test) need establish causation (correlation imply causation)

title: Measure How Strongly Variables Are Related — pearson_cor
text: Always plot data first check non-linear patterns Consider statistical significance (p-value) practical importance (r value) Remember: correlation imply causation Check outliers might inflate deflate correlations Use Spearman correlation ordinal data non-normal distributions

title: Measure How Strongly Variables Are Related — pearson_cor
text: Cohen, J. (1988). Statistical Power Analysis Behavioral Sciences (2nd ed.). Lawrence Erlbaum Associates. Fisher, R. . (1915). Frequency distribution values correlation coefficient samples indefinitely large population. Biometrika, 10(4), 507–521.

what: Transform Scores to Percent of Maximum Possible (POMPS) — pomps
title: Transform Scores to Percent of Maximum Possible (POMPS) — pomps
text: pomps() transforms scores 0-100 scale using Percent Maximum Possible Scores method. makes different scales directly comparable regardless original range. R equivalent SPSS formula: COMPUTE v81p = ((v81 - 1) / (7 - 1)) * 100. score 0 means minimum possible score, 100 means maximum. transformation preserves correlations variables.

title: Transform Scores to Percent of Maximum Possible (POMPS) — pomps
code: pomps(x, scale_min = NULL, scale_max = NULL)

title: Transform Scores to Percent of Maximum Possible (POMPS) — pomps
text: x numeric vector transform (e.g., column data). scale_min theoretical minimum scale. NULL (default), observed minimum x used. scale_max theoretical maximum scale. NULL (default), observed maximum x used.

title: Transform Scores to Percent of Maximum Possible (POMPS) — pomps
text: numeric vector length x, values rescaled 0-100 range.

title: Transform Scores to Percent of Maximum Possible (POMPS) — pomps
text: POMPS = ((score - scale_min) / (scale_max - scale_min)) * 100

title: Transform Scores to Percent of Maximum Possible (POMPS) — pomps
text: default, pomps() uses observed minimum maximum data. However, Likert scales specify theoretical range: 1-5 Likert scale: scale_min = 1, scale_max = 5 1-7 Likert scale: scale_min = 1, scale_max = 7 0-10 scale: scale_min = 0, scale_max = 10 Using theoretical values ensures transformation consistent across samples time points.

title: Transform Scores to Percent of Maximum Possible (POMPS) — pomps
text: Comparing variables measured different scales Creating profile plots across scales different ranges Reporting scale scores intuitive 0-100 format

title: Transform Scores to Percent of Maximum Possible (POMPS) — pomps
code: library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Transform a 1-5 Likert scale to POMPS survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(trust_gov_pomps = pomps(trust_government, scale_min = 1, scale_max = 5))  # Transform multiple variables with the same scale survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(across(     c(trust_government, trust_media, trust_science),     ~ pomps(.x, scale_min = 1, scale_max = 5),     .names = "{.col}_pomps"   ))  # Auto-detect range (uses observed min/max) survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(age_pomps = pomps(age))

title: Print Dunn post-hoc test results (compact) — print.dunn_test
code: result <- kruskal_wallis(survey_data, life_satisfaction,                          group = education) |> dunn_test() result              # compact overview #> Dunn Post-Hoc Test (Bonferroni) by education #>   life_satisfaction: 6 comparisons, 5 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table. summary(result)     # full comparison tables #> Dunn Post-Hoc Test (Bonferroni) Results #> --------------------------------------- #>  #> - Dependent variable: life_satisfaction #> - Grouping variable: education #> - P-value adjustment: Bonferroni #>  #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>                 Group 1                Group 2       Z p (unadj) p (adj) Sig  #>         Basic Secondary Intermediate Secondary  -7.658     <.001   <.001 ***  #>         Basic Secondary     Academic Secondary  -9.792     <.001   <.001 ***  #>         Basic Secondary             University -11.545     <.001   <.001 ***  #>  Intermediate Secondary     Academic Secondary  -2.042     0.041   0.247      #>  Intermediate Secondary             University  -4.696     <.001   <.001 ***  #>      Academic Secondary             University  -2.886     0.004   0.023   *  #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - Positive Z: First group has higher mean rank #> - Negative Z: First group has lower mean rank #> - p-values are adjusted for multiple comparisons

code: result <- friedman_test(survey_data, trust_government, trust_media,                         trust_science) result              # compact one-line overview #> Friedman Test: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #>   chi2(2) = 1009.035, p < 0.001 ***, W = 0.236, N = 2135 #> Use summary() for detailed output. summary(result)     # full detailed output #> Friedman Test Results #> --------------------- #>  #> - Variables: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - Number of conditions: 3 #>  #>   Ranks: #>   --------------------------- #>            Variable Mean Rank #>    trust_government      1.81 #>         trust_media      1.68 #>       trust_science      2.51 #>   --------------------------- #>  #>   Test Statistics: #>   ------------------------------------------- #>       N Chi-Square df p value Kendall's W sig #>    2135   1009.035  2       0       0.236 *** #>   ------------------------------------------- #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Effect Size Interpretation (Kendall's W): #> - Weak agreement: 0.1 - 0.3 #> - Moderate agreement: 0.3 - 0.5 #> - Strong agreement: > 0.5

code: test_data <- transform(survey_data,   trust_gov_high = as.integer(trust_government >= 4),   trust_media_high = as.integer(trust_media >= 4)) result <- mcnemar_test(test_data, var1 = trust_gov_high,                        var2 = trust_media_high) result              # compact one-line overview #> McNemar Test: trust_gov_high x trust_media_high #>   chi2 = 15.116, p < 0.001 (asymp), p < 0.001 (exact) ***, N = 2227 #> Use summary() for detailed output. summary(result)     # full detailed output #> McNemar Test Results #> -------------------- #>  #> - Variable 1: trust_gov_high #> - Variable 2: trust_media_high #>  #> 2x2 Contingency Table: #> ----------------------------------------  #>    v2 #> v1     0    1 #>   0 1342  338 #>   1  448   99 #> ----------------------------------------  #>  #> Test Results: #> -----------------------------------------  #>  Chi-Sq (cc) p (asymp) p (exact)    N Sig  #>       15.116     <.001     <.001 2227 ***  #> -----------------------------------------  #>  #> Discordant pairs: b = 338, c = 448 #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05

code: result <- oneway_anova(survey_data, life_satisfaction,                        group = education) result              # compact one-line overview #> One-Way ANOVA: life_satisfaction by education #>   F(3, 2417) = 67.096, p < 0.001 ***, eta2 = 0.077 (medium), N = 2421 summary(result)     # full detailed output #> One-Way ANOVA Results #> --------------------- #>  #> - Dependent variable: life_satisfaction #> - Grouping variable: education #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #>   Null hypothesis: All group means are equal #>   Alternative hypothesis: At least one group mean differs #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #> Descriptive Statistics by Group: #>   Basic Secondary: mean = 3.204, sd = 1.243, n = 809 #>   Intermediate Secondary: mean = 3.701, sd = 1.112, n = 618 #>   Academic Secondary: mean = 3.853, sd = 0.998, n = 607 #>   University: mean = 4.047, sd = 0.957, n = 387 #>  #> ANOVA Results: #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>          Source Sum_Squares   df Mean_Square      F p_value sig #>  Between Groups     247.347    3      82.449 67.096   <.001 *** #>   Within Groups    2970.080 2417       1.229                    #>           Total    3217.428 2420                                #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Assumption Tests: #> ----------------  #>  Assumption Statistic df1  df2 p_value sig #>       Welch    64.489   3 1229   <.001 *** #>  #> Effect Sizes: #> ------------  #>           Variable Eta_Squared Epsilon_Squared Omega_Squared Effect_Size #>  life_satisfaction       0.077           0.076         0.076      medium #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Effect Size Interpretation: #> - Eta-squared: Proportion of variance explained (biased upward) #> - Epsilon-squared: Less biased than eta-squared #> - Omega-squared: Unbiased estimate (preferred for publication) #> - Small effect: eta-squared ~ 0.01, Medium effect: eta-squared ~ 0.06, Large effect: eta-squared ~ 0.14 #>  #> Post-hoc tests: Use tukey_test() for pairwise comparisons

code: result <- oneway_anova(survey_data, life_satisfaction,                        group = education) |> scheffe_test() result              # compact overview #> Scheffe Post-Hoc Test by education #>   life_satisfaction: 6 comparisons, 4 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table. summary(result)     # full comparison tables #> Scheffe Post-Hoc Test Results #> ----------------------------- #>  #> - Dependent variable: life_satisfaction #> - Grouping variable: education #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #>   Family-wise error rate controlled using Scheffe's method #>   Note: Most conservative post-hoc test (widest confidence intervals) #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #> Scheffe Results: #>   -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>   Comparison                                   Difference  Lower CI  Upper CI  p-value  Sig  #>   -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>   Basic Secondary - Intermediate Secondary         -0.497    -0.662    -0.331    <.001  ***  #>   Basic Secondary - Academic Secondary             -0.649    -0.816    -0.483    <.001  ***  #>   Basic Secondary - University                     -0.843    -1.034    -0.651    <.001  ***  #>   Intermediate Secondary - Academic Secondary      -0.153    -0.330     0.024     .121       #>   Intermediate Secondary - University              -0.346    -0.547    -0.145    <.001  ***  #>   Academic Secondary - University                  -0.193    -0.395     0.009     .067       #>   -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - Positive differences: First group > Second group #> - Negative differences: First group < Second group #> - Confidence intervals not containing 0 indicate significant differences #> - p-values are adjusted for all possible contrasts (most conservative) #> - Scheffe test has wider CIs than Tukey HSD
