Eta-squared: Proportion of variance explained (biased upward) #> - Epsilon-squared: Less biased than eta-squared #> - Omega-squared: Unbiased estimate (preferred for publication) #> - Small effect: eta-squared ~ 0.01, Medium effect: eta-squared ~ 0.06, Large effect: eta-squared ~ 0.14 #>  #> Post-hoc tests: Use tukey_test() for pairwise comparisons summary(result, descriptives = FALSE)  # hide group statistics #> One-Way ANOVA Results #> --------------------- #>  #> - Dependent variable: life_satisfaction #> - Grouping variable: education #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #>   Null hypothesis: All group means are equal #>   Alternative hypothesis: At least one group mean differs #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #>  #> ANOVA Results: #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>          Source Sum_Squares   df Mean_Square      F p_value sig #>  Between Groups     247.347    3      82.449 67.096   <.001 *** #>   Within Groups    2970.080 2417       1.229                    #>           Total    3217.428 2420                                #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Assumption Tests: #> ----------------  #>  Assumption Statistic df1  df2 p_value sig #>       Welch    64.489   3 1229   <.001 *** #>  #> Effect Sizes: #> ------------  #>           Variable Eta_Squared Epsilon_Squared Omega_Squared Effect_Size #>  life_satisfaction       0.077           0.076         0.076      medium #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Effect Size Interpretation: #> - Dependent variable: life_satisfaction #> - Grouping variable: education #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #>   Family-wise error rate controlled using Scheffe's method #>   Note: Most conservative post-hoc test (widest confidence intervals) #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #> Scheffe Results: #>   -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>   Comparison                                   Difference  Lower CI  Upper CI  p-value  Sig  #>   -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>   Basic Secondary - Intermediate Secondary         -0.497    -0.662    -0.331    <.001  ***  #>   Basic Secondary - Academic Secondary             -0.649    -0.816    -0.483    <.001  ***  #>   Basic Secondary - University                     -0.843    -1.034    -0.651    <.001  ***  #>   Intermediate Secondary - Academic Secondary      -0.153    -0.330     0.024     .121       #>   Intermediate Secondary - University              -0.346    -0.547    -0.145    <.001  ***  #>   Academic Secondary - University                  -0.193    -0.395     0.009     .067       #>   -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - Positive differences: First group > Second group #> - Negative differences: First group < Second group #> - Confidence intervals not containing 0 indicate significant differences #> - p-values are adjusted for all possible contrasts (most conservative) #> - Scheffe test has wider CIs than Tukey HSD summary(result, comparisons = FALSE)  # hide comparison tables #> Scheffe Post-Hoc Test Results #> ----------------------------- #>  #> - Dependent variable: life_satisfaction #> - Grouping variable: education #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #>   Family-wise error rate controlled using Scheffe's method #>   Note: Most conservative post-hoc test (widest confidence intervals) #>  #>  #> Interpretation: #> - Positive differences: First group > Second group #> - Negative differences: First group < Second group #> - Confidence intervals not containing 0 indicate significant differences #> - p-values are adjusted for all possible contrasts (most conservative) #> - Scheffe test has wider CIs than Tukey HSD

Cohen's d: pooled standard deviation (classic) #> - Hedges' g: bias-corrected Cohen's d (preferred) #> - Glass' Delta: control group standard deviation only #> - Small effect: |effect| ~ 0.2 #> - Medium effect: |effect| ~ 0.5 #> - code: result <- wilcoxon_test(survey_data, x = trust_government, y = trust_media) result              # compact one-line overview #> Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test: trust_media - trust_government #>   Z = -5.097, p < 0.001 ***, r = 0.123 (small), N = 2227 #> Use summary() for detailed output. summary(result)     # full detailed output #> Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test Results #> --------------------------------- #>  #> - Pair: trust_media vs trust_government #>  #> trust_media - trust_government #> ------------------------------ #>   Ranks: #>   ------------------------------------------- #>                      N Mean Rank Sum of Ranks #>    Negative Ranks  955    887.53     847592.5 #>    Positive Ranks  770    832.57     641082.5 #>              Ties  502        NA           NA #>             Total 2227        NA           NA #>   ------------------------------------------- #>  #>   a trust_media < trust_government #>   b trust_media > trust_government #>   c trust_media = trust_government #>  #>   Test Statistics: #>   ---------------------------- #>         Z p value Effect r sig #>    -5.097       0    0.123 *** #>   ---------------------------- #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Effect Size Interpretation (r): #> - Small effect: 0.1 - 0.3 #> - Medium effect: 0.3 - 0.5 #> - Large effect: > 0.5

title: Read SAS Data with Tagged Missing Values — read_sas
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Read SAS file with native special missing values data <- read_sas("survey.sas7bdat")  # Read with catalog for value labels data <- read_sas("survey.sas7bdat", catalog_file = "formats.sas7bcat")  # Read SAS file with numeric missing codes data <- read_sas("survey.sas7bdat", tag_na = c(-9, -8, -42))  # Check what types of missing values exist na_frequencies(data$income)  # Recover original codes or convert to regular NAs untag_na(data$income)   # Recovers -9, -8, etc. strip_tags(data$income) # Converts all to NA } # }

what: Read SPSS Data with Tagged Missing Values — read_spss
title: Read SPSS Data with Tagged Missing Values — read_spss
text: Reads SPSS .sav file preserves user-defined missing values tagged NAs instead converting regular NA. allows distinguish different types missing data (e.g., "answer", "applicable", "refused") still treating NA standard R operations.

title: Read SPSS Data with Tagged Missing Values — read_spss
code: read_spss(path, tag_na = TRUE, encoding = NULL, verbose = FALSE)

title: Read SPSS Data with Tagged Missing Values — read_spss
text: path Path SPSS .sav file. tag_na TRUE (default), user-defined missing values converted tagged NAs using haven::tagged_na(). FALSE, file read standard haven::read_sav() behavior (missing values become regular NA). encoding Character encoding file. NULL, haven's default encoding detection used. verbose TRUE, prints message summarizing many values converted.

title: Read SPSS Data with Tagged Missing Values — read_spss
text: tibble SPSS data. tag_na = TRUE: User-defined missing values stored tagged NAs .na() returns TRUE values (standard R behavior) original SPSS missing codes can recovered via na_frequencies(), untag_na(), haven::na_tag() tagged variable "na_tag_map" attribute mapping tag characters original SPSS codes

title: Read SPSS Data with Tagged Missing Values — read_spss
text: SPSS allows defining specific values "user-defined missing values" (e.g., -9 = "answer", -8 = "know"). reading .sav files haven::read_sav(), silently converted NA, losing information value missing. read_spss() preserves information using haven's tagged NA system: missing value type gets unique tag character (-z, -Z, 0-9) can inspected haven::na_tag(). values still behave NA standard R operations (mean(), sum(), .na(), etc.). Use companion functions work tagged NAs: na_frequencies() - Frequency table missing types untag_na() - Convert tagged NAs back original SPSS codes strip_tags() - Convert tagged NAs regular NAs (drop tags)

title: Read SPSS Data with Tagged Missing Values — read_spss
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Read SPSS file with tagged missing values data <- read_spss("survey.sav")  # Check what types of missing values exist na_frequencies(data$satisfaction)  # Standard R operations work normally (NAs are excluded) mean(data$satisfaction, na.rm = TRUE)  # frequency() shows each missing type separately data %>% frequency(satisfaction)  # Recover original SPSS codes original_codes <- untag_na(data$satisfaction)  # Convert to regular NAs (standard behavior) data_clean <- strip_tags(data$satisfaction) } # }

title: Read Stata Data with Tagged Missing Values — read_stata
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Read Stata file with native extended missing values data <- read_stata("survey.dta")  # Read Stata file with SPSS-style missing codes data <- read_stata("survey.dta", tag_na = c(-9, -8, -42, -11))  # Check what types of missing values exist na_frequencies(data$income)  # frequency() and codebook() show each missing type separately data %>% frequency(income) codebook(data)  # Recover original codes or convert to regular NAs untag_na(data$income)   # Recovers -9, -8, etc. strip_tags(data$income) # Converts all to NA } # }

what: Recode Variables Using String Syntax — rec
title: Recode Variables Using String Syntax — rec
text: rec() recodes values variable using intuitive string syntax. consolidates recoding, reversing, dichotomizing, missing value handling single function — R equivalent SPSS's RECODE command.

title: Recode Variables Using String Syntax — rec
code: rec(   data,   ...,   rules,   as_factor = FALSE,   suffix = NULL,   var_label = NULL,   val_labels = NULL,   as.factor = NULL,   var.label = NULL,   val.labels = NULL )

title: Recode Variables Using String Syntax — rec
text: data data frame numeric vector. data frame passed, use ... select variables. ... Variables recode (tidyselect). used data data frame. rules character string defining recoding rules (see Details). as_factor TRUE, return result factor. Default: FALSE. suffix character string appended column names recoded variables (e.g., "_r"). NULL (default), original columns overwritten -place. var_label new variable label. NULL, existing label kept " (recoded)" appended. val_labels named character vector value labels new values (e.g., c("1" = "Low", "2" = "Medium", "3" = "High")). NULL, labels taken inline [Label] syntax rules (present). Explicit val_labels always override inline labels. .factor, var.label, val.labels Defunct dot-case argument names, removed mariposa 0.6.9. Calling function error; use snake_case equivalents instead. (formals retained old names error clearly instead swallowed ....)

title: Recode Variables Using String Syntax — rec
text: data vector, recoded vector returned. data data frame, modified data frame returned (invisibly).

title: Recode Variables Using String Syntax — rec
text: Rules specified semicolon-separated string "old=new" pairs: Rules evaluated order — first matching rule wins.

title: Recode Variables Using String Syntax — rec
text: single values ranges accept decimals (e.g. "3.6=2" "2.5:3.5=1"). Single-value matching compares values strings (rounded 15 significant digits), decimal codes match reliably even stored floating-point representation error.

title: Recode Variables Using String Syntax — rec
text: can attach value labels directly rules string using square brackets new value: "1:2=1 [Low]; 3=2 [Medium]; 4:5=3 [High]" equivalent specifying val_labels = c("1" = "Low", "2" = "Medium", "3" = "High") compact self-documenting. inline labels val_labels provided, val_labels takes precedence.

title: Recode Variables Using String Syntax — rec
text: "rev" reverses scale computing max(x) + min(x) - x. Value labels mirrored accordingly. "dicho" dichotomizes median: values \(\le\) median become 0, values \(>\) median become 1. "dicho(x)" dichotomizes fixed cut-point x: values \(\le x\) become 0, values \(> x\) become 1. "mean" dichotomizes arithmetic mean: values \(\le\) mean become 0, values \(>\) mean become 1. "quart" splits four quartile groups using quantile(): values \(\le\) Q1 become 1, Q1–Q2 become 2, Q2–Q3 become 3, \(>\) Q3 become 4. Quartile boundaries computed unweighted.

title: Recode Variables Using String Syntax — rec
code: library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Collapse a 5-point scale to 3 categories (inline labels) data <- rec(survey_data, trust_government,             rules = "1:2=1 [Low]; 3=2 [Medium]; 4:5=3 [High]")  # Reverse a scale (with suffix to keep original) data <- rec(survey_data, trust_government, trust_media,             rules = "rev", suffix = "_r")  # Dichotomize at the median data <- rec(survey_data, age, rules = "dicho", suffix = "_d")  # Set missing value codes to NA data <- rec(survey_data, starts_with("trust"),             rules = "-9=NA; -8=NA; else=copy")  # Replace NA with 0 data <- rec(survey_data, trust_government,             rules = "NA=0; else=copy")  # Quartile split data <- rec(survey_data, age, rules = "quart", suffix = "_q")  # Use inside mutate() survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(     trust_gov_3 = rec(trust_government,                       rules = "1:2=1 [Low]; 3=2 [Medium]; 4:5=3 [High]"),     age_q = rec(age, rules = "quart")   )

what: Check How Reliably Your Scale Measures a Concept — reliability
title: Check How Reliably Your Scale Measures a Concept — reliability
text: reliability() calculates Cronbach's Alpha, McDonald's Omega, detailed item statistics evaluate whether survey items form reliable scale. R equivalent SPSS's RELIABILITY /MODEL=ALPHA /STATISTICS=DESCRIPTIVE CORR /SUMMARY=TOTAL. example, 3 items measuring "trust", reliability analysis tells whether items consistently measure concept.

title: Check How Reliably Your Scale Measures a Concept — reliability
code: reliability(data, ..., weights = NULL, na.rm = TRUE)

title: Check How Reliably Your Scale Measures a Concept — reliability
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... items analyze. Use bare column names separated commas, tidyselect helpers like starts_with("trust"). weights Optional survey weights population-representative results na.rm Remove missing values calculating? (Default: TRUE). Uses listwise deletion (complete cases across items).

title: Check How Reliably Your Scale Measures a Concept — reliability
text: reliability result object containing: alpha Cronbach's Alpha (unstandardized) alpha_standardized Cronbach's Alpha based standardized items omega McDonald's Omega (raw/total, one-factor ML model; NA fewer 3 items) omega_std Standardized Omega (correlation metric) n_items Number items scale item_statistics Mean, SD, N item item_total Corrected Item-Total Correlation, Alpha Item Deleted, Omega Item Deleted inter_item_cor Inter-item correlation matrix n Sample size (listwise) Use summary() full SPSS-style output toggleable sections.

title: Check How Reliably Your Scale Measures a Concept — reliability
text: Cronbach's Alpha tells internally consistent scale : Alpha > 0.90: Excellent reliability Alpha 0.80 - 0.90: Good reliability Alpha 0.70 - 0.80: Acceptable reliability Alpha 0.60 - 0.70: Questionable reliability Alpha < 0.60: Poor reliability - reconsider items Item-Total Correlation shows well item fits scale: Values > 0.40: Item fits well Values 0.20 - 0.40: Item may need review Values < 0.20: Consider removing item Alpha Item Deleted shows happens remove item: alpha increases removing item, item hurts reliability alpha decreases, item contributes scale

title: Check How Reliably Your Scale Measures a Concept — reliability
text: McDonald's Omega (omega total) factor-model-based reliability coefficient. alpha assumes every item measures construct equally well (tau-equivalence), omega fits one-factor model lets item carry loading. two agree items roughly tau-equivalent; omega typically slightly higher (accurate estimate) loadings differ across items, common case survey scales (Hayes & Coutts, 2020). reliability() reports two variants, mirroring two alpha variants: omega covariance metric (analogous raw alpha) omega_std correlation metric (analogous standardized alpha). Omega Item Deleted refits one-factor model without item, mirroring Alpha Item Deleted. one-factor model needs least 3 items identified: fewer 3 items omega fields NA (alpha still computed), Omega Item Deleted NA whenever reduced scale fall 3 items.

title: Check How Reliably Your Scale Measures a Concept — reliability
text: Cronbach's alpha item statistics validated SPSS v29 RELIABILITY output (weighted unweighted). McDonald's omega currently R-statistic (Tier 4 per Validation Charter): SPSS offers omega v27 onward, IBM's algorithm documentation publicly retrievable SPSS v29 reference run exists yet. mariposa computes omega one-factor maximum-likelihood solution (estimator family efa fm = "ml"). weighted omega uses weighted correlation covariance matrices weighted alpha reduces exactly unweighted omega weights equal 1 (enforced internal invariance suite); see vignette("spss-compatibility") validation status.

title: Check How Reliably Your Scale Measures a Concept — reliability
text: Run reliability() creating scale scores row_means: Select items Check reliability acceptable (alpha > .70), create index , review items consider removing problematic ones

title: Check How Reliably Your Scale Measures a Concept — reliability
text: McDonald, R. P. (1999). Test Theory: Unified Treatment. Mahwah, NJ: Lawrence Erlbaum. Hayes, . F., & Coutts, J. J. (2020). Use omega rather Cronbach's alpha estimating reliability. ... Communication Methods Measures, 14(1), 1-24.

title: Check How Reliably Your Scale Measures a Concept — reliability
code: library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Check reliability of trust items reliability(survey_data, trust_government, trust_media, trust_science) #> Reliability Analysis: 3 items #>   Cronbach's Alpha = 0.047 (Poor), McDonald's Omega = 0.047, N = 2135  # With survey weights reliability(survey_data, trust_government, trust_media, trust_science,             weights = sampling_weight) #> Reliability Analysis: 3 items [Weighted] #>   Cronbach's Alpha = 0.052 (Poor), McDonald's Omega = 0.053, N = 2150  # Using tidyselect helpers reliability(survey_data, starts_with("trust")) #> Reliability Analysis: 3 items #>   Cronbach's Alpha = 0.047 (Poor), McDonald's Omega = 0.047, N = 2135  # Grouped by region survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   reliability(trust_government, trust_media, trust_science) #> [region = 1] #> Reliability Analysis: 3 items #>   Cronbach's Alpha = 0.037 (Poor), McDonald's Omega = 0.349, N = 422 #> [region = 2] #> Reliability Analysis: 3 items #>   Cronbach's Alpha = 0.050 (Poor), McDonald's Omega = 0.071, N = 1713  # --- Three-layer output --- result <- reliability(survey_data, trust_government, trust_media, trust_science) result              # compact one-line overview #> Reliability Analysis: 3 items #>   Cronbach's Alpha = 0.047 (Poor), McDonald's Omega = 0.047, N = 2135 summary(result)     # full detailed output with all sections #>  #> Reliability Analysis Results #> ---------------------------- #> - Items: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - N of Items: 3 #>  #> Reliability Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>   Cronbach's Alpha:              0.047 #>   Alpha (standardized):          0.048 #>   McDonald's Omega:              0.047 #>   Omega (standardized):          0.048 #>   N of Items:                    3 #>   N (listwise):                  2135 #>  #> Item Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item  mean    sd    n #>  trust_government 2.621 1.162 2135 #>       trust_media 2.430 1.156 2135 #>     trust_science 3.624 1.034 2135 #>  #> Inter-Item Correlation Matrix: #> ------------------------------  #>                  trust_government trust_media trust_science #> trust_government            1.000       0.014         0.020 #> trust_media                 0.014       1.000         0.015 #> trust_science               0.020       0.015         1.000 #> ------------------------------  #>  #> Item-Total Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item scale_mean_deleted scale_var_deleted corrected_r #>  trust_government               6.05             2.440       0.024 #>       trust_media               6.25             2.467       0.020 #>     trust_science               5.05             2.723       0.025 #>  alpha_deleted omega_deleted #>          0.029            NA #>          0.040            NA #>          0.027            NA #> Note: Omega if item deleted requires at least 4 items #> (a one-factor model on the remaining 2 items is not identified). summary(result, inter_item_correlations = FALSE)  # hide correlations #>  #> Reliability Analysis Results #> ---------------------------- #> - Items: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - N of Items: 3 #>  #> Reliability Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>   Cronbach's Alpha:              0.047 #>   Alpha (standardized):          0.048 #>   McDonald's Omega:              0.047 #>   Omega (standardized):          0.048 #>   N of Items:                    3 #>   N (listwise):                  2135 #>  #> Item Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item  mean    sd    n #>  trust_government 2.621 1.162 2135 #>       trust_media 2.430 1.156 2135 #>     trust_science 3.624 1.034 2135 #>  #> Item-Total Statistics #> ----------------------------------------  #>              item scale_mean_deleted scale_var_deleted corrected_r #>  trust_government               6.05             2.440       0.024 #>       trust_media               6.25             2.467       0.020 #>     trust_science               5.05             2.723       0.025 #>  alpha_deleted omega_deleted #>          0.029            NA #>          0.040            NA #>          0.027            NA #> Note: Omega if item deleted requires at least 4 items #> (a one-factor model on the remaining 2 items is not identified).

what: Compute Row Means Across Items — row_means
title: Compute Row Means Across Items — row_means
text: Calculates mean across multiple variables row. standard way create scale scores survey items — R equivalent SPSS's COMPUTE score = MEAN(var1, var2, var3). Use min_valid require minimum number non-missing items, mirroring SPSS's MEAN.n() syntax: min_valid = 2 corresponds MEAN.2(, b, c).

title: Compute Row Means Across Items — row_means
code: row_means(data, ..., min_valid = NULL, na.rm = TRUE)

title: Compute Row Means Across Items — row_means
text: data survey data (data frame tibble). used inside mutate(), pass . use pick(). ... variables average. Use bare column names separated commas, tidyselect helpers like starts_with("trust"). variables specified, numeric columns data used (useful pick()). min_valid Minimum number non-missing values required compute mean. row fewer valid values, NA returned. Default NULL (compute mean least 1 value valid). na.rm Remove missing values calculating? Default: TRUE.

title: Compute Row Means Across Items — row_means
text: numeric vector one value per row — mean across selected variables. Use inside dplyr::mutate() add new column.

title: Compute Row Means Across Items — row_means
text: row, row_means() computes arithmetic mean selected variables. Missing values ignored default, respondent answered 2 3 items still gets score.

title: Compute Row Means Across Items — row_means
text: practice, often want require minimum number valid responses. someone answered 1 5 items, mean based single item may reliable. Set min_valid control : min_valid = NULL (default): Compute mean number valid values (least 1) min_valid = 2: Require least 2 valid values min_valid = 3: Require least 3 valid values

title: Compute Row Means Across Items — row_means
text: Use row_means() checking reliability reliability: Run reliability() check items form reliable scale Cronbach's Alpha acceptable (typically > .70), create index Use index analyses (t-tests, correlations, regression)

title: Compute Row Means Across Items — row_means
code: library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Create a trust scale from 3 items survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(m_trust = row_means(., trust_government, trust_media, trust_science))  # Using pick() survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(m_trust = row_means(pick(starts_with("trust"))))  # Require at least 2 valid items (like SPSS MEAN.2) survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(m_trust = row_means(., trust_government, trust_media,                              trust_science, min_valid = 2))

what: Compute Row Sums Across Items — row_sums
title: Compute Row Sums Across Items — row_sums
text: Calculates sum across multiple variables row. R equivalent SPSS's COMPUTE total = SUM(var1, var2, var3). Use min_valid require minimum number non-missing items, mirroring SPSS's SUM.n() syntax.

title: Compute Row Sums Across Items — row_sums
code: row_sums(data, ..., min_valid = NULL, na.rm = TRUE)

title: Compute Row Sums Across Items — row_sums
text: data survey data (data frame tibble). used inside mutate(), pass . use pick(). ... variables average. Use bare column names separated commas, tidyselect helpers like starts_with("trust"). variables specified, numeric columns data used (useful pick()). min_valid Minimum number non-missing values required compute mean. row fewer valid values, NA returned. Default NULL (compute mean least 1 value valid). na.rm Remove missing values calculating? Default: TRUE.

title: Compute Row Sums Across Items — row_sums
text: numeric vector one value per row — sum across selected variables.

what: When to Use row_sums() vs row_means()
title: Compute Row Sums Across Items — row_sums
text: row_means(): Likert-type scales want average score (preserves original scale range) row_sums(): count-based scores (e.g., number symptoms endorsed) need total score

title: Compute Row Sums Across Items — row_sums
code: library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Total score across items survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(total = row_sums(., trust_government, trust_media, trust_science))  # With min_valid (like SPSS SUM.3) survey_data <- survey_data %>%   mutate(total = row_sums(., trust_government, trust_media,                           trust_science, min_valid = 3))

what: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
text: scheffe_test() tells groups differ ANOVA, using conservative approach. like Tukey's test even careful avoiding false positives. Think : cautious post-hoc test available way compare groups sample sizes unequal Insurance finding differences real

title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
code: scheffe_test(x, conf.level = 0.95, ...)  # Default S3 method scheffe_test(x, conf.level = 0.95, ...)

title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
text: x ANOVA results oneway_anova() conf.level Confidence level intervals (Default: 0.95 = 95%) ... Additional arguments (currently unused)

title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
text: Pairwise comparison results showing: group pairs significantly different Size difference pair Adjusted p-values (extra conservative) Confidence intervals difference

title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
text: Adjusted P-values: Extra conservative prevent false positives p < 0.05: Groups significantly different (can confident) p ≥ 0.05: significant difference groups Scheffe adjustments stricter methods Confidence Intervals: Wider Tukey's include 0: Groups differ significantly Include 0: significant difference Wider intervals reflect extra caution

title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
text: Use Scheffe test : ANOVA shows significant differences (p < 0.05) Group sizes unequal want extra cautious false positives might test complex comparisons (just pairs) Sample sizes small

title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
text: Scheffe Test: conservative (hardest find differences) Best unequal group sizes Protects possible comparisons Wider confidence intervals Tukey Test: Less conservative (easier find differences) Best equal group sizes Protects pairwise comparisons Narrower confidence intervals

title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
text: Example: "Group - Group B: Diff = 3.2, p = 0.082" Group 's average 3.2 units higher Group B's difference significant Scheffe (p > 0.05) might significant less conservative tests

title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
text: Scheffe may find differences even ANOVA normal - extra careful Consider Tukey group sizes similar Report post-hoc test used Focus confidence intervals, just p-values

title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
text: Scheffe, H. (1953). method judging contrasts analysis variance. Biometrika, 40(1-2), 87-110. Scheffe, H. (1959). Analysis Variance. New York: Wiley.

title: Compare All Groups More Conservatively After ANOVA — scheffe_test
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Perform ANOVA followed by Scheffe post-hoc test anova_result <- survey_data %>%   oneway_anova(life_satisfaction, group = education)  # Scheffe post-hoc comparisons anova_result %>% scheffe_test() #> Scheffe Post-Hoc Test by education #>   life_satisfaction: 6 comparisons, 4 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table.  # Multiple variables anova_result_multi <- survey_data %>%   oneway_anova(life_satisfaction, income, group = education)  anova_result_multi %>% scheffe_test() #> Scheffe Post-Hoc Test by education #>   life_satisfaction: 6 comparisons, 4 significant (p < .05) #>   income: 6 comparisons, 6 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table.  # Weighted analysis anova_weighted <- survey_data %>%   oneway_anova(life_satisfaction, group = education, weights = sampling_weight)  anova_weighted %>% scheffe_test() #> Scheffe Post-Hoc Test by education [Weighted] #>   life_satisfaction: 6 comparisons, 4 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table.  # Grouped analysis anova_grouped <- survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   oneway_anova(life_satisfaction, group = education)  anova_grouped %>% scheffe_test() #> Scheffe Post-Hoc Test by education #> [region = East] #>   life_satisfaction: 6 comparisons, 2 significant (p < .05) #> [region = West] #>   life_satisfaction: 6 comparisons, 4 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table.  # Custom confidence level (99%) anova_result %>% scheffe_test(conf.level = 0.99) #> Scheffe Post-Hoc Test by education #>   life_satisfaction: 6 comparisons, 4 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table.

title: Declare Values as Missing — set_na
text: Replaces specific numeric values NA (tagged NAs) across one variables. essential data cleaning workflows missing value codes (e.g., -9, -8, 99) stored regular values need declared missing import.

title: Declare Values as Missing — set_na
code: set_na(data, ..., tag = TRUE, verbose = FALSE)

title: Declare Values as Missing — set_na
text: data data frame, tibble, single vector. ... Values set missing. Can : Unnamed numeric values: Applied numeric columns (e.g., set_na(data, -9, -8)) Named pairs: Applied specific variables (e.g., set_na(data, income = c(-9, -8), age = -1)) tag TRUE (default), uses tagged NAs preserve distinct missing types. resulting tagged NAs integrate na_frequencies(), frequency(), codebook(). FALSE, replaces regular NA. verbose TRUE, prints summary conversions.

title: Declare Values as Missing — set_na
text: modified data (invisibly data frames, visibly vectors).

title: Declare Values as Missing — set_na
text: tag = TRUE (default), missing value code gets unique tag character, can distinguish "answer" (-9) "applicable" (-8) downstream analysis. system used read_spss() tag_na = TRUE. tag = FALSE, specified values become regular NA distinction different missing types lost.

title: Declare Values as Missing — set_na
text: value set missing existing value label, label preserved tagged NA label (tag = TRUE), making visible frequency() codebook() output.

title: Declare Values as Missing — set_na
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Set -9 and -8 as missing across all numeric variables data <- set_na(survey_data, -9, -8)  # Set missing for specific variables only data <- set_na(survey_data,   income = c(-9, -8, -42),   life_satisfaction = c(-9, -11) )  # Use regular NA instead of tagged NA data <- set_na(survey_data, -9, -8, tag = FALSE)  # Check the result na_frequencies(data$income) } # }

title: Spearman's Rank Correlation Analysis — spearman_rho
text: Calculates Spearman's rank correlation coefficients (rho) variables support weighted correlations, grouped data, multiple variable pairs. Provides significance testing SPSS-compatible output formatting. Spearman's rho non-parametric measure rank correlation assesses monotonic relationships variables. particularly suitable ordinal data assumptions Pearson correlation met.

title: Spearman's Rank Correlation Analysis — spearman_rho
code: spearman_rho(   data,   ...,   weights = NULL,   alternative = c("two.sided", "less", "greater"),   use = c("pairwise", "listwise"),   na.rm = NULL )

title: Spearman's Rank Correlation Analysis — spearman_rho
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... variables want correlate. List two single correlation correlation matrix. can use helpers like starts_with("trust"). weights Optional survey weights. Following SPSS NONPAR CORR convention, weights used filter cases (rows weight <= 0 NA dropped); rank correlation computed unweighted remaining sample. design-based weighted Spearman use survey::svyolr() wCorr::weightedCorr(method = "Spearman"). alternative Direction test: "two.sided" (default): Two-tailed test "less": One-tailed test (negative correlation) "greater": One-tailed test (positive correlation) use handle missing values: "pairwise" (default): Pairwise deletion - correlation uses available cases "listwise": Listwise deletion - complete cases across variables na.rm Deprecated. Use use instead.

title: Spearman's Rank Correlation Analysis — spearman_rho
text: Correlation results showing rank-based relationships variables, including rho coefficient, p-value, t-statistic, sample size pair. multiple variables, correlation, significance, sample size matrices also provided. Use summary() full SPSS-style output toggleable sections.

title: Spearman's Rank Correlation Analysis — spearman_rho
text: Spearman's rho measures strength direction monotonic relationship two variables. Unlike Pearson correlation, require linear relationship – just needs one variable consistently increase (decrease) increases. Think ranking data first, checking ranks tend go together. rho value ranges -1 +1: Strong positive (0.7 1.0): High ranks one variable go high ranks Moderate positive (0.3 0.7): Moderate tendency ranks increase together Weak positive (0 0.3): Slight tendency ranks increase together correlation (near 0): relationship variables Negative values: one variable's rank increases, 's tends decrease output also provides: p-value: Probability seeing correlation chance n: Number observation pairs used significance stars: Visual indicator (*** strong, ** strong, * moderate evidence)

title: Spearman's Rank Correlation Analysis — spearman_rho
text: Choose Spearman's rho : relationship monotonic necessarily linear data outliers (less impact ranks) variables ordinal (ordered categories) sure data meets Pearson correlation assumptions want detect monotonic trend, just linear ones

title: Spearman's Rank Correlation Analysis — spearman_rho
text: Spearman's rho usually larger magnitude Kendall's tau Spearman's rho better detecting linear relationships ranks Kendall's tau robust better statistical properties Spearman's rho commonly reported research

title: Spearman's Rank Correlation Analysis — spearman_rho
text: Spearman, C. (1904). proof measurement association two things. American Journal Psychology, 15(1), 72–101. Lehmann, E. L. (1975). Nonparametrics: Statistical Methods Based Ranks. Holden-Day.

title: Spearman's Rank Correlation Analysis — spearman_rho
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Basic correlation between two variables survey_data %>%   spearman_rho(life_satisfaction, political_orientation) #> Spearman Correlation: life_satisfaction x political_orientation #>   rho = -0.004, p = 0.833 , N = 2228  # Correlation matrix for multiple variables survey_data %>%   spearman_rho(life_satisfaction, political_orientation, trust_media) #> Spearman Correlation: 3 variables #>   life_satisfaction x political_orientation: rho = -0.004, p = 0.833   #>   life_satisfaction x trust_media: rho = 0.028, p = 0.181   #>   political_orientation x trust_media: rho = 0.003, p = 0.885   #>   0/3 pairs significant (p < .05), N = 2228  # Weighted correlations (mathematically correct, though SPSS may not apply weights) survey_data %>%   spearman_rho(age, income, weights = sampling_weight) #> Spearman Correlation: age x income [Weighted] #>   rho = 0.003, p = 0.870 , N = 2186  # Grouped correlations survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   spearman_rho(age, income, life_satisfaction) #> [region = 1] #> Spearman Correlation: 3 variables #>   age x income:                  rho = 0.058, p = 0.234   #>   age x life_satisfaction:       rho = -0.040, p = 0.391   #>   income x life_satisfaction:    rho = 0.440, p < 0.001 ***  #>   1/3 pairs significant (p < .05), N = 429 #> [region = 2] #> Spearman Correlation: 3 variables #>   age x income:                  rho = -0.008, p = 0.725   #>   age x life_satisfaction:       rho = -0.020, p = 0.382   #>   income x life_satisfaction:    rho = 0.470, p < 0.001 ***  #>   1/3 pairs significant (p < .05), N = 1757  # Using tidyselect helpers survey_data %>%   spearman_rho(starts_with("trust"), weights = sampling_weight) #> Spearman Correlation: 3 variables [Weighted] #>   trust_government x trust_media: rho = 0.008, p = 0.723   #>   trust_government x trust_science: rho = 0.027, p = 0.207   #>   trust_media x trust_science:   rho = 0.016, p = 0.453   #>   0/3 pairs significant (p < .05), N = 2227  # Listwise deletion for missing data survey_data %>%   spearman_rho(age, income, use = "listwise") #> Spearman Correlation: age x income #>   rho = 0.003, p = 0.870 , N = 2186  # One-tailed test survey_data %>%   spearman_rho(age, income, alternative = "greater") #> Spearman Correlation: age x income #>   rho = 0.003, p = 0.435 , N = 2186  # --- Three-layer output --- result <- spearman_rho(survey_data, age, income, life_satisfaction) result              # compact one-line overview #> Spearman Correlation: 3 variables #>   age x income:                  rho = 0.003, p = 0.870   #>   age x life_satisfaction:       rho = -0.024, p = 0.238   #>   income x life_satisfaction:    rho = 0.464, p < 0.001 ***  #>   1/3 pairs significant (p < .05), N = 2186 summary(result)     # full correlation, p-value, and N matrices #>  #> Spearman's Rank Correlation Analysis  #> ------------------------------------- #>  #> - Method: Spearman's rho (rank correlation) #> - Variables: age, income, life_satisfaction #> - Missing data handling: pairwise deletion #> - Alternative hypothesis: two.sided #>  #>  #> Spearman's Rho Matrix: #> ----------------------  #>                       age  income life_satisfaction #> age                 1.000   0.003            -0.024 #> income              0.003   1.000             0.464 #> life_satisfaction  -0.024   0.464             1.000 #> ----------------------  #>  #> Significance Matrix (p-values, 2-tailed): #> -----------------------------------------  #>                       age  income life_satisfaction #> age                0.0000  0.8703            0.2383 #> income             0.8703  0.0000            0.0000 #> life_satisfaction  0.2383  0.0000            0.0000 #> -----------------------------------------  #>  #> Sample Size Matrix: #> -------------------  #>                     age income life_satisfaction #> age                2500   2186              2421 #> income             2186   2186              2115 #> life_satisfaction  2421   2115              2421 #> -------------------  #>  #> Pairwise Results: #> ----------------  #>                        Pair    rho      t      p    n sig #>                age × income  0.003  0.163 0.8703 2186     #>     age × life_satisfaction -0.024 -1.180 0.2383 2421     #>  income × life_satisfaction  0.464 24.073 0.0000 2115 *** #> ----------------  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 summary(result, pvalue_matrix = FALSE)  # hide p-values #>  #> Spearman's Rank Correlation Analysis  #> ------------------------------------- #>  #> - Method: Spearman's rho (rank correlation) #> - Variables: age, income, life_satisfaction #> - Missing data handling: pairwise deletion #> - Alternative hypothesis: two.sided #>  #>  #> Spearman's Rho Matrix: #> ----------------------  #>                       age  income life_satisfaction #> age                 1.000   0.003            -0.024 #> income              0.003   1.000             0.464 #> life_satisfaction  -0.024   0.464             1.000 #> ----------------------  #>  #> Sample Size Matrix: #> -------------------  #>                     age income life_satisfaction #> age                2500   2186              2421 #> income             2186   2186              2115 #> life_satisfaction  2421   2115              2421 #> -------------------  #>  #> Pairwise Results: #> ----------------  #>                        Pair    rho      t      p    n sig #>                age × income  0.003  0.163 0.8703 2186     #>     age × life_satisfaction -0.024 -1.180 0.2383 2421     #>  income × life_satisfaction  0.464 24.073 0.0000 2115 *** #> ----------------  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05

title: Standardize Variables (Z-Scores) — std
text: Standardizes variables centering mean dividing measure spread. Supports multiple standardization methods including robust alternatives. used grouped data frame (via dplyr::group_by()), standardization performed within group.

title: Standardize Variables (Z-Scores) — std
code: std(data, ..., method = "sd", weights = NULL, suffix = NULL, na.rm = TRUE)

title: Standardize Variables (Z-Scores) — std
text: data data frame numeric vector. ... Variables standardize (tidyselect). used data data frame. method Standardization method: "sd" Standard z-score: (x - mean) / sd (default) "2sd" Gelman's 2-SD method: (x - mean) / (2 * sd). Useful regression binary predictors. "mad" Robust: (x - median) / mad. Resistant outliers. "gmd" Gini's Mean Difference: (x - mean) / gmd. robust alternative. weights Optional survey weights (unquoted column name numeric vector). provided, weighted mean weighted SD used standardization. supported methods "sd" "2sd". suffix character string appended column names (e.g., "_z"). NULL (default), original columns overwritten. na.rm Remove missing values computing mean SD? Default: TRUE.

title: Standardize Variables (Z-Scores) — std
text: data vector, standardized numeric vector. data data frame, modified data frame (invisibly).

title: Standardize Variables (Z-Scores) — std
text: sd (default): Standard z-transformation. Mean = 0, SD = 1. 2sd: Divides 2 standard deviations (Gelman, 2008). makes standardized continuous predictors comparable binary predictors regression. mad: Uses Median Absolute Deviation instead SD. Robust outliers. gmd: Uses Gini's Mean Difference — robust spread measure based pairwise absolute differences.

title: Standardize Variables (Z-Scores) — std
text: weights provided, weighted mean weighted standard deviation (using SPSS frequency weight formula) used. supported methods "sd" "2sd". robust methods "mad" "gmd" support weights.

title: Standardize Variables (Z-Scores) — std
text: data grouped (via group_by()), standardization performed separately within group. useful within-group comparisons. Weights also subsetted per group.

title: Standardize Variables (Z-Scores) — std
code: library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Standard z-scores data <- std(survey_data, age, income, suffix = "_z")  # Gelman 2-SD standardization (for regression) data <- std(survey_data, income, age, method = "2sd",             suffix = "_z")  # Robust standardization data <- std(survey_data, income, method = "mad", suffix = "_z")  # Weighted standardization data <- std(survey_data, income, age,             weights = sampling_weight, suffix = "_z")  # Group-wise standardization data <- survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   std(income, suffix = "_z")

Academic Secondary University #>   Male               401                  289                320        184 #>   Female             440                  340                311        215 #>  #> Expected Frequencies: #>         education #> gender   Basic Secondary Intermediate Seco... Academic Secondary University #>   Male           401.662               300.41            301.366    190.562 #>   Female         439.338               328.59            329.634    208.438 #>  #> Chi-Squared Test Results: #> --------------------------------------------------  #>  Chi_squared df p_value sig #>         3.47  3   0.325     #> --------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Effect Sizes: #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------  #>     Measure  Value p_value sig Interpretation #>  Cramer's V  0.037   0.325            Neglig. #>       Gamma -0.008   0.850               Weak #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------  #> Table size: 2×4 | N = 2500 #> Note: Phi coefficient only shown for 2x2 tables #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 summary(result, expected = FALSE) #>  #> Chi-Squared Test of Independence  #> --------------------------------- #>  #> - Variables: gender × education #>  #> Observed Frequencies: #>         education #> gender   Basic Secondary Intermediate Seco... title: Summary method for Dunn post-hoc test results — summary.dunn_test
text: summary.dunn_test object.

title: Summary method for Dunn post-hoc test results — summary.dunn_test
code: result <- kruskal_wallis(survey_data, life_satisfaction,                          group = education) |> dunn_test() summary(result) #> Dunn Post-Hoc Test (Bonferroni) Results #> --------------------------------------- #>  #> - Dependent variable: life_satisfaction #> - Grouping variable: education #> - P-value adjustment: Bonferroni #>  #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>                 Group 1                Group 2       Z p (unadj) p (adj) Sig  #>         Basic Secondary Intermediate Secondary  -7.658     <.001   <.001 ***  #>         Basic Secondary     Academic Secondary  -9.792     <.001   <.001 ***  #>         Basic Secondary             University -11.545     <.001   <.001 ***  #>  Intermediate Secondary     Academic Secondary  -2.042     0.041   0.247      #>  Intermediate Secondary             University  -4.696     <.001   <.001 ***  #>      Academic Secondary             University  -2.886     0.004   0.023   *  #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - Positive Z: First group has higher mean rank #> - Negative Z: First group has lower mean rank #> - p-values are adjusted for multiple comparisons summary(result, interpretation = FALSE) #> Dunn Post-Hoc Test (Bonferroni) Results #> --------------------------------------- #>  #> - Dependent variable: life_satisfaction #> - Grouping variable: education #> - P-value adjustment: Bonferroni #>  #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>                 Group 1                Group 2       Z p (unadj) p (adj) Sig  #>         Basic Secondary Intermediate Secondary  -7.658     <.001   <.001 ***  #>         Basic Secondary     Academic Secondary  -9.792     <.001   <.001 ***  #>         Basic Secondary             University -11.545     <.001   <.001 ***  #>  Intermediate Secondary     Academic Secondary  -2.042     0.041   0.247      #>  Intermediate Secondary             University  -4.696     <.001   <.001 ***  #>      Academic Secondary             University  -2.886     0.004   0.023   *  #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05

what: Summarize an exploratory factor analysis — summary.efa
title: Summarize an exploratory factor analysis — summary.efa
text: Creates detailed summary EFA result. sections shown default; set individual toggles FALSE suppress specific sections.

title: Summarize an exploratory factor analysis — summary.efa
code: # S3 method for class 'efa' summary(   object,   kmo_bartlett = TRUE,   communalities = TRUE,   variance_explained = TRUE,   unrotated_matrix = TRUE,   rotated_matrix = TRUE,   pattern_matrix = TRUE,   structure_matrix = TRUE,   factor_correlations = TRUE,   digits = 3,   ... )

title: Summarize an exploratory factor analysis — summary.efa
text: object efa result object kmo_bartlett Show KMO Bartlett's test? (Default: TRUE) communalities Show communalities table? (Default: TRUE) variance_explained Show total variance explained? (Default: TRUE) unrotated_matrix Show unrotated component/factor matrix? (Default: TRUE) rotated_matrix Show rotated matrix (varimax)? (Default: TRUE) pattern_matrix Show pattern matrix (oblimin/promax)? (Default: TRUE) structure_matrix Show structure matrix (oblimin/promax)? (Default: TRUE) factor_correlations Show factor correlation matrix (oblimin/promax)? (Default: TRUE) digits Number decimal places (Default: 3) ... Additional arguments (ignored)

title: Summarize an exploratory factor analysis — summary.efa
text: summary.efa object (list $show toggles)

code: result <- friedman_test(survey_data, trust_government, trust_media,                         trust_science) summary(result) #> Friedman Test Results #> --------------------- #>  #> - Variables: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - Number of conditions: 3 #>  #>   Ranks: #>   --------------------------- #>            Variable Mean Rank #>    trust_government      1.81 #>         trust_media      1.68 #>       trust_science      2.51 #>   --------------------------- #>  #>   Test Statistics: #>   ------------------------------------------- #>       N Chi-Square df p value Kendall's W sig #>    2135   1009.035  2       0       0.236 *** #>   ------------------------------------------- #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Effect Size Interpretation (Kendall's W): #> - Weak agreement: 0.1 - 0.3 #> - Moderate agreement: 0.3 - 0.5 #> - Strong agreement: > 0.5 summary(result, ranks = FALSE) #> Friedman Test Results #> --------------------- #>  #> - Variables: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - Number of conditions: 3 #>  #>   Test Statistics: #>   ------------------------------------------- #>       N Chi-Square df p value Kendall's W sig #>    2135   1009.035  2       0       0.236 *** #>   ------------------------------------------- #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Effect Size Interpretation (Kendall's W): #> - Weak agreement: 0.1 - 0.3 #> - Moderate agreement: 0.3 - 0.5 #> - Strong agreement: > 0.5

code: result <- mann_whitney(survey_data, life_satisfaction, group = gender) summary(result) #> Mann-Whitney U Test Results #> --------------------------- #>  #> - Grouping variable: gender #> - Groups compared: Male vs. Female #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #> - Alternative hypothesis: two.sided #> - Null hypothesis (mu): 0.000 #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #>   Male: rank mean = 1196.7, n = 1149.0 #>   Female: rank mean = 1223.9, n = 1272.0 #>  #>  #> Mann-Whitney U Test Results: #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>            Test       U         W      Z p_value effect_r sig #>  Mann-Whitney U 714,347 1,375,022 -0.989   0.323     0.02     #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Effect Size Interpretation (r): #> - Negligible effect: |r| < 0.1 #> - Small effect: |r| ~ 0.1 #> - Medium effect: |r| ~ 0.3 #> - Large effect: |r| ~ 0.5 summary(result, effect_sizes = FALSE) #> Mann-Whitney U Test Results #> --------------------------- #>  #> - Grouping variable: gender #> - Groups compared: Male vs. Female #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #> - Alternative hypothesis: two.sided #> - Null hypothesis (mu): 0.000 #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #>   Male: rank mean = 1196.7, n = 1149.0 #>   Female: rank mean = 1223.9, n = 1272.0 #>  #>  #> Mann-Whitney U Test Results: #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>            Test       U         W      Z p_value effect_r sig #>  Mann-Whitney U 714,347 1,375,022 -0.989   0.323     0.02     #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05

code: test_data <- transform(survey_data,   trust_gov_high = as.integer(trust_government >= 4),   trust_media_high = as.integer(trust_media >= 4)) result <- mcnemar_test(test_data, var1 = trust_gov_high,                        var2 = trust_media_high) summary(result) #> McNemar Test Results #> -------------------- #>  #> - Variable 1: trust_gov_high #> - Variable 2: trust_media_high #>  #> 2x2 Contingency Table: #> ----------------------------------------  #>    v2 #> v1     0    1 #>   0 1342  338 #>   1  448   99 #> ----------------------------------------  #>  #> Test Results: #> -----------------------------------------  #>  Chi-Sq (cc) p (asymp) p (exact)    N Sig  #>       15.116     <.001     <.001 2227 ***  #> -----------------------------------------  #>  #> Discordant pairs: b = 338, c = 448 #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 summary(result, contingency_table = FALSE) #> McNemar Test Results #> -------------------- #>  #> - Variable 1: trust_gov_high #> - Variable 2: trust_media_high #>  #> Test Results: #> -----------------------------------------  #>  Chi-Sq (cc) p (asymp) p (exact)    N Sig  #>       15.116     <.001     <.001 2227 ***  #> -----------------------------------------  #>  #> Discordant pairs: b = 338, c = 448 #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05

title: Summary method for one-way ANOVA results — summary.oneway_anova
code: result <- oneway_anova(survey_data, life_satisfaction, group = education) summary(result) #> One-Way ANOVA Results #> --------------------- #>  #> - Dependent variable: life_satisfaction #> - Grouping variable: education #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #>   Null hypothesis: All group means are equal #>   Alternative hypothesis: At least one group mean differs #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #> Descriptive Statistics by Group: #>   Basic Secondary: mean = 3.204, sd = 1.243, n = 809 #>   Intermediate Secondary: mean = 3.701, sd = 1.112, n = 618 #>   Academic Secondary: mean = 3.853, sd = 0.998, n = 607 #>   University: mean = 4.047, sd = 0.957, n = 387 #>  #> ANOVA Results: #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>          Source Sum_Squares   df Mean_Square      F p_value sig #>  Between Groups     247.347    3      82.449 67.096   <.001 *** #>   Within Groups    2970.080 2417       1.229                    #>           Total    3217.428 2420                                #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Assumption Tests: #> ----------------  #>  Assumption Statistic df1  df2 p_value sig #>       Welch    64.489   3 1229   <.001 *** #>  #> Effect Sizes: #> ------------  #>           Variable Eta_Squared Epsilon_Squared Omega_Squared Effect_Size #>  life_satisfaction       0.077           0.076         0.076      medium #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Effect Size Interpretation: #> - Eta-squared: Proportion of variance explained (biased upward) #> - Epsilon-squared: Less biased than eta-squared #> - Omega-squared: Unbiased estimate (preferred for publication) #> - Small effect: eta-squared ~ 0.01, Medium effect: eta-squared ~ 0.06, Large effect: eta-squared ~ 0.14 #>  #> Post-hoc tests: Use tukey_test() for pairwise comparisons summary(result, effect_sizes = FALSE) #> One-Way ANOVA Results #> --------------------- #>  #> - Dependent variable: life_satisfaction #> - Grouping variable: education #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #>   Null hypothesis: All group means are equal #>   Alternative hypothesis: At least one group mean differs #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #> Descriptive Statistics by Group: #>   Basic Secondary: mean = 3.204, sd = 1.243, n = 809 #>   Intermediate Secondary: mean = 3.701, sd = 1.112, n = 618 #>   Academic Secondary: mean = 3.853, sd = 0.998, n = 607 #>   University: mean = 4.047, sd = 0.957, n = 387 #>  #> ANOVA Results: #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>          Source Sum_Squares   df Mean_Square      F p_value sig #>  Between Groups     247.347    3      82.449 67.096   <.001 *** #>   Within Groups    2970.080 2417       1.229                    #>           Total    3217.428 2420                                #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Assumption Tests: #> ----------------  #>  Assumption Statistic df1  df2 p_value sig #>       Welch    64.489   3 1229   <.001 *** #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Post-hoc tests: Use tukey_test() for pairwise comparisons

code: result <- friedman_test(survey_data, trust_government, trust_media,                         trust_science) |> pairwise_wilcoxon() summary(result) #> Pairwise Wilcoxon Post-Hoc Test (Bonferroni) Results #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>  #> - Variables: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - P-value adjustment: Bonferroni #> - Number of comparisons: 3 #>  #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>             Var 1         Var 2      Z p (unadj) p (adj) Sig  #>  trust_government   trust_media -5.097     <.001   <.001 ***  #>  trust_government trust_science 25.945     <.001   <.001 ***  #>       trust_media trust_science 29.091     <.001   <.001 ***  #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - Positive Z: First variable tends to have higher values #> - Negative Z: Second variable tends to have higher values #> - p-values are adjusted for multiple comparisons summary(result, interpretation = FALSE) #> Pairwise Wilcoxon Post-Hoc Test (Bonferroni) Results #> ---------------------------------------------------- #>  #> - Variables: trust_government, trust_media, trust_science #> - P-value adjustment: Bonferroni #> - Number of comparisons: 3 #>  #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>             Var 1         Var 2      Z p (unadj) p (adj) Sig  #>  trust_government   trust_media -5.097     <.001   <.001 ***  #>  trust_government trust_science 25.945     <.001   <.001 ***  #>       trust_media trust_science 29.091     <.001   <.001 ***  #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05

Intermediate Secondary         -0.497    -0.662    -0.331    <.001  ***  #>   Basic Secondary - Academic Secondary             -0.649    -0.816    -0.483    <.001  ***  #>   Basic Secondary - University                     -0.843    -1.034    -0.651    <.001  ***  #>   Intermediate Secondary - Academic Secondary      -0.153    -0.330     0.024     .121       #>   Intermediate Secondary - University              -0.346    -0.547    -0.145    <.001  ***  #>   Academic Secondary - University                  -0.193    -0.395     0.009     .067       #>   -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - Positive differences: First group > Second group #> - Negative differences: First group < Second group #> - Confidence intervals not containing 0 indicate significant differences #> - p-values are adjusted for all possible contrasts (most conservative) #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #> - Alternative hypothesis: two.sided #> - Null hypothesis (mu): 0.000 #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #>   Male: mean = 3.603, n = 1149.0 #>   Female: mean = 3.651, n = 1272.0 #>  #> t-test Results: #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>         Assumption t_stat       df p_value mean_diff        conf_int sig #>    Equal variances -1.019 2419.000   0.308    -0.048 [-0.140, 0.044]     #>  Unequal variances -1.018 2384.147   0.309    -0.048 [-0.140, 0.044]     #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Effect Sizes: #> ------------  #>           Variable Cohens_d Hedges_g Glass_Delta Effect_Size #>  life_satisfaction   -0.041   -0.041      -0.041  negligible #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Effect Size Interpretation: #> - Cohen's d: pooled standard deviation (classic) #> - Hedges' g: bias-corrected Cohen's d (preferred) #> - Glass' Delta: control group standard deviation only #> - Small effect: |effect| ~ 0.2 #> - Medium effect: |effect| ~ 0.5 #> - Large effect: |effect| ~ 0.8 summary(result, effect_sizes = FALSE) #> t-Test Results #> -------------- #>  #> - Grouping variable: gender #> - Groups compared: Male vs. Female #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #> - Alternative hypothesis: two.sided #> - Null hypothesis (mu): 0.000 #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #>   Male: mean = 3.603, n = 1149.0 #>   Female: mean = 3.651, n = 1272.0 #>  #> t-test Results: #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>         Assumption t_stat       df p_value mean_diff        conf_int sig #>    Equal variances -1.019 2419.000   0.308    -0.048 [-0.140, 0.044]     #>  Unequal variances -1.018 2384.147   0.309    -0.048 [-0.140, 0.044]     #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05

code: result <- oneway_anova(survey_data, life_satisfaction,                        group = education) |> tukey_test() summary(result) #> Tukey HSD Post-Hoc Test Results #> ------------------------------- #>  #> - Dependent variable: life_satisfaction #> - Grouping variable: education #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #>   Family-wise error rate controlled using Tukey HSD #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #> Tukey Results: #>   ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>   Comparison                                 Difference  Lower CI  Upper CI  p-value  Sig  #>   ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>   Intermediate Secondary-Basic Secondary          0.497     0.344     0.649    <.001  ***  #>   Academic Secondary-Basic Secondary              0.649     0.496     0.802    <.001  ***  #>   University-Basic Secondary                      0.843     0.666     1.019    <.001  ***  #>   Academic Secondary-Intermediate Secondary       0.153    -0.010     0.316     .075       #>   University-Intermediate Secondary               0.346     0.161     0.531    <.001  ***  #>   University-Academic Secondary                   0.193     0.008     0.379     .037    *  #>   ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Interpretation: #> - Positive differences: First group > Second group #> - Negative differences: First group < Second group #> - Confidence intervals not containing 0 indicate significant differences #> - p-values are adjusted for multiple comparisons (family-wise error control) summary(result, interpretation = FALSE) #> Tukey HSD Post-Hoc Test Results #> ------------------------------- #>  #> - Dependent variable: life_satisfaction #> - Grouping variable: education #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #>   Family-wise error rate controlled using Tukey HSD #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #> Tukey Results: #>   ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>   Comparison                                 Difference  Lower CI  Upper CI  p-value  Sig  #>   ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>   Intermediate Secondary-Basic Secondary          0.497     0.344     0.649    <.001  ***  #>   Academic Secondary-Basic Secondary              0.649     0.496     0.802    <.001  ***  #>   University-Basic Secondary                      0.843     0.666     1.019    <.001  ***  #>   Academic Secondary-Intermediate Secondary       0.153    -0.010     0.316     .075       #>   University-Intermediate Secondary               0.346     0.161     0.531    <.001  ***  #>   University-Academic Secondary                   0.193     0.008     0.379     .037    *  #>   ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05

title: Social Survey Data (Synthetic) — survey_data
text: synthetic dataset modeled large-scale social surveys demographics, attitudes, survey design features. Created testing demonstration purposes without licensing concerns.

title: Social Survey Data (Synthetic) — survey_data
code: survey_data

title: Social Survey Data (Synthetic) — survey_data
text: data frame 2,500 rows 16 variables: id Unique identifier (1-2500) age Age years (18-95) gender Gender (Male, Female) region Region (East, West) education Education level (Basic Secondary, Intermediate Secondary, Academic Secondary, University) income Monthly household income EUR (800-15000) employment Employment status (Student, Employed, Unemployed, Retired, ) political_orientation Political orientation (1=left 5=right) environmental_concern Environmental concern (1=concerned 5=concerned) life_satisfaction Life satisfaction (1=dissatisfied 5=satisfied) trust_government Trust government (1=trust 5=complete trust) trust_media Trust media (1=trust 5=complete trust) trust_science Trust science (1=trust 5=complete trust) sampling_weight Post-stratification sampling weight (0.7-1.4) stratum Stratification variable combining region age group interview_mode Interview mode (Face--face, Telephone, Online)

title: Social Survey Data (Synthetic) — survey_data
text: Generated synthetically using realistic demographic attitudinal patterns. real survey data used.

title: Social Survey Data (Synthetic) — survey_data
text: dataset contains realistic patterns correlation variables: Education correlates income political attitudes Regional differences reflect East/West patterns Age effects employment attitudes Realistic missing data patterns (3-12% depending sensitivity) Survey weights post-stratification adjustment data includes proper variable labels follows social survey conventions coding structure. Generated set.seed(2024) reproducibility.

title: Social Survey Data (Synthetic) — survey_data
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Basic descriptive statistics survey_data %>% describe(age, income, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Descriptive Statistics #> ------------------------------- #>  Variable     Mean Median       SD Range  IQR Skewness Effective_N #>       age   50.514     50   17.084    77   25    0.159      2468.8 #>    income 3743.099   3500 1423.966  7200 1900    0.725      2158.9 #> ----------------------------------------  # Frequency analysis survey_data %>% frequency(education, region, weights = sampling_weight) #> Frequency: education [Weighted] #>   4 categories, N valid = 2516, missing = 0 #> Frequency: region [Weighted] #>   2 categories, N valid = 2516, missing = 0 #> Use summary() for detailed output.  # Group comparisons survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   t_test(life_satisfaction, group = gender, weights = sampling_weight) #> [region = 1] #> t-Test: life_satisfaction by gender [Weighted] #>   t(484.7) = 0.641, p = 0.522 , g = 0.058 (negligible), N = 488 #> [region = 2] #> t-Test: life_satisfaction by gender [Weighted] #>   t(1901.1) = -1.548, p = 0.122 , g = -0.070 (negligible), N = 1949

what: Test If Two Groups Differ — t_test
title: Test If Two Groups Differ — t_test
text: t_test() helps determine two groups different average values. example, men women different satisfaction scores? Region spend Region B? year better last year? test tells : Whether difference statistically significant big difference (effect sizes) likely range true difference (confidence interval)

title: Test If Two Groups Differ — t_test
code: t_test(   data,   ...,   group = NULL,   weights = NULL,   var.equal = FALSE,   mu = 0,   alternative = c("two.sided", "less", "greater"),   conf.level = 0.95 )

title: Test If Two Groups Differ — t_test
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... numeric variables test. List multiple variables use tidyselect helpers like starts_with("trust"). group variable defines two groups (e.g., gender, region). Must exactly two categories. Leave empty one-sample tests. weights Optional survey weights population-representative results var.equal assume equal variances? (Default: FALSE) FALSE: Safer, works even groups vary differently (Welch's test) TRUE: Traditional approach, assumes equal spread (Student's test) mu Comparison value (Default: 0). two-sample tests, usually 0. one-sample tests, benchmark value. alternative Direction test: "two.sided" (default): difference "greater": First group higher "less": First group lower conf.level Confidence level intervals (Default: 0.95 = 95%)

title: Test If Two Groups Differ — t_test
text: Test results showing whether groups differ, including: t-statistic p-value statistical significance Mean difference confidence interval Effect sizes (Cohen's d, Hedges' g, Glass' Delta) Group statistics (mean, SD, sample size) Use summary() full SPSS-style output toggleable sections.

title: Test If Two Groups Differ — t_test
text: P-value: difference statistically significant? p < 0.001: strong evidence difference p < 0.01: Strong evidence difference p < 0.05: Moderate evidence difference p ≥ 0.05: significant difference found Effect Sizes (big difference?): Cohen's d: standard measure |d| < 0.2: Negligible difference |d| = 0.2-0.5: Small difference |d| = 0.5-0.8: Medium difference |d| > 0.8: Large difference Hedges' g: Corrected small samples Glass' Delta: Uses control group SD Confidence Interval: Range true difference likely falls includes 0, groups may differ Width indicates precision (narrower = precise)

title: Test If Two Groups Differ — t_test
text: Use t-test : exactly two groups compare outcome variable numeric (continuous) Groups independent (different people ) Data roughly normally distributed (n ≥ 30 per group) use : two groups (use ANOVA) Data severely skewed small samples (use Mann-Whitney) Groups paired/matched (use paired t-test - coming soon) Variables categorical (use chi-square)

title: Test If Two Groups Differ — t_test
text: results show variance assumptions: Welch's test (var.equal = FALSE): Safer, assume equal variances Student's test (var.equal = TRUE): Traditional, assumes equal variances result t = 2.45, p = 0.015, d = 0.62 means: Groups 2.45 standard errors apart (t-statistic) 1.5% chance due random variation (p-value) Medium-sized practical difference (Cohen's d)

title: Test If Two Groups Differ — t_test
text: Always check sample sizes (aim 30+ per group) Consider statistical significance effect size Use weights population-level conclusions Plot data first check assumptions Report confidence intervals along p-values

title: Test If Two Groups Differ — t_test
text: Cohen, J. (1988). Statistical Power Analysis Behavioral Sciences (2nd ed.). Lawrence Erlbaum Associates. Hedges, L. V. (1981). Distribution theory Glass's estimator effect size related estimators. Journal Educational Statistics, 6(2), 107-128. Glass, G. V. (1976). Primary, secondary, meta-analysis research. Educational Researcher, 5(10), 3-8. Welch, B. L. (1947). generalization "Student's" problem several different population variances involved. Biometrika, 34(1-2), 28-35.

title: Test If Two Groups Differ — t_test
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Basic two-sample test survey_data %>%   t_test(life_satisfaction, group = gender) #> t-Test: life_satisfaction by gender #>   t(2384.1) = -1.018, p = 0.309 , g = -0.041 (negligible), N = 2421  # With survey weights survey_data %>%   t_test(life_satisfaction, group = gender, weights = sampling_weight) #> t-Test: life_satisfaction by gender [Weighted] #>   t(2391.3) = -1.069, p = 0.285 , g = -0.043 (negligible), N = 2436  # Multiple variables survey_data %>%   t_test(age, income, life_satisfaction, group = region, weights = sampling_weight) #> t-Test: age by region [Weighted] #>   t(763.8) = 2.551, p = 0.011 *, g = 0.130 (negligible), N = 2516 #> t-Test: income by region [Weighted] #>   t(713.7) = 0.299, p = 0.765 , g = 0.016 (negligible), N = 2200 #> t-Test: life_satisfaction by region [Weighted] #>   t(721.0) = -0.031, p = 0.975 , g = -0.002 (negligible), N = 2437  # One-sample test against a benchmark survey_data %>%   t_test(life_satisfaction, mu = 5, weights = sampling_weight) #> t-Test: life_satisfaction [Weighted] #>   t(2435.6) = -58.916, p < 0.001 ***  # Grouped analysis survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   t_test(life_satisfaction, group = gender, weights = sampling_weight) #> [region = 1] #> t-Test: life_satisfaction by gender [Weighted] #>   t(484.7) = 0.641, p = 0.522 , g = 0.058 (negligible), N = 488 #> [region = 2] #> t-Test: life_satisfaction by gender [Weighted] #>   t(1901.1) = -1.548, p = 0.122 , g = -0.070 (negligible), N = 1949  # Equal variance assumption survey_data %>%   t_test(life_satisfaction, group = gender, var.equal = TRUE) #> t-Test: life_satisfaction by gender #>   t(2419.0) = -1.019, p = 0.308 , g = -0.041 (negligible), N = 2421  # One-sided test survey_data %>%   t_test(income, group = gender, alternative = "greater") #> t-Test: income by gender #>   t(2169.3) = 0.690, p = 0.245 , g = 0.030 (negligible), N = 2186  # Using tidyselect helpers survey_data %>%   t_test(starts_with("trust"), group = gender, weights = sampling_weight) #> t-Test: trust_government by gender [Weighted] #>   t(2322.8) = -0.682, p = 0.496 , g = -0.028 (negligible), N = 2371 #> t-Test: trust_media by gender [Weighted] #>   t(2350.1) = -2.196, p = 0.028 *, g = -0.090 (negligible), N = 2382 #> t-Test: trust_science by gender [Weighted] #>   t(2360.9) = -1.421, p = 0.156 , g = -0.058 (negligible), N = 2414  # Store results for further analysis result <- survey_data %>%   t_test(life_satisfaction, group = gender, weights = sampling_weight) print(result) #> t-Test: life_satisfaction by gender [Weighted] #>   t(2391.3) = -1.069, p = 0.285 , g = -0.043 (negligible), N = 2436  # --- Three-layer output --- result <- t_test(survey_data, life_satisfaction, group = gender) result              # compact one-line summary #> t-Test: life_satisfaction by gender #>   t(2384.1) = -1.018, p = 0.309 , g = -0.041 (negligible), N = 2421 summary(result)     # full detailed output with all sections #> t-Test Results #> -------------- #>  #> - Grouping variable: gender #> - Groups compared: Male vs. Female #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #> - Alternative hypothesis: two.sided #> - Null hypothesis (mu): 0.000 #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #>   Male: mean = 3.603, n = 1149.0 #>   Female: mean = 3.651, n = 1272.0 #>  #> t-test Results: #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>         Assumption t_stat       df p_value mean_diff        conf_int sig #>    Equal variances -1.019 2419.000   0.308    -0.048 [-0.140, 0.044]     #>  Unequal variances -1.018 2384.147   0.309    -0.048 [-0.140, 0.044]     #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #> Effect Sizes: #> ------------  #>           Variable Cohens_d Hedges_g Glass_Delta Effect_Size #>  life_satisfaction   -0.041   -0.041      -0.041  negligible #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 #>  #> Effect Size Interpretation: #> - Cohen's d: pooled standard deviation (classic) #> - Hedges' g: bias-corrected Cohen's d (preferred) #> - Glass' Delta: control group standard deviation only #> - Small effect: |effect| ~ 0.2 #> - Medium effect: |effect| ~ 0.5 #> - Large effect: |effect| ~ 0.8 summary(result, effect_sizes = FALSE)  # hide effect sizes #> t-Test Results #> -------------- #>  #> - Grouping variable: gender #> - Groups compared: Male vs. Female #> - Confidence level: 95.0% #> - Alternative hypothesis: two.sided #> - Null hypothesis (mu): 0.000 #>  #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>  #>   Male: mean = 3.603, n = 1149.0 #>   Female: mean = 3.651, n = 1272.0 #>  #> t-test Results: #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>         Assumption t_stat       df p_value mean_diff        conf_int sig #>    Equal variances -1.019 2419.000   0.308    -0.048 [-0.140, 0.044]     #>  Unequal variances -1.018 2384.147   0.309    -0.048 [-0.140, 0.044]     #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #>  #>  #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05

title: Convert Labelled Variables to Character — to_character

Optional: unquoted variable names (tidyselect supported). empty, converts haven_labelled columns. drop_na TRUE (default), tagged NAs become regular NA. add_non_labelled TRUE, unlabelled values included numeric string representation. Default: FALSE. drop.na, add.non.labelled Defunct dot-case argument names, removed mariposa 0.6.9. Calling function error; use snake_case equivalents instead. (formals retained old names error clearly instead swallowed ....)
