title: Create Dummy Variables (One-Hot Encoding) — to_dummy
text: Creates 0/1 dummy variables categorical variables. Column names derived value labels available, making results readable SPSS-style data. Unlike model.matrix(), to_dummy() uses value labels column naming handles haven_labelled vectors correctly.

title: Create Dummy Variables (One-Hot Encoding) — to_dummy
code: to_dummy(data, ..., suffix = "val", ref = NULL, append = TRUE)

title: Create Dummy Variables (One-Hot Encoding) — to_dummy
text: data data frame vector. data frame passed, use ... select variables. ... Variables dummy-code (tidyselect). used data data frame. suffix name dummy columns: "val" (default) uses raw value (e.g., gender_1), "label" uses value label (e.g., gender_Male). ref value use reference category (omitted output). NULL (default), categories get dummy variable. Set specific value n-1 coding (e.g., regression). append TRUE (default), dummy columns appended original data frame. FALSE, dummy columns returned. Ignored data vector.

title: Create Dummy Variables (One-Hot Encoding) — to_dummy
text: append = TRUE (default), original data frame dummy columns appended. append = FALSE, tibble dummy columns. vector input, always tibble dummy columns.

title: Create Dummy Variables (One-Hot Encoding) — to_dummy
text: suffix = "val": {varname}_{value} (e.g., gender_1, gender_2). suffix = "label": {varname}_{label} labels cleaned (spaces replaced _, special characters removed).

title: Create Dummy Variables (One-Hot Encoding) — to_dummy
text: regression, typically need n-1 dummy variables. Set ref value reference category omit .

title: Create Dummy Variables (One-Hot Encoding) — to_dummy
code: library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Create dummies and append to data (default) data <- to_dummy(survey_data, gender)  # Use labels for column names data <- to_dummy(survey_data, gender, suffix = "label")  # n-1 dummies with reference category data <- to_dummy(survey_data, gender, ref = 1)  # Multiple variables data <- to_dummy(survey_data, gender, education, suffix = "label")  # Return only the dummy columns (without original data) dummies <- to_dummy(survey_data, gender, append = FALSE)

what: Convert Labelled Variables to Factors — to_label
title: Convert Labelled Variables to Factors — to_label
text: Converts haven_labelled variables factors, using value labels factor levels. primary function making labelled survey data ready plotting, cross-tabulation, regression analysis.

title: Convert Labelled Variables to Factors — to_label
code: to_label(   data,   ...,   ordered = FALSE,   drop_na = TRUE,   drop_unused = FALSE,   add_non_labelled = FALSE,   drop.na = NULL,   drop.unused = NULL,   add.non.labelled = NULL )

title: Convert Labelled Variables to Factors — to_label
text: data data frame, tibble, single vector. ... Optional: unquoted variable names (tidyselect supported). empty, converts haven_labelled columns. ordered TRUE, creates ordered factor. Default: FALSE. drop_na TRUE (default), tagged NAs converted regular NA (excluded factor levels). FALSE, tagged NAs kept factor levels label text. drop_unused TRUE, removes factor levels zero observations. Default: FALSE. add_non_labelled TRUE, values without labels included factor levels using numeric value level name. Default: FALSE (unlabelled values become NA). drop.na, drop.unused, add.non.labelled Defunct dot-case argument names, removed mariposa 0.6.9. Calling function error; use snake_case equivalents instead. (formals retained old names error clearly instead swallowed ....)

title: Convert Labelled Variables to Factors — to_label
text: input labelled variables converted factors. single vector input, returns factor.

title: Convert Labelled Variables to Factors — to_label
text: labelled variable, numeric codes replaced associated value labels. resulting factor levels ordered original numeric values (alphabetically).

title: Convert Labelled Variables to Factors — to_label
text: Use to_label() : Want human-readable labels plots (ggplot2) Need factor variables regression models Want create frequency tables label text

what: Convert Variables to Labelled Format — to_labelled
title: Convert Variables to Labelled Format — to_labelled
text: Converts factors, character vectors, plain numeric vectors haven_labelled class, optionally assigning value labels variable label. reverse to_label().

title: Convert Variables to Labelled Format — to_labelled
code: to_labelled(data, ..., labels = NULL, label = NULL)

title: Convert Variables to Labelled Format — to_labelled
text: data data frame, tibble, single vector. ... Optional: unquoted variable names (tidyselect supported). empty data frame, converts factor columns. labels Optional: named numeric vector value labels assign. NULL input factor, factor levels used labels. label Optional: variable label string.

title: Convert Variables to Labelled Format — to_labelled
text: input variables converted haven_labelled. single vector input, returns haven_labelled vector.

title: Convert Variables to Labelled Format — to_labelled
text: converting factor, integer codes (1, 2, 3, ...) become numeric values factor levels become value labels.

title: Convert Variables to Labelled Format — to_labelled
text: Character vectors converted numeric (sequential integers) unique character values labels.

title: Convert Variables to Labelled Format — to_labelled
code: # Factor → haven_labelled x <- factor(c("Male", "Female", "Male")) to_labelled(x) #> <labelled<double>[3]> #> [1] 2 1 2 #>  #> Labels: #>  value  label #>      1 Female #>      2   Male  # Numeric with custom labels to_labelled(c(1, 2, 3),   labels = c("Low" = 1, "Medium" = 2, "High" = 3),   label = "Satisfaction level" ) #> <labelled<double>[3]>: Satisfaction level #> [1] 1 2 3 #>  #> Labels: #>  value  label #>      1    Low #>      2 Medium #>      3   High  # All factors in a data frame data <- to_labelled(survey_data)

what: Convert Factors or Labelled Variables to Numeric — to_numeric
title: Convert Factors or Labelled Variables to Numeric — to_numeric
text: Converts factor levels labelled values numeric. factors, uses underlying integer codes (numeric value levels numeric strings). labelled variables, extracts underlying numeric values.

title: Convert Factors or Labelled Variables to Numeric — to_numeric
code: to_numeric(   data,   ...,   use_labels = TRUE,   start_at = NULL,   keep_labels = FALSE,   use.labels = NULL,   start.at = NULL,   keep.labels = NULL )

title: Convert Factors or Labelled Variables to Numeric — to_numeric
text: data data frame, tibble, single vector. ... Optional: unquoted variable names (tidyselect supported). empty, converts factor columns. use_labels TRUE (default), attempts use numeric value factor levels (e.g., level "3" becomes 3). FALSE, uses sequential integers (1, 2, 3, ...). start_at NULL, lowest numeric value output starts number. Default: NULL (use original values). keep_labels TRUE, former factor levels stored value labels result. Default: FALSE. use.labels, start., keep.labels Defunct dot-case argument names, removed mariposa 0.6.9. Calling function error; use snake_case equivalents instead. (formals retained old names error clearly instead swallowed ....)

title: Convert Factors or Labelled Variables to Numeric — to_numeric
text: input variables converted numeric. single vector input, returns numeric vector.

title: Convert Factors or Labelled Variables to Numeric — to_numeric
text: function handles three input types: Numeric factors (levels like "1", "2", "3"): Extracts numeric values level names. Text factors (levels like "Male", "Female"): Converts sequential integers default; use use_labels = FALSE force behavior even numeric-looking levels. haven_labelled: Extracts underlying numeric vector, stripping labelled class.

title: Convert Factors or Labelled Variables to Numeric — to_numeric

what: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
text: tukey_test() tells exactly groups different ANOVA finds overall differences. like follow-investigation pinpoints differences lie. Think : ANOVA says "differences somewhere" Tukey test says "specifically, Group differs Group C" way make possible comparisons controlling error rates

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
code: tukey_test(x, conf.level = 0.95, ...)  # Default S3 method tukey_test(x, conf.level = 0.95, ...)

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
text: x ANOVA results oneway_anova() conf.level Confidence level intervals (Default: 0.95 = 95%) ... Additional arguments (currently unused)

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
text: Pairwise comparison results showing: group pairs significantly different Size difference pair Adjusted p-values (controlling multiple comparisons) Confidence intervals difference

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
text: Adjusted P-values: Control multiple comparisons p < 0.05: Groups significantly different p ≥ 0.05: significant difference groups make many comparisons, chance alone produce false positives Tukey adjustment protects conservative Mean Differences: Positive: First group higher average second Negative: Second group higher average first Zero confidence interval: significant difference

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
text: Use Tukey test : ANOVA shows significant differences (p < 0.05) want know specific groups differ need compare possible pairs Group sizes roughly equal Variances roughly equal across groups

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
text: Tukey Test: Less conservative (easier find differences) Best equal group sizes Protects pairwise comparisons Narrower confidence intervals Scheffe Test: conservative (hardest find differences) Best unequal group sizes Protects possible comparisons Wider confidence intervals

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
text: Example: "Group - Group B: Diff = 3.2, p = 0.012" Group 's average 3.2 units higher Group B's difference statistically significant (p < 0.05) can confident groups truly differ

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
text: run post-hoc tests ANOVA significant Focus comparisons make theoretical sense Consider practical significance, just statistical Report difference confidence interval Remember: non-significant mean "exactly equal"

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
text: Tukey, J. W. (1949). Comparing individual means analysis variance. Biometrics, 5(2), 99-114. Kramer, C. Y. (1956). Extension multiple range tests group means unequal numbers replications. Biometrics, 12(3), 307-310.

title: Find Which Specific Groups Differ After ANOVA — tukey_test
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Perform ANOVA followed by Tukey post-hoc test anova_result <- survey_data %>%   oneway_anova(life_satisfaction, group = education)  # Tukey post-hoc comparisons anova_result %>% tukey_test() #> Tukey HSD Post-Hoc Test by education #>   life_satisfaction: 6 comparisons, 5 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table.  # With weights anova_weighted <- survey_data %>%   oneway_anova(life_satisfaction, group = education, weights = sampling_weight)  anova_weighted %>% tukey_test() #> Tukey HSD Post-Hoc Test by education [Weighted] #>   life_satisfaction: 6 comparisons, 5 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table.  # Multiple variables anova_multi <- survey_data %>%   oneway_anova(trust_government, trust_science, group = education)  anova_multi %>% tukey_test() #> Tukey HSD Post-Hoc Test by education #>   trust_government: 6 comparisons, 0 significant (p < .05) #>   trust_science: 6 comparisons, 0 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table.  # Grouped analysis anova_grouped <- survey_data %>%   group_by(region) %>%   oneway_anova(life_satisfaction, group = education)  anova_grouped %>% tukey_test() #> Tukey HSD Post-Hoc Test by education #> [region = East] #>   life_satisfaction: 6 comparisons, 2 significant (p < .05) #> [region = West] #>   life_satisfaction: 6 comparisons, 5 significant (p < .05) #> Use summary() for the full comparison table.

what: Get or Set Value Labels — val_labels
title: Get or Set Value Labels — val_labels
text: Retrieves assigns value labels ("labels" attribute) labelled vectors data frame columns. Value labels map numeric codes descriptive text (e.g., 1 = "Male", 2 = "Female"). function operates two modes: GET mode: Called bare variable names, returns existing value labels. SET mode: Called name = c(...) pairs, assigns value labels variables.

title: Get or Set Value Labels — val_labels
code: val_labels(data, ..., .add = FALSE, drop_na = TRUE)

title: Get or Set Value Labels — val_labels
text: data data frame, tibble, single vector. ... GET mode: unquoted variable names (tidyselect supported). SET mode: named pairs name variable value named vector labels (e.g., c("Male" = 1, "Female" = 2)). Use NULL remove value labels variable. .add TRUE, adds labels existing ones instead replacing . Default: FALSE. drop_na TRUE (default), tagged NA labels excluded GET results.

title: Get or Set Value Labels — val_labels
text: GET mode (vector input): named numeric vector value labels, NULL. GET mode (data frame, single variable): named numeric vector value labels. GET mode (data frame, multiple variables): named list label vectors. SET mode: modified data frame (invisibly).

title: Get or Set Value Labels — val_labels
text: Value labels stored "labels" attribute haven's format: named numeric vector names label text values numeric codes. standard used read_spss(), read_stata(), read_sas().

title: Get or Set Value Labels — val_labels
text: default, SET mode replaces existing value labels. Use .add = TRUE keep existing labels add new ones. value already label, new label overwrites .

title: Get or Set Value Labels — val_labels
code: # GET: retrieve value labels from a vector val_labels(survey_data$gender) #> NULL  # GET: from specific variables (returns list) val_labels(survey_data, gender, region) #> $gender #> NULL #>  #> $region #> NULL #>   # SET: assign value labels data <- val_labels(survey_data,   gender = c("Male" = 1, "Female" = 2, "Non-binary" = 3) )  # ADD: add labels without removing existing ones data <- val_labels(data,   gender = c("Prefer not to say" = 4),   .add = TRUE )  # REMOVE: set to NULL data <- val_labels(data, gender = NULL)

what: Get or Set Variable Labels — var_label
title: Get or Set Variable Labels — var_label
text: Retrieves assigns variable labels ("label" attribute) vectors data frame columns. Variable labels describe variable measures (e.g., "Age respondent") commonly used survey data imported SPSS, Stata, SAS. function operates two modes: GET mode: Called bare variable names ... arguments, returns existing labels. SET mode: Called name = "label" pairs, assigns labels variables returns modified data.

title: Get or Set Variable Labels — var_label
code: var_label(data, ...)

title: Get or Set Variable Labels — var_label
text: data data frame, tibble, single vector. ... GET mode: unquoted variable names (tidyselect supported). empty, returns labels variables. SET mode: named pairs name variable value label string. Use NULL remove label.

title: Get or Set Variable Labels — var_label
text: GET mode (vector input): single character string (label), NULL label exists. GET mode (data frame input): named character vector labels. Variables without labels return NA. SET mode: modified data frame (invisibly), labels assigned.

title: Get or Set Variable Labels — var_label
text: Variable labels stored "label" attribute column, standard used haven package SPSS/Stata/SAS imports. labels preserved mariposa's codebook(), frequency(), describe() functions.

title: Get or Set Variable Labels — var_label
text: Use var_label() : Want inspect variables measure imported survey data Need add labels manually created data using codebook() write_spss() Want update correct labels import

title: Get or Set Variable Labels — var_label
code: # GET: retrieve all variable labels var_label(survey_data) #>                                                id  #>                                                NA  #>                                               age  #>                                    "Age in years"  #>                                            gender  #>                                          "Gender"  #>                                            region  #>                              "Region (East/West)"  #>                                         education  #>                  "Highest educational attainment"  #>                                            income  #>                  "Monthly household income (EUR)"  #>                                        employment  #>                               "Employment status"  #>                             political_orientation  #>         "Political orientation (1=left, 5=right)"  #>                             environmental_concern  #>           "Environmental concern (1=low, 5=high)"  #>                                 life_satisfaction  #> "Life satisfaction (1=dissatisfied, 5=satisfied)"  #>                                  trust_government  #>        "Trust in government (1=none, 5=complete)"  #>                                       trust_media  #>             "Trust in media (1=none, 5=complete)"  #>                                     trust_science  #>           "Trust in science (1=none, 5=complete)"  #>                                   sampling_weight  #>                                "Weighting factor"  #>                                           stratum  #>                         "Stratification variable"  #>                                    interview_mode  #>                                  "Interview mode"   # GET: specific variables var_label(survey_data, age, gender) #>            age         gender  #> "Age in years"       "Gender"   # GET: from a single vector var_label(survey_data$age) #> [1] "Age in years"  # SET: assign labels data <- var_label(survey_data,   age = "Age of respondent",   gender = "Gender identity" )  # REMOVE: set to NULL data <- var_label(data, age = NULL)

title: Measure Population-Representative Spread (IQR) — w_iqr
text: w_iqr() calculates interquartile range using survey weights. IQR distance 25th 75th percentiles – tells range contains middle 50% population. Unlike standard deviation, IQR affected outliers, making robust measure spread.

title: Measure Population-Representative Spread (IQR) — w_iqr
code: w_iqr(data, ..., weights = NULL, na.rm = TRUE)

title: Measure Population-Representative Spread (IQR) — w_iqr
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... numeric variables want analyze. can list multiple variables use helpers like starts_with("income") weights Survey weights make results representative population. Without weights, get simple sample IQR. na.rm Remove missing values calculating? (Default: TRUE)

title: Measure Population-Representative Spread (IQR) — w_iqr
text: Population-weighted IQR(s) sample size information, including weighted IQR, effective sample size (effective N), number valid observations used.

title: Measure Population-Representative Spread (IQR) — w_iqr
text: Weighted IQR: range covers middle 50% weighted population. larger IQR means spread central part data. Effective N: many independent observations weighted data represents. N: actual number observations used. IQR especially useful data skewed. example, income data, IQR gives better sense "typical spread" SD extreme incomes distort .

title: Measure Population-Representative Spread (IQR) — w_iqr
text: Use w_iqr() : data outliers skewed (e.g., income, response times) want robust measure spread influenced extremes need describe spread middle 50% population need SPSS-compatible weighted IQR values

title: Measure Population-Representative Spread (IQR) — w_iqr
text: \(IQR_w = Q_{3,w} - Q_{1,w}\) \(Q_{1,w}\) \(Q_{3,w}\) weighted 25th 75th percentiles, calculated using cumulative weights (see w_quantile).

title: Measure Population-Representative Spread (IQR) — w_iqr
text: IBM Corp. (2023). IBM SPSS Statistics 29 Algorithms. IBM Corporation.

title: Measure Population-Representative Spread (IQR) — w_iqr
text: Unweighted values use quantile Type 6 (SPSS HAVERAGE). Weighted values apply HAVERAGE position cumulative weights linear interpolation - R-internal extension (Tier 4 Validation Charter); SPSS reference validation weighted percentiles pending.

title: Measure Population-Representative Spread (IQR) — w_iqr
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Basic weighted IQR survey_data %>% w_iqr(age, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Interquartile Range Statistics #> --------------------------------------- #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable weighted_iqr Effective_N #>       age           25      2468.8 #>   # Multiple variables survey_data %>% w_iqr(age, income, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Interquartile Range Statistics #> --------------------------------------- #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable weighted_iqr Effective_N #>       age           25      2468.8 #>  #> --- income --- #>  Variable weighted_iqr Effective_N #>    income         1900      2158.9 #>   # Grouped data survey_data %>% group_by(region) %>% w_iqr(age, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Interquartile Range Statistics #> --------------------------------------- #>  #> Group: region = East #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable weighted_iqr Effective_N #>       age           24         477 #>  #> Group: region = West #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable weighted_iqr Effective_N #>       age           24      1993.1 #>   # In summarise context survey_data %>% summarise(iqr_age = w_iqr(age, weights = sampling_weight)) #> # A tibble: 1 × 1 #>   iqr_age #>     <dbl> #> 1      25  # Unweighted (for comparison) survey_data %>% w_iqr(age) #>  #> Interquartile Range Statistics #> ------------------------------ #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable iqr    N #>       age  24 2500 #>

title: Measure Population-Representative Kurtosis — w_kurtosis
text: w_kurtosis() measures heavy tails data's distribution , using survey weights population-representative results. Kurtosis tells whether data unusually many extreme values (outliers) compared normal distribution: Positive (excess) kurtosis: Heavier tails normal (outliers) Negative (excess) kurtosis: Lighter tails normal (fewer outliers) Near zero: Similar tail behavior normal distribution

title: Measure Population-Representative Kurtosis — w_kurtosis
code: w_kurtosis(data, ..., weights = NULL, na.rm = TRUE, excess = TRUE)

title: Measure Population-Representative Kurtosis — w_kurtosis
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... numeric variables want analyze. can list multiple variables use helpers like starts_with("trust") weights Survey weights make results representative population. Without weights, get simple sample kurtosis. na.rm Remove missing values calculating? (Default: TRUE) excess Show excess kurtosis? (Default: TRUE, matching SPSS). Excess kurtosis subtracts 3 normal distribution kurtosis 0, making interpretation easier.

title: Measure Population-Representative Kurtosis — w_kurtosis
text: Population-weighted kurtosis value(s) sample size information, including weighted kurtosis, effective sample size (effective N), number valid observations used.

title: Measure Population-Representative Kurtosis — w_kurtosis
text: Weighted Kurtosis (excess, default): Near 0: Tail behavior similar normal distribution Positive (> 0): Heavier tails, extreme values normal Negative (< 0): Lighter tails, fewer extreme values normal Values beyond 2 -2 indicate notable departure normality Effective N: many independent observations weighted data represents. N: actual number observations used. Kurtosis often checked together skewness assess normality. close zero normally distributed data.

title: Measure Population-Representative Kurtosis — w_kurtosis
text: Use w_kurtosis() : need assess whether variable follows normal distribution want check unusually many outliers deciding whether parametric tests appropriate need SPSS-compatible weighted kurtosis values

title: Measure Population-Representative Kurtosis — w_kurtosis
text: Uses SPSS Type 2 (sample-corrected) excess kurtosis formula. First, population excess kurtosis (\(g_2\)) calculated: \(g_2 = \frac{m_4}{m_2^2} - 3\) \(m_2 = \sum w_i(x_i - \bar{x}_w)^2 / V_1\) \(m_4 = \sum w_i(x_i - \bar{x}_w)^4 / V_1\) \(V_1 = \sum w_i\). , bias-corrected (Type 2) kurtosis : \(G_2 = \frac{(n+1) \cdot g_2 + 6}{(n-2)(n-3)} \cdot (n-1)\) \(n = V_1\) weighted data.

title: Measure Population-Representative Kurtosis — w_kurtosis
text: Joanes, D. N., & Gill, C. . (1998). Comparing measures sample skewness kurtosis. Statistician, 47(1), 183-189. IBM Corp. (2023). IBM SPSS Statistics 29 Algorithms. IBM Corporation.

title: Measure Population-Representative Kurtosis — w_kurtosis
code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Basic weighted kurtosis (excess kurtosis, default) survey_data %>% w_kurtosis(age, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Excess Kurtosis Statistics #> ----------------------------------- #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable weighted_kurtosis Effective_N #>       age            -0.396      2468.8 #>   # Multiple variables survey_data %>% w_kurtosis(age, income, life_satisfaction, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Excess Kurtosis Statistics #> ----------------------------------- #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable weighted_kurtosis Effective_N #>       age            -0.396      2468.8 #>  #> --- income --- #>  Variable weighted_kurtosis Effective_N #>    income             0.388      2158.9 #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>           Variable weighted_kurtosis Effective_N #>  life_satisfaction            -0.598      2390.9 #>   # Grouped data survey_data %>% group_by(region) %>% w_kurtosis(age, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Excess Kurtosis Statistics #> ----------------------------------- #>  #> Group: region = East #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable weighted_kurtosis Effective_N #>       age            -0.389         477 #>  #> Group: region = West #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable weighted_kurtosis Effective_N #>       age            -0.396      1993.1 #>   # Raw kurtosis (not excess) survey_data %>% w_kurtosis(age, weights = sampling_weight, excess = FALSE) #>  #> Weighted Kurtosis Statistics #> ---------------------------- #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable weighted_kurtosis Effective_N #>       age             2.604      2468.8 #>   # In summarise context survey_data %>% summarise(kurt_age = w_kurtosis(age, weights = sampling_weight)) #> # A tibble: 1 × 1 #>   kurt_age #>      <dbl> #> 1   -0.396  # Unweighted (for comparison) survey_data %>% w_kurtosis(age) #>  #> Excess Kurtosis Statistics #> -------------------------- #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable kurtosis    N #>       age   -0.364 2500 #>

Without weights, get simple sample average. na.rm Remove missing values calculating? (Default: TRUE)

title: Calculate Population-Representative Averages — w_mean
text: Population-weighted average(s) sample size information

title: Calculate Population-Representative Averages — w_mean
text: Use w_mean() survey uses sampling weights need population-representative averages. Weights correct : Oversampling certain groups (weights < 1) Undersampling groups (weights > 1) Non-response patterns Complex survey designs

title: Calculate Population-Representative Averages — w_mean
text: Weighted Mean: population-representative average Effective N: many independent observations weighted data represents N: Actual number observations used

title: Calculate Population-Representative Averages — w_mean
text: \(\bar{x}_w = \frac{\sum w_i \cdot x_i}{\sum w_i}\) effective sample size : \(n_{eff} = (\sum w_i)^2 / \sum w_i^2\)

title: Calculate Population-Representative Averages — w_mean
text: IBM Corp. (2023). IBM SPSS Statistics 29 Algorithms. IBM Corporation.

code: # Load required packages and data library(dplyr) data(survey_data)  # Basic weighted usage survey_data %>% w_mean(age, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Mean Statistics #> ------------------------ #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable weighted_mean Effective_N #>       age        50.514      2468.8 #>   # Multiple variables survey_data %>% w_mean(age, income, life_satisfaction, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Mean Statistics #> ------------------------ #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable weighted_mean Effective_N #>       age        50.514      2468.8 #>  #> --- income --- #>  Variable weighted_mean Effective_N #>    income      3743.099      2158.9 #>  #> --- life_satisfaction --- #>           Variable weighted_mean Effective_N #>  life_satisfaction         3.625      2390.9 #>   # Grouped data survey_data %>% group_by(region) %>% w_mean(age, weights = sampling_weight) #>  #> Weighted Mean Statistics #> ------------------------ #>  #> Group: region = East #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable weighted_mean Effective_N #>       age        52.278         477 #>  #> Group: region = West #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable weighted_mean Effective_N #>       age        50.067      1993.1 #>   # In summarise context survey_data %>% summarise(mean_age = w_mean(age, weights = sampling_weight)) #> # A tibble: 1 × 1 #>   mean_age #>      <dbl> #> 1     50.5  # Unweighted (for comparison) survey_data %>% w_mean(age) #>  #> Mean Statistics #> --------------- #>  #> --- age --- #>  Variable  mean    N #>       age 50.55 2500 #>

title: Find the Population-Representative Middle Value — w_median
text: w_median() finds median (middle value) data using survey weights. weighted median value half population falls half falls . Unlike mean, median pulled extreme values, making robust measure "typical" value population.

code: w_median(data, ..., weights = NULL, na.rm = TRUE)

Without weights, get simple sample median. na.rm Remove missing values calculating? (Default: TRUE)

title: Find the Population-Representative Middle Value — w_median
text: Population-weighted median(s) sample size information, including weighted median, effective sample size (effective N), number valid observations used.

title: Find the Population-Representative Middle Value — w_median
text: Weighted Median: value splits weighted population half. 50% population (weight) falls value, 50% . Effective N: many independent observations weighted data represents. N: actual number observations used. Comparing weighted median weighted mean informative: similar, distribution roughly symmetric. mean much larger median, distribution right-skewed (high values pull mean , e.g., income).

title: Find the Population-Representative Middle Value — w_median
text: Use w_median() : data outliers skewed (e.g., income, housing prices) want robust "typical value" influenced extremes need weighted 50th percentile need SPSS-compatible weighted median values

title: Find the Population-Representative Middle Value — w_median
text: weighted median weighted 50th percentile, computed SPSS HAVERAGE (quantile Type 6) position h = 0.5 * (W + 1) cumulative weights, linear interpolation bracketing observations. identical w_quantile(x, probs = 0.5) construction. Unweighted values match SPSS FREQUENCIES; weighted values non-integer weights R-internal extension HAVERAGE rule (Tier 4 Validation Charter) pending SPSS reference runs.

title: Find the Range of Your Data — w_range
text: w_range() calculates range (maximum minus minimum) data. range gives total spread smallest largest observed value. provides w_* interface consistency weighted statistics, though range affected weights (minimum maximum values remain regardless weighting).

title: Find the Range of Your Data — w_range

title: Calculate Population-Representative Standard Deviations — w_sd
text: w_sd() calculates standard deviations accurately represent population using survey weights. standard deviation tells spread data around average – larger SD means variation responses, smaller SD means responses cluster tightly around mean. Without weights, describe spread sample . weights, estimate spread values entire population.

title: Calculate Population-Representative Standard Deviations — w_sd
code: w_sd(data, ..., weights = NULL, na.rm = TRUE)

title: Calculate Population-Representative Standard Deviations — w_sd
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... numeric variables want analyze. can list multiple variables use helpers like starts_with("trust") weights Survey weights make results representative population. Without weights, get simple sample standard deviation. na.rm Remove missing values calculating? (Default: TRUE)

title: Calculate Population-Representative Standard Deviations — w_sd
text: Population-weighted standard deviation(s) sample size information, including weighted SD, effective sample size (effective N), number valid observations used.

title: Calculate Population-Representative Standard Deviations — w_sd
text: Weighted SD: population-representative standard deviation. Roughly 68% population falls within one SD weighted mean. Effective N: many independent observations weighted data represents. Always less equal actual sample size. N: actual number observations used calculation. large difference weighted unweighted SD suggests variability sample accurately reflect population.

title: Calculate Population-Representative Standard Deviations — w_sd
text: Use w_sd() : need report spread variable population want compare variability across groups proper weighting survey used complex sampling (oversampling, stratification) need SPSS-compatible weighted standard deviations

title: Calculate Population-Representative Standard Deviations — w_sd
text: weighted standard deviation uses SPSS frequency weights formula: \(s_w = \sqrt{\frac{\sum w_i (x_i - \bar{x}_w)^2}{V_1 - 1}}\) \(V_1 = \sum w_i\) sum weights \(\bar{x}_w = \sum w_i x_i / V_1\) weighted mean. effective sample size : \(n_{eff} = (\sum w_i)^2 / \sum w_i^2\)

title: Calculate Population-Representative Standard Errors — w_se
text: Weighted SE: precision weighted mean estimate. Smaller values mean precise estimates. can build 95% confidence interval : weighted mean +/- 1.96 * weighted SE. Effective N: many independent observations weighted data represents. Weights vary lot reduce effective N, increasing SE. N: actual number observations used.

title: Calculate Population-Representative Standard Errors — w_se

title: Calculate Population-Representative Standard Errors — w_se
text: weighted standard error calculated : \(SE_w = \frac{s_w}{\sqrt{V_1}}\) \(s_w\) weighted standard deviation (see w_sd) \(V_1 = \sum w_i\) sum weights. unweighted case: \(SE = s / \sqrt{n}\)

title: Measure Population-Representative Skewness — w_skew
text: w_skew() measures asymmetric data's distribution , using survey weights population-representative results. Skewness tells whether values tend trail left right: Positive skew: long tail right (e.g., income – many low, high) Negative skew: long tail left (e.g., exam scores – many high, low) Near zero: Roughly symmetric (e.g., height population)

title: Measure Population-Representative Skewness — w_skew
code: w_skew(data, ..., weights = NULL, na.rm = TRUE)

title: Measure Population-Representative Skewness — w_skew
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) ... numeric variables want analyze. can list multiple variables use helpers like starts_with("trust") weights Survey weights make results representative population. Without weights, get simple sample skewness. na.rm Remove missing values calculating? (Default: TRUE)

title: Measure Population-Representative Skewness — w_skew
text: Population-weighted skewness value(s) sample size information, including weighted skewness, effective sample size (effective N), number valid observations used.

title: Measure Population-Representative Skewness — w_skew
text: Weighted Skewness: population-representative skewness value. Near 0: Distribution roughly symmetric -0.5 0.5: Approximately symmetric -1 -0.5 0.5 1: Moderately skewed Beyond -1 1: Highly skewed Effective N: many independent observations weighted data represents. N: actual number observations used. High skewness suggests might want use median instead mean measure center, non-parametric tests instead t-tests.

title: Measure Population-Representative Skewness — w_skew
text: Use w_skew() : need check variable normally distributed want decide parametric non-parametric tests need assess whether mean good summary measure need SPSS-compatible weighted skewness values

title: Measure Population-Representative Skewness — w_skew
text: Uses SPSS Type 2 (sample-corrected) skewness formula. First, population skewness (\(g_1\)) calculated: \(g_1 = \frac{m_3}{m_2^{3/2}}\) \(m_2 = \sum w_i(x_i - \bar{x}_w)^2 / V_1\) \(m_3 = \sum w_i(x_i - \bar{x}_w)^3 / V_1\) \(V_1 = \sum w_i\). , bias-corrected (Type 2) skewness : \(G_1 = g_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{n(n-1)}}{n-2}\) \(n = V_1\) weighted data.

title: Measure Population-Representative Skewness — w_skew
text: Joanes, D. N., & Gill, C. . (1998). Comparing measures sample skewness kurtosis. Statistician, 47(1), 183-189. IBM Corp. (2023). IBM SPSS Statistics 29 Algorithms. IBM Corporation.

title: Compare Two Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — wilcoxon_test
text: wilcoxon_test() compares two paired measurements subjects data normally distributed. non-parametric alternative paired t-test. Think : way test whether scores changed two time points Comparing ratings two items respondents robust paired comparison works data shape test tells : Whether scores significantly different two measurements many subjects increased, decreased, stayed strength effect (effect size r)

title: Compare Two Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — wilcoxon_test
text: data survey data (data frame tibble) wide format, one row per subject two measurements separate columns x first measurement variable (e.g., pre-test, trust government) y second measurement variable (e.g., post-test, trust media). difference computed y - x weights Optional survey weights population-representative results conf.level Confidence level intervals (Default: 0.95 = 95 percent)

title: Compare Two Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — wilcoxon_test
text: Test results showing whether two measurements differ, including: Z statistic (standardized test statistic, normal approximation) P-value (significant difference?) Effect size r (strong difference?) Rank statistics (negative ranks, positive ranks, ties)

title: Compare Two Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — wilcoxon_test
text: P-value: p < 0.05, two measurements significantly different p < 0.001: strong evidence difference p < 0.01: Strong evidence difference p < 0.05: Moderate evidence difference p > 0.05: significant difference found Effect Size r (strong difference?): < 0.1: Negligible effect 0.1 - 0.3: Small effect 0.3 - 0.5: Medium effect 0.5 higher: Large effect Rank Categories: Negative Ranks: subjects y < x (decreased) Positive Ranks: subjects y > x (increased) Ties: subjects y = x (change)

title: Compare Two Related Measurements Without Assuming Normality — wilcoxon_test
